Arjuna, I am the universal Self seated in the hearts of all beings; so I alone am
the beginning, the middle and also the end of all beings.                                 (20)
*
The universe consists of an infinite number of objects and an infinite variety of creatures. For
their proper regulation and control these diverse orders of objects, ideas and beings have been classified
into various broad divisions or groups under GodГӯs immutable laws; and each broad division or group has
been placed under the control of a supervisor or chief, so that the processess of their creation, maintenance
and destruction may be carried on strictly according to law. The Rudras, Vasus. Г…dityas, SГҘdhyas,
ViвҖ№vedevas, Maruts, PitГҰdevas, Manus and SaptarВ¶is etc., are the various order of these functionaries or
chiefs. They are believed to have both an embodied and unembodied form. All of them represent, so many
glories of God.
The ViВ¶Ж’u-PurГҘЖ’a says:Гі
вҖҡп¬ӮпЈҝВё Гё Е’пЈҝп¬ӮГҠ в—ҠЕёп¬ӮвҖ” вҖҡв—ҠSГғГҠвҖ” вҖҡ#В·ВёГҝГҠпЈҝ ГҝпЈҝ в—ҠЕёГҲвҖҡГҚЕёп¬ӮpвҖ“ ГҹГЈВәЛқp ГҝГҠпЈҝЛҳГҝВў ГҒГІГҠЕ’вүҘГҠпЈҝвүҘГҠГ·ГҚГғГҠпЈҝ ГҒп¬ӮcГЎГҠГҠпЈҝвҒ„вүҘГҠпЈҝВ·ГҠSГғГҲ ГҒп¬ӮГ·ГҚГғГҝSГғГҠвҖ”H
(III.i.46)
Г¬All the gods, all Manus, the seven seers, as well as the sons of Manus, and Indra, the chief of gods,Гі
all these are special manifestations or glories of BhagavГҘn ViВ¶Ж’u.Г®
Besides the above, special representatives are duly chosen from the various order of creations for the
proper functioning of creation. In this selection, the choice particularly falls on those who manifest GodГӯs
lustre, energy, strength, learning, knowledge and power in a special degree. It is, therefore, that the Lord
speaks of them all as His glories or manifestations.
In Chapter LXX of the VГҘyu-PurГҘЖ’a we read the following account:ГіГ¬The creation of beings by
KaвҖ№yapa having been over, the said PrajГҘpati selected from the various orders of creation those who excelled
their group in merit and glory and for the governance of those groups made them lords over the rest. Thus
the moon was made the king of the stars and planets; BГҰhaspati was appointed as the chief of Г…вҲҶgirasas
(the son of AвҲҶgirГҘ); вҖәukrГҘcГҘrya was appointed as the head of the BhГҘrgavas (the scions of BhГҰgu; ViВ¶Ж’u
was made the chief of the Г…dityas (sons of Aditi); PГҘvaka (the god of fire), of the Vasus; DakВ¶a, of the
PrajГҘpatis (Lords of creation); PrahlГҘda, of the Daityas; Indra, of the Maruts;  NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a, of the SГҘdhyas;
вҖәaвҲҶkara, of the Rudras; VaruЖ’a was made the ruler of the waters; Kubera, of the YakВ¶a (a class of demigods);
вҖәВөulapГҘЖ’i (Bhairava), of the BhВөutas and PiвҖ№ГҘcas; the ocean, of the rivers; Citraratha, of the Gandharvas (the
celestial bards); UccaiВЁвҖ№ravas, of the horses; the lion, of the beasts; the bull, of he quadrupeds; GaruЕ’a, of
the birds; вҖәeВ¶a, of the snakes and other reptiles; VГҘsuki, of the NГҘgas; TakВ¶aka, of other varieties of snakes
and NГҘgas; the HimГҘlaya range, of the mountains; Vipracitti, of the DГҘnavas; Vaivasvata, of the manes;
Parjanya (the god of rains), of the seas, rivers and clouds; KГҘma (the god of Love), of the ApsarГҘs (celestial
hymphs); SaвүҲvatsara the year, of the various divisions of time such as the seasons and months etc.;
SudhГҘmГҘ, of the East, KetumГҘn, of the West and Vaivasvata Manu was made the king of all human beings.
The administration and maintenance of the entire creation is being carried on by these functionaries.Г® The
description of the glories of God contained in the present chapter agrees in many respects with the above
description.

Г«GuЕ’ГҘkГҘГӯ means sleep. And a conqueror

Being a fragment of God (XV.7), it is in

is therefore that the Lord declares the soul as
identical with Him.
The word Г«BhВөutГҘnГҘmГӯ stands for all
embodied beings both sentient and insentient.
The creation, maintenance and destruction of
beings proceed from God. All beings emanate
from Him, exist in Him, and even at the time
of final dissolution get merged in Him. In other
words, He is the root cause and substratum
of all. This is what is meant by the Lord when
He calls Himself the beginning, the middle
and also the end of all beings.

Of the eleven Indriyas or organs of

* Having lost many of her sons in the war with gods, Diti propitiated her husband, KaвҖ№yapa, by her
loving services. KaвҖ№yapa, who was the foremost of all ascetics, pleased her in his turn by granting her a
boon. Diti asked of him most glorious son capable of killing Indra. The great sage KaвҖ№yapa granted her
the desired boon, and while conferring that terrible boon told her that if she could bear the foetus in her
womb for a hundred years observing strict purity and self-restraint, and keeping her mind constantly fixed
on God, she would surely get a son capable of killing Indra. Knowing the foetus in DitiГӯs womb as his deadly
enemy, Indra, the chief of celestials, too made his appearance there to serve Diti with great humility. He
kept himself by her side all the twentyfour hours waiting for an opportunity to do something whenever he
found some lapse in her purity. At last, only a few days before the expiry of a hundred years, it so happened
that Diti went to bed, one day, without washing her feet and was soon overtaken by sleep. Availing himself
of that opportunity, Indra entered her womb with his weapon, a thunderbolt, in hand, and split the great
foetus into seven parts. Smitten by the thunderbolt, the foetus raised a loud cry. Indra tried to hush it up
by repeatedly whispering into its ears Г«MГҘ rodiВЁГӯ (donГӯt cry). Finding that, though split into seven parts,
the foetus was still alive, Indra got much exasperated and again split each of those seven parts into seven
each. Even though multiplied fortynine times, the foetus still remained alive. Indra was now convinced that
it would not die. These fortynine parts became known as the mighty Maruts or wind-gods. They got
the collective name of Г«MarutsГӯ from IndraГӯs words addressed to them: Г«MГҘ rodiВЁГӯ (donГӯt cry). (see ViВ¶Ж’u-
PВөurГҘЖ’a, Part I, Ch. 21).

ГІГҝГӘвҖҷвҲ‘В§pГҠВ¬вҒ„UГҠГҒВЎГғвҖ”вҖ“ п¬ӮГҺВ·ГҠвҲ‘В§ГҒВ¬p вүҘГҠГӘГ·ГҲp вҲ‘В§В¬Е’ГӢВё вҒ„Г’Uп¬ӮГғSГғГ•ГҠH
в—ҠГҺВӘвҲҡГҝГҠЕ“p вүҘГҠп¬ӮВёp вҲ‘В§В¬ГҠвҖ№ГӢ Гё ГҒп¬ӮвүҘГҠГҠГӘВ¬ГғпЈҝвҖ“ вҲһвҲ‘В§ГҠЕ’вүҘГҠГ’ГғпЈҝ вҲ‘В§ГҒГ•ГғГҠ LВ§ВәЛқГҠГҒГ№Г·ГҲп¬ӮЕёпЈҝEвҒ„UГҠвҖ”H
(HarivaвүҲвҖ№a, I.iii.51-52)
вҖ  Kubera is a grandson of the sage Pulastya and a son of ViвҖ№ravГҘ. He was born of DevavarЖ’inвҲӮ, a
daughter of sage BharadvГҘja. Pleased with his long and arduous penance, BrahmГҘ asked him to seek a boon
from him. He sought from him the custodianship of the riches of the universe. BrahmГҘ thereupon said, Г¬I
am also anxious to appoint someone as the fourth guardian of the universe; therefore, I call upon you to
accept this high office as Indra, Yama and VaruЖ’a have done.Г® It was BrahmГҘ again who made a gift to
him of the celebrated aerial car known as PuВ¶paka. Since then Kubera has held the office of the god of riches.
RГҘvaЖ’a, KuвүҲbhakarЖ’a and others were born of his step-mother, KaikasвҲӮ (VГҘlmikiГӯs RГҘmГҘyaЖ’a, UttarakГҘвҲҶЕ’a,
III). The celebrated NalakВөubara and MaЖ’igrвҲӮva, who had been turned into a pair of Arjuna trees by the curse
of NГҘrada and were redeemed by BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a, were the sons of no other than Kubera. (Vide вҖәrвҲӮmad
BhГҘgavata X.10)
вҖЎ Е“вҒ„UГҠпЈҝ Е“ЛқГҲп¬Ӯp вҖҡГҠпЈҝв—Ҡp вҖўвҖһUpГ’п¬ӮГҠГҒЕёвҲӮUГҠпЈҝЛҳЕёвҲӮUвҖ”вҖ“ В¬ЛқГ ГҝГҚВ·p В¬ЛқГ·ГҠвҖҡp п¬ӮвҖҡп¬ӮГҠпЈҝЛҳCГ”UГҠГ’ В¬ЛқвҲ‘В§ГӢГҒГғВёГғГҠвҖ”H

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
В¬ГҲвҒ„UГҠпЈҝЕ“вҖҡГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the priests; Гё and; в—ҠГҲГ…Гҝв—ҠЛңГ” (their) chief; в—ҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ” Me; ГҒп¬ӮГҒh know; В¬ГҠГ•Вё O son
of PГҰthГҘ, Arjuna; вҖҷГҺвҖһUSВ¬ГҒГғв—ҠЛңГ” BГҰhaspati; вҖҡпЈҝЕёГҠЕёГӢЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among warrior-chiefs; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I; SвҲ‘В§ГЈЕ’вҖ” am Skanda
(the generalissimo of the gods); вҖҡвҒ„UвҖҡГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the reservoirs of water; вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; вҖҡГҠВӘвҒ„UвҖ”Г” ocean.
Among the priests, Arjuna, know Me to be their chief, BГҰhaspati. Among
warrior-chiefs, I am Skanda (the generalissimo of the gods); and among the reservoirs
of water, I am the ocean.                                                                             (24)
A son of Lord вҖәivaвҖ , he is the generalissimo
of the gods. He is the foremost of all generals
in the world. It is therefore, that the Lord
declares him to be His very Self.
Among all seats of water in the world,
the oceanвҖЎ is the largest and is believed to be
their lord. Hence the ocean is their chief
representative; and it is, therefore, that the
Lord declares it to be His own Self.
BГҰhaspati* is the preceptor of Indra, the
chief of the celestials, the family priest of the
gods, the greatest repository of learning and
wisdom, the foremost of all the priests of the
world and the head of the Г…вҲҶgirasas (sons of
AвҲҶgirГҘ). Therefore, BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a calls
him His own self.
Skanda is also known by the name of
KГҘrtikeya. He has six heads and twelve arms.
в—ҠвҖһUВ·ГӢВёГЎГҠГҠВў 
Г·ГҺВӘГҲвҒ„UвҖһВўU 
ГҒВӘвҒ„UГҠв—ҠSГӘГҝпЈҝвҲ‘В§в—ҠЛҮГҠвҒ„Uв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“
ГҝГҙГҠГҠЕёГҠВў ВЎВ¬ГҝГҙГҠГҠпЈҝЛҳГҒSв—Ҡ SГ•ГҠп¬ӮвҒ„UГҠГЎГҠГҠВў ГҒвҖһUв—ҠГҠвҲӮUГҝвҖ”Г”H 25H
в—ҠвҖһUВ·ГӢВёГЎГҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the great seers; Г·ГҺВӘГҲвҖ” the sage BhГҰgu; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I; ГҒВӘвҒ„UГҠв—ҠЛңГ” (and) among words;
вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; вҲһвҲ‘В§в—ҠЛңГ” вҖўЛҮГҠвҒ„Uв—ҠЛңГ” the one syllable OM; ГҝГҙГҠГҠЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among sacrifices; ВЎВ¬ГҝГҙГҠвҖ” the
sacrifice of Japa; вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ I am; SГ•ГҠп¬ӮвҒ„UГҠГЎГҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ” (and) among the immovables; ГҒвҖһUв—ҠГҠвҲӮUГҝвҖ” the HimГҘlaya
mountain.
Among the great seers, I am BhГҰgu; among words, I am the sacred syllable OM.
Among sacrifices, I am the sacrifice of Japa (muttering of sacred formulas); and
among the immovables, the HimГҘlaya.                                                          (25)
bounded by limits, in other words, that which
is infinite in extent, and which, though
all-pervasive, is yet visible to us, is called Г«MahatГӯ.
And those wise men who having transcended
the limits of the human intellect depend
entirely on Him, who is infinite in virtues
MaharВ¶is there are many; their distinctive
marks and the names of the ten most prominent
of them are given below:Гі
Г¬The mind-born sons of BrahmГҘ are all
independent in the matter of birth and endowed
with supernatural powers. That which is not
* BГҰhaspati is the most illustrious son of MaharВ¶i AвҲҶgirГҘ. He was the chief of the seven seers that held
office in the second or SvГҘrociВ¶a Manvantara of the present Kalpa (HarivaвүҲвҖ№a, VII.12; Matsya-PurГҘЖ’a
IX.8). He is a great scholar. It was he who taught the Vedas with their limbs and other auxiliary branches
of learning, the six systems of philosophy, the SmГҰti texts and Г…gama etc., to the Lord when he assumed
the form of a Dwarf (BГҰhaddharma-PurГҘЖ’a, Madhya., XVI.69Гі73). It was his son, Kaca, who learnt the
science of reviving the dead from вҖәukrГҘcГҘrya. BГҰhaspati is the family priest of Indra, the chief of celestials.
The sublime teachings that he imparted to Indra from time to time form a most useful study for the humanity.
Accounts of these teachings are to be found in the вҖәГҘnti and AnuвҖ№ГҘsana Parvas of the MahГҘbhГҘrata.
вҖ 
In some books he is held to be born of DakВ¶aГӯs daughter, SwГҘhГҘ, through Agni, the god of fire
(MahГҘbhГҘrta, Vanaparva, 223). The MahГҘbhГҘrata and the PurГҘЖ’as contain wonderful stories about him.
вҖЎ
The word Г«oceanГӯ in this verse means all the oceans taken together.

(MahГҘntam В°RВ¶anti), are called MaharВ¶is. BhГҰgu,
MarвҲӮci, Atri, AвҲҶgirГҘ Pulaha, Kratu, Manu,
DakВ¶a, VasiВ¶Вўha and PulastyaГіthese are the
ten principal MaharВ¶is. They all sprang of their
own will from the mind of BrahmГҘ and are
possessed of supernatural powers. Because
God Himself appeared through BrahmГҘ in the
form of these seers, they are called MaharВ¶is.Г®*
(VГҘyu-PurГҘЖ’a LIX.82-83, 89-90).
BhГҰguвҖ  is the chief of all great seers.
He is a great devotee of God, highly
enlightened and full of spiritual glow: hence
the Lord declares him to be His own self.
The substantive Г«GirГӯ (the uninflected
form of Г«GirГҘmГӯ) denotes a word expressive
of meaning, and the word OM, also known
as the Г«PraЖ’avaГӯ is termed as the Г«one syllableГӯ
(VIII.13). Of all significant words Г«PraЖ’avaГӯ
stands the foremost; for it is an appelation of
God (XVII.23). The repetition of this word
brings God-realization and there is identity
between word and its meaning. Hence the
Lord speaks of the Г«PraЖ’avaГӯ as His own self.
Japa or repetition of the Divine Name
involves no violence in any form, and the
practice of Japa leads to the direct perception
of God. The Code of Manu also speaks very
highly of Japa.вҖЎ
Hence Г«JapayajВӨaГӯ excels all other forms
of sacrifice, and it is in order to bring out the
superiority of Г«JapayajВӨaГӯ to every other
sacrifice that the Lord calls it His very Self.
That which has a fixed position or is
immovable is called Г«SthГҘvaraГӯ. Mountains,
because they are immovable, fall under the
category of Г«SthГҘvarasГӯ.
The HimГҘlaya is the foremost of them
all. It is a most sacred place for the practice
of austerities and is helpful in the attainment
of liberation. The divine sages Nara and
NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a performed their austerities on the
Himalayan peaks. On top of this, the HimГҘlaya
is the king of mountains. Hence the Lord
speaks of it as His very self.

Among the mind-born sons of BrahmГҘ, BhГҰgu is a prominent figure. He has successively held the
position of one of the seven seers in the SvГҘyambhuva, CГҘkВ¶uВ¶a and other Manvantaras. In his line have
appeared a number of seers, revealers of Mantras or sacred hymns, and founders of independent Gotras or
families. He holds a position of great eminence among the seers. He married KhyГҘti, a daughter of DakВ¶a,
who gave birth to two sons, DhГҘtГҘ and VidhГҘtГҘ by name, and a daughter named вҖәrвҲӮ. This latter became the
Consort of BhagavГҘn NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a. The celebrated sage Cyavana too was begotten by him. His sons JotiВ¶mГҘn,
вҖәukГҰti, HaviВ¶mГҘn, TapodhГҰti, Nirutsuka and AtibГҘhu have figured as the chief among the seven seers in
different Manvantaras. He is one of the greatest seers and revealers of Mantras. It was he who tested the
SГҘttvika character of вҖәrвҲӮ ViВ¶Ж’uГӯs forbearance by kicking His chest. In commemoration of that incident
BhagavГҘn ViВ¶Ж’u still bears on His chest the mark of the kick in the shape of a footprint. Having been
entrusted with the work of creation, BhГҰgu, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, AвҲҶgirГҘ, MarвҲӮci, DakВ¶a, Atri and VasiВ¶Вўha
are also known as the Г¬Nine BrahmГҘsГ®. Almost all the PurГҘЖ’as are replete with references to this sage (vide
HarivaвүҲвҖ№a, the Matsya, вҖәiva, BrahmГҘЖ’Е’a, DevвҲӮ-BhГҘgavata, MГҘrkaЖ’Е’eya, Padma, VГҘyu and the BhГҘgavata
PurГҘЖ’as and the MahГҘbhГҘrata).

504
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The holy fig tree* (ficus religiosa) is
held to be the ruler of the kingdom of plants
and the most sacred of all trees; hence the
Lord declares it to be His very self.
For the marks of a DevarВ¶i or celestial
sage the reader is referred to the commentary
on verses 12 and 13 above, where they have
been already discussed. NГҘrada is the chief
of all such DevarВ¶is. Besides this, he is a
great devotee, a highly enlightened soul and
an expert revealer of Mantras. Hence it is
that the Lord declares him to be His very
Self. For a detailed account of NГҘrada, the
reader is referred to the footnote under the
commentary on verses 12 and 13.
The Gandharvas are a particular class of
celestial beings. They are celestial musicians
and actors. In the celestial regions they are
considered to be most handsome and attractive
in appearance. The abode of the Gandharvas
or Gandharvaloka is situated above the abode
of the Guhyakas and below that of the
VidyГҘdharas. Like the gods and the manes,
there are two varieties of GandharvasГіearthly
and celestial. A human being ascending to
the abode of the Gandharvas by dint of his
merits after death is an Г«earthlyГӯ Gandharva;
while he who is a Gandharva from the
beginning of a Kalpa is called a celestial
Г«GandharvaГӯ. The celestial Gandharvas are
again divided into two classesГіГ«MauneyasГӯ
and Г«PrГҘdheyasГӯ. Two of sage KaвҖ№yapaГӯs
wives bore the names of Muni and PradhГҘ,
respectively. Most of the ApsarГҘs and
Gandharvas took their birth from them. Sixteen
celestial Gandharva, viz., BhвҲӮmasena, Ugrasena,
SuparЖ’a, 
VaruЖ’a, 
Gopati, 
DhГҰtarГҘВ¶Вўra,
SВөuryavarcГҘ, SatyavГҘk, ArkaparЖ’a, Prayuta,
BhвҲӮma, Citraratha, вҖәГҘliвҖ№irГҘ, Parjanya, Kali
and NГҘrada, being the progeny of Muni, are
called Г«MauneyasГӯ. Another group of fourteen
Gandharvas, viz., Siddha, PВөurЖ’a, Barhi,
вҖўEГ Г•вҖ” (I am) the AвҖ№vattha (the holy fig tree); вҖҡп¬ӮВёп¬ӮГҺЛҮГҠГҠГЎГҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ” of all trees; Е’пЈҝп¬ӮВ·ГӢВёГЎГҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the

souls); вҲ‘В§ГҒВ¬вҲӮUвҖ” в—ҠГҲГҒЕёвҖ”Г” (am) the sage Kapila.
Among all trees, I am the AвҖ№vattha (the holy fig tree); among the celestial sages,
NГҘrada; among the Gandharvas (celestial musicians), Citraratha; and among the
Siddhas, I am the sage Kapila.                                                          (26)

В¬ГҠВ¬вҖҡвҖһUdвҖһUГЈГғГҠ 
Г·п¬ӮпЈҝГӣГҠГҺГЎГҠГҠВў 
вҲ‘В§ГҠв—ҠЕ’ГҲГүГҠГҠпЈҝ 
ВӘГҲГЎГҠГҠвҖҰKвҖ”H
(Skanda-PurГҘЖ’a, NГҘgara-KhaЖ’Е’aГі247, 41, 42, 44)
Г¬In the root of the holy fig tree resides ViВ¶Ж’u; in the trunk, KeвҖ№ava; in the branches, NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a; in
the leaves, Lord Hari, and in the fruits abides Acyuta in conjunction with all the gods. There is not the
least doubt about this. This tree is ViВ¶Ж’u Himself appearing in a concrete form; exalted souls worship the
sacred root of this tree. Dependence on this tree wipes out thousands of menГӯs sins, grants their prayers and
bestows merit.Г®
Besides this, work on Indian medicine also speak highly of this tree; its leaves, fruit and barkГіall
possess medicinal properties. They are made use of in treating ailments such as impurity of blood, excess
of phlegm wind and bile, a burning sensation all over the body, vomitting, dropsy, want of appetite,
poisoning, cough, remittent fever, hiccough, a bruise in the heart, disease of the nose, dry spreading itches,
worms, leprosy, a sore on the skin, a burn etc.

PВөurЖ’ГҘyu, BrahmacГҘrвҲӮ, RatiguЖ’a, SuparЖ’a,

HВөuhВөu and Tumburu, being born of PradhГҘ,
are called Г«PrГҘdheyasГӯ (MahГҘbhГҘrata, Г…di.,
LXV). Among them, HГҘhГҘ, HВөuhВөu, ViвҖ№vГҘvasu,
Tumburu, Citraratha and others are more
prominent than the rest; and Citraratha is
considered to be the ruler of them all. Citraratha
is highly proficient in celestial music, and a
master of this art. It is therefore, that the Lord
calls him His very self. Accounts of these
Gandharvas are contained in the Agni,
MГҘrkaЖ’Е’eya, VГҘyu and KГҘlikГҘ PurГҘЖ’as, the
MahГҘbhГҘrata, Г…di-Parva and other scriptures.
A Г«SiddhaГӯ is one who has attained
supernatural powers of all kinds obtaining of
the physical and astral planes, and is fully
endowed with noble virtues such as
righteousness, wisdom, glory and dispassion
etc. There are thousands of such Siddhas,
Kapila being the foremost of them all. Lord
Kapila is a direct manifestation of God. He
took his descent from the womb of DevahВөuti,
wife of the great YogвҲӮ, Kardama, in order to
impart divine knowledge to his mother. At
the time of his birth the Creator, BrahmГҘ
himself, made his appearance in the hermitage
of Kardama and said to DevahВөuti:Гі
Г¬Held in high esteem by the teachers of
SГҘвҲҶkhya, this lord of the Siddhas will enhance
your glory and will be known throughout the
world by the name of Г«KapilaГӯ.* (вҖәrвҲӮmad
BhГҘgavata III.xxiv.19)
Kapila is eternally endowed by nature
with 
virtues 
like 
knowledge, 
glory,
righteousness and dispassion etc. There is
none among the Siddhas who can even stand
comparison with him, much less excel him. It
is, therefore, that the Lord calls sage Kapila
His own self.

A king devoted to his duty and
adorned with virtues enumerated in the
scriptures urges his subjects to righteous acts,
deterring them from sinful pursuits, and protects
them; hence he is regarded as superior to
other men. Such kings exhibit the divine
power more than ordinary men. It is, therefore,
that the Lord calls him His own self.

Among horses, know Me to be the celestial horse, UccaiВЁвҖ№ravГҘ, begotten of the

The celestial horse, UccaiВЁвҖ№ravГҘ, was
received during the churning of the ocean. along
with nectar. Hence it is counted as one of the

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The term Г«NarГҘdhipaГӯ can also be taken
to refer to the Manu presiding over a particular
Manvantara, since he is the lord of men of
that Manvantara. It is well-known that PrajГҘpati
appointed Vaivasvata Manu as the Lord of
men of the existing Manvantara.

Among weapons, I am the thunderbolt; among cows, I am the celestial cow,
KГҘmadhenu (the cow of plenty). I am the the generative passion which leads to
procreation (as enjoined by the scriptures); among serpents, I am VГҘsuki.                          (28)
Him. The same idea was expressed in verse
11 of Chapter VII by adding the qualification
Г«DharmГҘviruddhaВЁГӯ (not inconsistent with
virtue) to the word Г«KГҘmaВЁГӯ (sexual desire).
The intention is to show that sexual enjoyment
indulged in by sensual men for the gratification
of the sense is beastly and hardly consistent
with virtue; only such enjoyment as is indulged
in by men of self-control only for the sake of
procreation, and as enjoined by the scriptures,
is consistent with virtue and, therefore, good.
Hence it finds a place among the glories of
God.
VГҘsuki, being the king of serpents and
a devotee of God, is regarded as the best of
serpents. Hence the Lord declares him to be
His own self.
Among all the weapons of the universe
the thunderbolt (IndraГӯs weapon) is the most
powerful, because it embodies the glory of
sage DadhвҲӮciГӯs austerities and the lustre of
God Himself; and it is said to be unfailing in
effect (вҖәrвҲӮmad BhГҘgavata VI.xi.19-20). Hence
the Lord speaks of it as His very self.
The celestial cow KГҘmadhenu (the cow
of plenty) is the best of all cows. She can satisfy
all the desires of gods and men and was begotten
during the churning of the ocean. Hence the
Lord declares her to be His own self.
Г«KandarpaГӯ is an appellation of the god
of sexual love. By adding the qualification
Г«PrajanaГӯ to it, the Lord shows that only that
sexual urge which leads to procreation as
enjoined by the scriptures is identical with

am VaruЖ’a, the lord of waters and aquatic creatures. Among the manes, I am AryamГҘ

* Chapter X *
507
The serpent-god вҖәeВ¶a is the king of all
NГҘgas and is endowed with a thousand heads.
Serving as a cosy bed for the Lord and
rendering other services to Him, he is constantly
engaged in gratifying Him. Besides he is a
great devotee of the Lord, and often
manifesting himself along with the Lord, he
participates in His divine sports. His birth
also is attributed to God* Hence he is declared
to be the LordГӯs own self.
VaruЖ’a, being the Lord of all aquatic
creatures and deities presiding over the waters,
the guardian of a quarter of the universe, a
celestial being and a devotee of God, is held
to be an outstanding personality. Hence the
Lord speaks of him as His own self.
There are seven principal manes;вҖ 
KavyavГҘha, Anala, Soma, Yama, AryamГҘ,
the AgniВ¶vГҘttas and the BarhiВ¶ads. AryamГҘ,
being the head of the PitГҰs, is recognized as
the best of them all; therefore, the Lord
speaks of him as His own self.
Among all the rulers on earth, as well as
in the celestial world, Yama (the god of
death) is the greatest. The awards of Yama
are all equitable and righteous, salutary as
well as expiatory in character. He is an
enlightened devotee of God and the guardian
of a quarter of the universe. Hence the Lord
speaks of him as His own Self.вҖЎ

вҲ‘В§вҲҡГҝп¬ӮГҠвҖһUГҠпЈҝЛҳЕёвҲӮUвҖ” вҖҡГҠпЈҝв—ҠГҠпЈҝ Гҝв—ҠpГ’п¬ӮГҠГҝВёв—ҠГҠ ГғГ•ГҠвҖ“ вҖўГҒВӘAcп¬ӮГ”ГҠГ»ГҠГҠ вҖҷГҒвҖһВёUВ·Е’Г№ГҝpГҠГЈГ ГҝГҠ sГ”в—ҠГҚГғВёГҝвҖ”H
(вҖәiva., Dharma., LXIII.2)
In some books we read the following names: SukГҘla, Г…вҲҶgirasa, SusvadhГҘ, SomapГҘ, VairГҘja, the
AgniВ¶vГҘttas and the BarhiВ¶ads (HarivaвүҲвҖ№a, PВөurva, XVIII). The names may have differed in the different
Manvantaras.
вҖЎ
In the court of Yama no one is treated with partiality on any score, nor is there any scope for
recommendation, bribery or flattery. His laws are inexorable. It is therefore that he is considered the greatest
among rulers. Indra (the lord of paradise), Agni (the god of fire), NirГҰti (the goddess of death), VaruЖ’a (the
god of water), VГҘyu (the god of air), Kubera (the god of riches), вҲ«вҖ№ГҘna (вҖәiva) and BrahmГҘГіthese are the
ten regional gods (vide BГҰhaddharma-PurГҘЖ’a, Uttara., 9). They are the guardians of all the quarters of the
universe.
It is said that to the virtuous Yama presents a naturally genial aspect; whereas to the sinners he appears
in a dark and ghastly form with bloodshot eyes, terrible jaws, a tongue shooting like lightning, and dreadful
bristling hair and with the rod of Punishment raised in his hand  (Skanda., KГҘвҖ№вҲӮ., PВөurvГҘrdha, VIII.55,56).
Yama is possessed of supreme wisdom. It was he who imparted the knowledge of the Self to NaciketГҘ.
The story of NaciketГҘ appears in the KaВўhopaniВ¶ad, the MahГҘbhГҘrata AnuвҖ№ГҘsana-Parva and the VГҘrГҘha-
PurГҘЖ’a. Over and above this he is a great devotee of God. The glories of God, as well as of the Divine
Name, sung by him before his messengers in вҖәrвҲӮmada BhГҘgavata VI.iii, ViВ¶Ж’u-PurГҘЖ’a III.vii, and Skanda-
PurГҘЖ’a, KГҘвҖ№вҲӮ-KhaЖ’Е’a, PВөurvГҘrdha. Ch. VIII, should be carefully studied by all without fail.
But occasionally there appear on earth men who bring even Yama to his witГӯs end. The Skanda-
PurГҘЖ’a records the story of a famous emperor, KвҲӮrtimГҘn by name, who was a great devotee of God. Through
his lofty teachings, all his subjects were inspired with piety and devotion. By virtue of his religious merits,

508
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The progeny of Diti are known as Daityas.
PrahlГҘda has been recognized as the best of
the Daityas inasmuch as he is endowed with
all good qualities, an extremely pious soul, a
faithful, loving and disinterested devotee of
God, and the ruler of the Daityas. Hence the
Lord speaks of him as His very self.
The word Г«KГҘlaГӯ denotes time with its
various divisions, e.g., a moment, an hour, a
day, a fortnight, a month, a year and so on.
It is the basis of all astronomical calculations.
Hence the Lord declares it to be identical
with Himself.
The lion is recognized as the king of all
beasts. It is the strongest, the boldest and the
bravest of animals and has a most dignified
bearing. Hence the Lord counts it among His
glories.
GaruЕ’a, son of VinatГҘ, is the king of
birds. Being the largest and most powerful of
all birds, he is regarded as the best of them.
Over and above this, he is a great devotee of
God and the LordГӯs own carrier. Hence the
Lord declares him to be His own Self.
В¬ЛқtГ”UГҠЕ’вҖ”Г” (the famous devotee) PrahlГҘda; ГёГ” and; вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; Е’Г’Г ГҝГҠЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the Daityas; вҲ‘В§ГҠвҲӮUвҖ”
Time; вҲ‘В§вҲӮUГҝГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among reckoners; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I; в—ҠГҺВӘГҠГЎГҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among quadrupeds; Гё even so; в—ҠГҺВӘпЈҝГЈВәЛқвҖ” the
king of beasts, lion; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I (am); п¬ӮГ’ЕёГғпЈҝГҝвҖ”Г” GaruЕ’a (son of VinatГҘ); ГёГ” and; В¬ГҒЛҮГҠГЎГҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among birds;
Among the Daityas, I am the great devotee, PrahlГҘda; and among reckoners, I am
Time; among quadrupeds, I am the lion, and among birds, I am GaruЕ’a.           (30)
the souls that were already rotting in the abode of Yama gradually began to be redeemed; while those who
had newly left their mortal coil attained the supreme state. Thus fresh entry into the abode of Yama was
completely stopped. The result was that in due course not a single soul was left in his abode. Thereupon
Yama lodged a complaint with BrahmГҘ, who in his turn referred him to BhagavГҘn ViВ¶Ж’u. The latter told
him that there was no help so long as the pious and devoted Emperor KвҲӮrtimГҘn ruled over the earth. But
such a state of affairs could not last for ever. (Skanda, ViВ¶Ж’u., Vai., XI.xii.13)

В¬п¬ӮЕёвҖ”Г” the wind; В¬п¬ӮГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the purifiers; вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; вҒ„UГҠв—ҠвҖ” вҖәrвҲӮ RГҘma; вүҘГҠSГІГҠГ·ГҺГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ” (and)

am;  dГҠпЈҝГғвҖҡГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among rivers; вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; ВЎГҠqГ”Uп¬ӮГӢ the GaвҲҶgГҘ.
Among purifiers, I am the wind; among warriors, I am вҖәrвҲӮ RГҘma. Among fishes,
I am the alligator; and among streams, I am the GaвҲҶgГҘ.                              (31)
RГҘma, the Lord shows that it is He who
assumes different forms for carrying on different
kinds of sports in different ages. There is no
difference between вҖәrвҲӮ RГҘma and вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a;
for it is He who appears in the form of вҖәrвҲӮ
RГҘma.
Among fishes, the alligator is the largest
and most powerful. It is on account of this
distinction that the Lord declares it to be His
own Self.
The river GaвҲҶgГҘ or BhГҘgвҲӮrathвҲӮ, is the
Some commentators have interpreted the
word Г«PavatГҘmГӯ to mean those possessed of
swift motion, though such an interpretation is
not supported by grammar. The wind has
been regarded as the best even among those
possessing speed as well as among purifiers.
Hence the superiority of the wind is established
from both the points of view.
The word Г«RГҘmaГӯ in this verse refers to
BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ RГҘmacandra, son of king

(вҖәrвҲӮmad BhГҘgavata VIII.xxi.4)
Г¬O king, that water poured from the pot of BrahmГҘ (the Creator), and hallowed by washing the feet
of the Lord, turned to be heavenly counterpart of the holy GaвҲҶgГҘ (MandГҘkinвҲӮ). Descending on earth from
the heavens, like the spotless fame of the Lord, it continues to sanctify the three worlds to this day.Г®

sГ”пЈҝГғГ В¬вҒ„Uв—ҠГҠpГҝZ 
Sп¬ӮЕ“ГҲВёГЈГҝГҠ ГҝГҒЕ’вҖһUГҠпЈҝГҒЕ’Гғв—ҠЛң Г”вҖ“ вҖўЕёГЈГғГёвҒ„UГЎГҠГҠГӘГ·ГҠпЈҝВЎВ¬ЛқвҖҡГҚГғГҠГҝГҠ

вҖҡВўГҒЕёп¬ӮпЈҝвүҘГҝ в—ҠЕёГҠпЈҝ ГҝГҒSв—ҠГңВҝЛӣUhГҝГҠ в—ҠЕёГҲГҝГҠпЈҝЛҳв—ҠвҲӮUГҠвҖ”вҖ“ ГІГҠГ’ВӘГҲГЎГҝВў Е’ГҲSГ ГҝВЎВў ГҒвҖһUГ п¬ӮГҠ вҖҡlГҠпЈҝ ГҝГҠГғГҠSГғЕ’ГҠГ в—ҠГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ”H
(Ibid., IX.ix.14-15)
Г¬By firmly and devoutly fixing their unsullied mind on the lotus-feet of the Lord, sages have speedily
attained identity with Him, renouncing the world, which is a product of the three GuЖ’as or modes of PrakГҰti,
so difficult to renounce. Hence what has been stated above in praise of the holy GaвҲҶgГҘ, which cuts asunder
the bonds of earthly existence, and has proceeded from the same lotus feet of the Lord, is no cause for any
great wonder.Г®
вҖ 
When the great goddess SatвҲӮ, daughter of DakВ¶a and Mother of the whole universe, dropped her
body, and her consort, BhagavГҘn вҖәiva, embarked on a life of rigorous penance, the gods began to invoke
the goddess, who revealed herself before them. The gods thereupon implored her to seek the hand of Lord
вҖәiva once more. The goddess assured them that she would descend in two forms in the house of HimГҘlaya,
the king of mountains, from the womb of MenakГҘ, daughter of Sumeru. Thereupon she first appeared as
the GaвҲҶgГҘ. Offering praises to her, the gods took her to heaven. Assuming an embodied form, she thence
repaired to the KailГҘsa mountain with BhagavГҘn вҖәaвҲҶkara, and at the request of BrahmГҘ established herself
in the water-pot of BrahmГҘ in her formless aspect (вҖўГЈГғЕ“ГҠВёЕёГҠВўвүҘГҠГ·ГҠВӘпЈҝЕё ГҒSГ•ГғГҠ вҖҷЛқrГҠГ”вҲ‘В§в—ҠГЎВ«UвҲӮUГҠГ’). BrahmГҘ carried her in that
water-pot to Brahmaloka, his own abode. Thereafter, on one occasion, BhagavГҘn вҖәaвҲҶkara paid a friendly
visit to VaikuЖ’Вўha (the abode of BhagavГҘn ViВ¶Ж’u), accompanied by goddess GaвҲҶgГҘ. There, at the request
of ViВ¶Ж’u, Lord вҖәiva entertained Him with songs. Whatever tune he sang materialized before them. As
вҖәaвҲҶkara sang the tune technically known by the name of вҖәrвҲӮ, the spirit of that tune also materialized.
Enchanted by that melody, BhagavГҘn NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a, who is Rasa (Bliss) personified, turned into a fluid and
glided along. Thereupon BrahmГҘ said to himself, Г«The melody that has emerged from Brahma (the eternal
principle of sound) is BrahmГҘ Itself, and BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ Hari, who is Brahma-embodied, has also melted on
this occasion; let the GaвҲҶgГҘ, who is identical with Brahma, absrob this liquid Brahma.Гӯ Reflecting thus, he
brought his water-pot into contact with that flowing form of NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a. As soon as this was done, the whole
of that liquid got mixed with the GaвҲҶgГҘ, and the formless GaвҲҶgГҘ now assumed the form of water. BrahmГҘ
then returned to his abode. Later on, when BhagavГҘn ViВ¶Ж’u, appearing as the Divine Dwarf, covered the
entire heaven-world with one of His feet consisting of Sattva, BrahmГҘ washed that foot with the water
contained in his water-pot. At the touch of that water, the divine foot got rooted there, and even after the
disappearance of Lord, the foot stayed along with the GaвҲҶgГҘ in heaven. The same GaвҲҶgГҘ, which emerged
from that foot, was later brought down to earth by BhagвҲӮratha, after great exertion in the shape of austere
penance, for the deliverance of his ancestors. Here too it was BhagavГҘn вҖәaвҲҶkara who bore her on his head.
This wonderful, beautiful and instructive story dealing with the glory of the GaвҲҶgГҘ appear in the
BГҰhaddharma-PurГҘЖ’a. Madhya-KhaЖ’Е’a, Chapter 12 to 28.

Arjuna, I am the beginning, the middle and the end of all creations. Of all
knowledge, I am the knowledge of the soul (metaphysics); among disputants, I am the
right type of reasoning.                                                                  (32)
knowledge and that is why the Lord declares
it to be His own self.
There are three forms of argumentation
known by the names of Г«JalpaГӯ, Г«VitaЖ’Е’ГҘГӯ
and Г«VГҘdaГӯ. A disputation which is held with
a view to establishing oneГӯs own viewpoint
and for the demolition of the adversaryГӯs
standpoint irrespective of which is right and
which is wrong, is called Г«JalpaГӯ. That which
is resorted to only for the demolition of the
opponentГӯs standpoint is known as Г«VitaЖ’Е’ГҘГӯ;
while reasoning with the pure motive of
arriving at the right conclusion is known by
the name of Г«VГҘdaГӯ. While Г«JalpaГӯ and
Г«VitaЖ’Е’ГҘГӯ give rise to anger, hatred, violence,
pride and other evils, Г«VГҘdaГӯ is helpful in
ascertaining the truth and attaining oneГӯs
spiritual goal. So, while Г«JalpaГӯ and Г«VitaЖ’Е’ГҘГӯ
are worth giving up, Г«VГҘdaГӯ is worth adopting
wherever necessary. It is because of this
merit possessed by Г«VГҘdaГӯ that the Lord
declares it as one of His glories.
In verse 20 above, the Lord declared
Himself as the beginning, the middle and
also the end of all beings; and in the present
verse He says that He is the beginning, the
middle and the end of all creations. There is
no tautology here; for whereas the word
Г«BhВөutГҘnГҘmГӯ in verse 20 stands for sentient
beings alone, the word Г«SargГҘЖ’ГҘmГӯ in the
present verse stands for the whole creation
consisting of sentient and insentient beings
and including all the worlds.
Г«AdhyГҘtmavidyГҘГӯ or BrahmavidyГҘ is that
branch of knowledge which deals with the
Self, throws light on the Self and enables the
soul to realize Brahma easily. All other branches
of knowledge in the world, known as well as
unknown, are inferior to BrahmavidyГҘ; for
they strengthen the bonds of ignorance rather
than break them. Through BrahmavidyГҘ,
however, the knot of ignorance is untied for
ever and the truth relating to God realized.
Hence it is superior to all other branches of

* Chapter X *
511
The sound represented by Г«AГӯ is the
first of all vowels and consonants and it runs
through the sounds represented by all other
letters. The вҖәruti also says: Г¬All speech is
represented by the sound Г«AГӯ.Г®* For these
reasons the sound Г«AГӯ is the best of all other
sounds. Hence the Lord declares it to be His
own self.
The copulative compound is the best of
all compounds, inasmuch as all its components
carry equal emphasis.вҖ  Hence the Lord
enumerates it as one of His glories.
The word Г«KГҘlaГӯ occurring in verse 30
denotes Time as understood in terms of its
various divisions such as a Kalpa, Yuga,
year, half-year, month, day, hour, minute,
second and so on. It is an evolute of PrakГҰti
and ceases to exist in what is known as the
MahГҘpralaya, or Final Dissolution. Therefore,
it is not Г«unendingГӯ. The Г«TimeГӯ referred to
in the present verse, however, is the same
as the supreme Brahma or God Himself,
who is constant, eternal and everlasting
and without beginning or end. Hence the
word has been qualified by the adjective
Г«endlessГӯ (AkВ¶ayaВЁ). Thus there is a world
of difference between the Г«TimeГӯ referred
to in verse 30 and the Г«TimeГӯ spoken of
in the present verse. One is a product of
PrakГҰti, while the other is wholly beyond
PrakГҰti.вҖЎ
Speaking of Himself as Г¬the sustainer of
all, having His face on all sidesГ®, the Lord
brings out His identity with the Cosmic Form
of God. The intention is to show that the all-
formed and all-pervading God, the sustainer
and nourisher of all, is the same as вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a,
and no other.
grammar, I am the copulative compound. I am verily the endless Time (the devourer
of Time, God); I am the sustainer of all, having My face on all sides.             (33)
*
вҖўвҲ‘В§ГҠвҒ„UГҠпЈҝ п¬ӮГ’ вҖҡп¬ӮГҠВё п¬ӮГҠвҲ‘ЛңВ§Г”вҖ“ (Aitareya BrГҘhmaЖ’a, PВөurvГҘrdha, III.6)
вҖ 
In SaвүҲskГҰt grammar there are four varieties of compounds, viz., (1) AvyayвҲӮbhГҘva, (2) TatpuruВ¶a,
(3) BahuvrвҲӮhi, and (4) Dvandva. The other two compounds, KarmadhГҘraya and Dvigu, are covered by the
TatpuruВ¶a. In the AvyayвҲӮbhГҘva compound, emphasis lies on the first word of the compound. For example,
in the compound word Г«AdhihariГӯ, meaning Г«in HariГӯ, the indeclinable Г«AdhiГӯ denotes the seventh case-
ending; and this is what is sought to be conveyed by this compound. In the TatpuruВ¶a compound, however,
the emphasis lies on the second word. For example, of the two components of the compound word Г«SвҲӮtГҘpatiГӯ,
the emphasis lies on Г«PatiГӯ (husband): for the whole expression signifies вҖәrвҲӮ RГҘma, husband of SвҲӮtГҘ. In the
compound called Г«BahuvrвҲӮhiГӯ, the compounded whole signifies an object other than the meaning of the two
components. For example, the compound word Г«PвҲӮtГҘmbaraГӯ refers to the person wearing a yellow garment.
Neither of the two components constituting this word, viz., Г«PвҲӮtaГӯ (yellow) and Г«AmbaraГӯ (garment) carry
emphasis individually; both of them taken together point to a third objects, viz., the Lord who wears a
yellow robe. In the form of compound known as Dvandva (the copulative compound), however, all the
components carry equal emphasis. For example, the compound word Г«RГҘmalakВ¶maЖ’auГӯ refers to RГҘma and
LakВ¶maЖ’a both. Hence both the components forming this compound carry equal emphasis.
вҖЎ
There are three varieties of KГҘla:Гі
(1) Г«KГҘlaГӯ in the sense of Time.
(2) Г«KГҘlaГӯ in the form of PrakГҰti. The period immediately following the MahГҘpralaya; of Final
Dissolution, during which PrakГҰti exists in a state of equillibrium, is Time in the form of PrakГҰti.
(3) The eternal and everlasting God Himself, the embodiment of Knowledge and Bliss.
Higher and subtler than Г«KГҘlaГӯ in the gross form of Time is Г«KГҘlaГӯ in the form of PrakГҰti, which is
incomprehensible to the intellect; whereas Г«KГҘlaГӯ in the shape of God is even subtler and higher than, and
far superior to, KГҘla in the form of PrakГҰti. Really speaking, God is wholly beyond time and space; but where
we speak of PrakГҰti and its evolute, the world of matter, God alone, who is all Knowledge and all Bliss,
and who is the source, substratum and inspirer of all, is the real Г«KГҘlaГӯ. It is He who is referred to here as
the Г«endless TimeГӯ.

=== PAGE 513 ===

512
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
It is God Himself who appears as Death
to destroy all. That is why He speaks of
Death as His very self in the present verse.
He has already stated in verse IX.19 that He
is Death and Immortality both.
Even as God appearing as Death destroys
all living beings, i.e., disunites them from their
body, so does He unite them with other bodies
and thus causes their birth. This is what is
meant when the Lord declares Himself to be
the origin of all that are to be born.
PrasВөuti, daughter of SvГҘyambhuva Manu,
was married to DakВ¶a PrajГҘpati and gave
birth to twentyfour daughters. KвҲӮrti, MedhГҘ,
DhГҰti, SmГҰti, and KВ¶amГҘ are included in
them. Of these, KвҲӮrti, MedhГҘ and DhГҰti were
given in marriage to Dharma; SmГҰti was
married to AвҲҶgirГҘ, and KВ¶amГҘ to sage Pulaha.
вҖәrвҲӮ is a daughter of BhГҰgu, born of DakВ¶aГӯs
daughter Г«KhyГҘtiГӯ; her hand was espoused
by BhagavГҘn NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a. And Г«VГҘkГӯ is an
offspring of BrahmГҘ. All these seven are
deities presiding over the seven virtues indicated
by their names, viz., glory, prosperity, speech,
memory, intelligence, steadfastness and
forbearance, and are recognized as the best
among women. It is, therefore, that the Lord
speaks of them as His glories.
в—ҠГҺГ ГҝГҲвҖ” вҖҡп¬ӮВёвҖһUвҒ„UpГҠвҖһUв—ҠГҲjп¬Ӯp Г·ГҒп¬ӮcГҝГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“
вҲ‘В§ГӢГҢГғвҖ” ГјГҠГӢп¬ӮГҠВёBВ§ ЕёГҠвҒ„UГӢГЎГҠГҠВў Sв—ҠГҺГҒГғв—ҠпЈҝВёЕ“ГҠ Е“ГҺГҒГғвҖ” ЛҮГҠв—ҠГҠГ”H 34H
в—ҠГҺГ ГҝГҲвҖ” the Death; вҖҡп¬ӮВёвҖһUвҒ„UвҖ”Г” that annihilates all; ГёГ” and; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I am; В©Ujп¬ӮвҖ” (I am) the origin; ГёГ”
even so; Г·ГҒп¬ӮcГҝГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ” of all that shall be; вҲ‘В§ГӢГҢГғвҖ”Г” glory; ГјГҠГӢвҖ”Г” prosperity; п¬ӮГҠвҲ‘ЛңВ§Г” speech; Гё and; ЕёГҠвҒ„UГӢГЎГҠГҠв—ҠЛңГ”
of feminities; Sв—ҠГҺГҒГғвҖ” memory; в—ҠпЈҝЕ“ГҠГ” intelligence; Е“ГҺГҒГғвҖ” steadfastness; ЛҮГҠв—ҠГҠ forbearance.
I am the all-destroying Death that annihilates all, and the origin of all that shall
be born. Of feminities, I am KвҲӮrti, вҖәrвҲӮ, VГҘk, SmГҰti, MedhГҘ, DhГҰti and KВ¶amГҘ (the
goddesses presiding over glory, prosperity, speech, memory, intelligence, steadfastness
and forbearance, respectively).                                                           (34)
вҖҷГҺвҖһUГ вҖҡГҠв—Ҡ ГғГ•ГҠ вҖҡГҠв—ҠAГҠВў ВӘГҠГҝГІГҠГӢ ВҝUГЈЕ’вҖҡГҠв—ҠвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“
в—ҠГҠвҖҡГҠЕёГҠВў в—ҠГҠВӘВёвүҘГҠГӢВ·ГҠпЈҝВёЛҳвҖһUв—ҠГҺГғГҚЕёГҠВў вҲ‘ГҲВ§вҖҡГҲв—ҠГҠвҲ‘В§вҒ„UвҖ”Г”H 35H
вҖҷГҺвҖһUГ вҖҡГҠв—ҠГ” the class of вҖәrutis known as BГҰhatsГҘma; ГғГ•ГҠГ” likewise; вҖҡГҠв—ҠAГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among all the вҖәrutis
that can be sung; ВӘГҠГҝГІГҠГӢ the metre known as GГҘyatrвҲӮ; ВҝUГЈЕ’вҖҡГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the Vedic hymns; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ”
I (am); в—ҠГҠвҖҡГҠЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the twelve months (of the Hindu calendar); в—ҠГҠВӘВёвүҘГҠГӢВ·ВёвҖ”Г” the month known
as MГҘrgaвҖ№вҲӮrВ¶a (corresponding approximately to November); вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I (am); ВҙВ§ГғГҚЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the
six seasons (successively appearing in India in course of a year); вҲ‘ГҲВ§вҖҡГҲв—ҠГҠвҲ‘В§вҒ„UвҖ”Г” the spring season
(lit., the season of flowers).
Likewise, among the вҖәrutis that can be sung, I am the BГҰhatsГҘma; while among
the Vedic hymns, I am the hymn known as GГҘyatrвҲӮ. Again, among the twelve months
of the Hindu calendar, I am the month known as Г«MГҘrgaвҖ№вҲӮrВ¶aГӯ (corresponding
approximately to November-December); and among the six seasons (successively
appearing in India in the course of a year), I am the spring season.                (35)
the variety known as BГҰhatsГҘma* is the most
prominent and therefore the best. Hence the
Among the different varieties (Rathantara
etc.) of hymns appearing in the SГҘmaveda,
* BГҰhatsГҘma is a special variety of psalms finding place in the SГҘmaveda and devoted to the praises of God
under the name of Indra. These psalms are sung towards the end of the sacrifice known by the name of AtirГҘtra.

* Chapter X *
513
Lord speaks of it as His very self.
The GГҘyatrвҲӮ is the most important of all
the hymns or metrical compositions comprised
in the Vedas. The glory of the GГҘyatrвҲӮ is
scattered through the entire body of sacred
literature consisting of the Vedas, SmГҰti-texts,
ItihГҘsas and PurГҘЖ’as.* It is due to this excellence
of the GГҘyatrвҲӮ that the Lord speaks of it as
His very self.
In the days of the MahГҘbhГҘrata, the
year used to begin with the month of MГҘrgaвҖ№вҲӮrВ¶a
(MahГҘ., Anu., Ch. 106 and 109). Hence it is
the first of the twelve months. Besides, the
scriptures promise great rewards to those
who observe sacred vows and fastsвҖ  in this
month. The offering of the newly harvested
crop to the sacred fire has been recommended
in this very month. The RГҘmГҘyaЖ’a of VГҘlmвҲӮki
speaks of this month as the ornament of the
year. Thus the month of MГҘrgaвҖ№вҲӮrВ¶a is superior
to all other months of the year in many
respects. Hence the Lord declares it to be His
very self.
The spring is the best of all seasons and
has been recognized as their queen. During
this season the whole plant kingdom wears a
sprightly appearance and is clothed in fresh
leaves and flowers even without water. The
weather in this season is neither too hot nor
too cold, and almost all creatures feel cheerful
and happy. That is why the Lord declares it
to be His own self.

=== PAGE 515 ===

514
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
God is present in all living beings
and objects, good, bad or middling, existing
in this world. It is His presence and inspiration
that lend activity to the whole creation. There
is hardly any substance which is devoid of
the presence or power of God. Of all such
beings and objects exhibiting the SГҘttvika,
RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika qualities, only that being
or object which is possessed of some
extraordinary virtue, exceptional glory and
special attraction exhibits the presence and
power of God in a special degree. It is from
this point of view that the Lord has briefly
mentioned in this connection even abstract ideas
and virtues like victory, resolution, glory,
righteousness and knowledge along with
sentient beings like gods, demons, men, beasts,
birds and serpents etc., and inanimate objects
like the thunderbolt, senses, mind and ocean.
It is for brevityГӯs sake that the Lord has
mentioned only the main categories or groups.
The intention is to show that whatever being,
object, action or concept may be thought of,
He alone should be contemplated in that form.
It is from this point of view, and not with the
object of extolling gambling and encouraging
people to practise it, that of all deceitful practices
the Lord declares it to be His own self.
In this connection it should be
remembered that the Lord speaks of the most
ferocious and bloodthirsty lion and shark as
well as the all-consuming fire and all-devouring
Death as His very self. This does not mean
that anybody should take into his head to
play with a lion or a shark, jump into fire or
deliberately enter the jaws of death. The
objection that lies in adopting these courses
of action holds good in the case of gambling
as well.
Glory, victory, resolution and goodnessГі
all these virtues and achievements are helpful
in God-realization; hence the Lord declares
them as His own self. This declaration is
further intended to show that the glory of the
glorious really belongs to Him. He who
prides himself on it as a manifestation of his
own power commits a blunder. Even so, all
such virtues and achievements as the victory
of the victorious, the resolution of the resolute
and the goodness of the good are also His.
Priding oneself on these also constitutes the
height of folly.* Besides the above idea the
statement also indicates that those who are
blessed with these virtues should be regarded
as noble inasmuch as the divine glory is
manifested in them in a special degree.
lГҚГғВў ВҝUвҲӮUГҝГғГҠв—ҠГҒSв—Ҡ ГғпЈҝВЎSГғпЈҝВЎГҒSп¬ӮЕёГҠв—ҠвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“
ВЎГҝГҠпЈҝЛҳГҒSв—Ҡ вҲҡГҝп¬ӮвҖҡГҠГҝГҠпЈҝЛҳГҒSв—Ҡ вҖҡГ»п¬ӮВў вҖҡГ»п¬Ӯп¬ӮГғГҠв—ҠвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ”H 36H
lГҚГғв—ҠЛңГ” gambling; ВҝUвҲӮUГҝГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among all deceitful practices; вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; ГғпЈҝВЎвҖ” glory; ГғпЈҝВЎГҒSп¬ӮЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” of
the glorious; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I; ВЎГҝвҖ”Г” the victory (of the victorious); вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; вҲҡГҝп¬ӮвҖҡГҠГҝвҖ” the resolve (of the
resolute); вҖўГҒSв—Ҡ am; вҖҡГ»п¬Ӯв—ҠЛңГ” goodness; вҖҡГ»п¬Ӯп¬ӮГғГҠв—ҠЛңГ” of the good; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I.
I am gambling among deceitful practices, and the glory of the glorious. I am the
victory of the victorious, the resolve of the resolute, the goodness of the good.    (36)
*
There is a story in the KenopaniВ¶ad which runs as under. Once upon a time the gods of heaven
scored a victory over the demons through the grace of God. The fame and glory of the gods filled all
quarters. Intoxicated by their triumph, the gods turned their face from God and said to each other: Г¬The
victory is, indeed ours. We have vanquished the demons by dint of our own valour and shrewdness. It is
for this reason that the world worships us and sings songs commemorating our victory.Г® In order to do a
good turn to the gods by humbling their pride, the Supreme Being or Brahma sportively manifested such
a wonderful form, the very sight of which puzzled the mind of the gods. In order to discover the identity
of that wonderful Being in the form of a YakВ¶a or demigod, the gods approached their leader, the god of

* Chapter X *

в—ҠГҲЕёГӢЕёГҠв—ҠГҘГҝвҖһВўU вҲҡГҝГҠвҖҡвҖ” вҲ‘В§п¬ӮГӢЕёГҠв—ҠГҲвүҘГҠЕёГҠ вҲ‘В§ГҒп¬ӮвҖ”H 37H
п¬ӮГҺcГЎГҠГӢЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the members of the VГҰВ¶Ж’i clan of KВ¶atriyas; п¬ӮГҠвҖҡГҲЕ’пЈҝп¬ӮвҖ” вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a (son of

Arjuna (lit., a conqueror of riches); в—ҠГҲЕёГӢЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” among the sages; вҖўГҒВ¬ too; вҖўвҖһUв—ҠЛңГ” I; вҲҡГҝГҠвҖҡвҖ”Г” the great

I am KГҰВ¶Ж’a among the VГҰВ¶Ж’is; Arjuna, among the sons of PГҘЖ’Е’u; VyГҘsa, among
the sages, and the sage вҖәukrГҘcГҘrya among the wise.                                  (37)
fire, and said to him, Г¬O god of fire, you are the most glorious of all of us. Please go and ascertain who
this YakВ¶a really is.Г® The god of fire gave his assent and assured them of the success of his mission. He
then approached the YakВ¶a, but was so overpowered by his brilliance that he had no courage even to speak.
At last the divine YakВ¶a accosted the god of fire and asked him who he was. The god of fire replied, Г¬My
name is widely known. They call me both by the name of Agni and JГҘtavedГҘ.Г® Г¬This is all right;Г® rejoined
the Supreme Being, Г¬but O god of fire, tell Me what prowess you possess and what you are capable ofГ®.
Г¬O YakВ¶a !Г® the god of fire retorted, Г¬I can reduce to ashes whatever exists on earth or in the intermediate
region, animate or inanimate.Г®
Throwing a dry blade of grass before the god of fire, the Supreme Being asked him to burn it. The
god of fire exerted all his strength, and tried every means to burn it, but failed to do so. Hanging down
his head in shame and without saying a word more to the YakВ¶a, he returned to the gods with a sense of
frustration writ large on his face and reported that he had failed to discover the identity of the YakВ¶a.
Now it was the turn of the wind-god to approach the YakВ¶a; but he too had the same experience as
the god of fire and could not utter a single word. On the YakВ¶a enquiring who he was, the wind-god replied,
Г¬I am wind personified; my name and virtues are widely known. I constantly blow and carry smell from
one place to another. As I move in the intermediate region I am also called by the name of MГҘtariвҖ№vГҘГ®.
The Supreme Being asked him what he was capable of. The wind-god replied that he could blow away all
that existed on earth or in the intermediate region. Throwing the same dry blade of grass before him, the
Supreme Being asked him to blow it away. Exerting all his strength, the wind-god failed even to move the
blade of grass. Feeling much ashamed at this, he returned forthwith to the gods and reported that he had
failed to make out who the YakВ¶a was.
Indra, the Lord of paradise, was the next to approach the YakВ¶a. Finding him full of pride and egotism,
Brahma in the form of the YakВ¶a disappeared from the scene. In order to crush IndraГӯs pride, He did not
even condescend to speak to him. Though much abashed at this initial rebuff, Indra did not lose heart and
started meditation on God. Presently he noticed in the sky goddess UmГҘ, daughter of HimavГҘn (the deity
presiding over the HimГҘlayas) standing before him in all her splendour and adorned with the best of
ornaments of all kinds. Indra was delighted to see goddess PГҘrvatвҲӮ and thought that since she constantly
lived with BhagavГҘn вҖәiva, the embodiment of Knowledge, she must be aware who the YakВ¶a was. Therefore,
approaching her with great humility, he asked her to disclose the identity of the YakВ¶a who having revealed
himself before him, had forthwith disappeared. UmГҘ replied, Г¬the YakВ¶a was no other than Brahma. It was
Brahma who vanquished the demons. You were merely His instruments. Your glory increased because of
this victory of Brahma. It was His victory that had enhanced your glory and made you objects of worship.
It is your vanity that make you feel triumphant and glorified. Give up this false pride and know that
whatever happens is attributable to Him.Г®
This revelation made by UmГҘ opened the eyes of Indra and humbled his pride. Awakened to a sense
of the power of Brahma, Indra returned to his abode in heaven and imparted the knowledge of Brahma to
the gods of fire and wind as well. Thus these two gods also came to know Brahma. It was this knwledge
of Brahma which gave these three precedence over all other gods. Of these three, again, Indra occupies the
highest place, because he was the first to know Brahma.

516
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
By calling VГҘsudeva His own self, the
Lord 
establishes 
identity 
between 
a
manifestation of God and God himself. The
intention is to show that it is the integral
Brahma or the Supreme Person, the unborn,
immortal and almighty Overlord of all created
beings, who manifested Himself as вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a,
son of Vasudeva (IV.6). Therefore, he who
regards the Lord as an ordinary human being
commits an egregious blunder.
Even though YudhiВ¶Вўhira was the eldest
of the PГҘЖ’Е’avas, a highly virtuous soul and a
great devotee of God, yet it is Arjuna who is
recognized as the greatest of the PГҘЖ’Е’avas.
The reason for this is that when the Lord
appeared as the sage NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a, Arjuna was
present with Him in the form of Nara. Over
and above this, he was the dearest friend
and a great lover of the Lord. That is why
the Lord declares him to be His very self.*
A Г«MuniГӯ is one who meditates on God
and makes a deep study of the Vedas
and other scriptures. BhagavГҘn VedavyГҘsa
divided the Vedas after thoroughly examining
them, and is the author of the MahГҘbhГҘrata,
the various PurГҘЖ’as and many other
scriptures. He is a part manifestation of God,
and a repository of all noble virtues. Being
thus the foremost of all sages, VyГҘsa has
been declared by the lord as the same as
Himself.
The word Г«KaviВЁГӯ stands for a learned
and wise man. The great вҖәukrГҘcГҘrya is the
lord of the BhГҘrgavas (sons of BhГҰgu), an
adept in all branches of learning, and in the
science of reviving the dead, and the foremost
of the wise. Hence he has been declared to
be the very self of the Lord.вҖ

Among the seven sons, Cyavana and others, of the great sage BhГҰgu, вҖәukra is the principal figure.
Through the worship of God вҖәaвҲҶkara, he obtained knowledge of the science of reviving the dead and a
body free from death and old age and as strong as adamant. It was through the grace of God вҖәaвҲҶkara, again,
that he acquired proficiency in the science of Yoga and attained the position of a teacher of Yoga. He is
the family priest of the demons. His other names are KГҘvya, Kavi and UвҖ№anГҘ. He married the mind-born
daughter of the manes, known by the name of Go. В®aЖ’Е’a and Amarka, the celebrated teachers of PrahlГҘda,
were the sons of вҖәukra. A master of many secret and rare Mantras, proficient in many sciences and
exceptionally wise, вҖәukra is a great adept in politics. His monumental work on political science, known
by the name of Г«вҖәukranвҲӮtiГӯ is widely known. It was from him that Kaca, the son of BГҰhaspati, learnt the
science of revival of the dead. Many wonderful and instructive stories connected with вҖәukra appear in the
MahГҘbhГҘrata, as well as in the BhГҘgavata, VГҘyu, Brahma, Matsya and Skanda PurГҘЖ’as and other works.

* Chapter X *
517
Deterring the unrighteous and unruly
element from sinful conduct, the fear of
punishment urges them to right action. It is
through self-restraint that the mind and
senses of man are controlled and help him in
God-realization. It is through fear of punishment
that all living beings perform their respective
functions. Hence the authority possessed by
gods, kings and rulers who rule according to
law has been declared by the Lord to be the
same as Himself.
The word Г«NвҲӮtiГӯ, in this verse, stands
for righteousness. It is righteousness alone
which brings one true victory. A Government
which abandons righteousness, and follows
the path of unrighteousness, is doomed.
Therefore, righteousness, is the best means
I am the subduing power in rulers; I am righteousness in those who seek to
conquer. Of things to be kept secret, I am the custodian in the form of reticence; and
I am the wisdom of the wise.                                                              (38)
for the attainment of victory. Hence the Lord
declares righteousness to be the same as
Himself.
Secrets can be maintained only through
reticence. Without restraint over speech, it is
exceptionally 
difficult 
to 
preserve 
a
secret. Reticence being thus the best custodian
of secrets, the Lord speaks of it as His own
self.
The word Г«JВӨГҘnavatГҘmГӯ stands for those
truly wise men who have realized the supreme
Brahma or God. Their knowledge alone is
the best of all knowledge. Hence the Lord
declares such knowledge to be His own self.
In verse 17 of Chapter XIII too the Lord
likewise declares Himself to be the same as
Knowledge.
ГҝГ¬ГҠГҠГҒВ¬ вҖҡп¬ӮВёГ·ГҚГғГҠЕёГҠВў вҖҷГӢВЎВў ГғЕ’вҖһUв—ҠВЎВёГҲЕёГ”вҖ“

Arjuna, I am even That which is the seed of all life. For there is no creature,
moving or unmoving, which exists without Me.                                      (39)
with all. That is to say, all creatures, animate
or inanimate, are pervaded by Him, and
there is no creature whatsoever which is
devoid of His presence. Therefore, regarding
all beings as identical with God, and
knowing them as pervaded by Him, one
should think of God alone in the form
of whatever presents itself before his mind.
This concludes the reply to ArjunaГӯs
question contained in X.17, viz., in what
particular forms He should be meditated upon
by him.
God alone is the ultimate support and
substratum of the whole of this animate and
inanimate creation; it is from Him that everything
originates. Hence He alone is the seed or
ultimate cause of all. It is for this reason that
in VII.10 He has been declared as the Г¬eternal
seedГ® of all beings, and in IX.18 as the
Г¬imperishable seedГ®. Hence, in the present
verse, He proclaims His identity with the
seed of all beings.
By the latter half of this verse the Lord
reveals His omnipresence and His identity
In verse 19 the Lord stated that there was no end to His divine glories, and therefore

518
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
By the first half of this verse the Lord
indicates that even His divine glories are
unlimited, much more His ordinary glories.
Even as the particles of water and earth
cannot be counted, so it is impossible to
count His glories. They are so numerous that
they can neither be known nor described by
anyone. His glories are endless in the countless
universes, and none can ever expect to find
their limit.
By the second half of the verse the Lord
intends to convey that the extent of His
divine glories described in this chapter represents
only a small fraction of them and that their
full description is very difficult. Hence He
concludes the discussion of those glories.
described those glories. Now He winds up the topic revealing once more the infinite variety
of His divine glories.
ЕёГҠГЈГғГҠпЈҝЛҳГҒSГғ в—Ҡв—Ҡ ГҒЕ’вҲҡГҝГҠЕёГҠВў ГҒп¬ӮГ·ГҚГғГӢЕёГҠВў В¬вҒ„UГЈГғВ¬Г”вҖ“

In verse 18 Arjuna requested the Lord to describe His glories and His power of Yoga.
Having described them accordingly, He now briefly describes His power of Yoga.

manifestation of My effulgence.
Every such being as is glorious, brilliant and powerful, know that to be a part
manifestation of My glory.                                                               (41)
The electric current gives us light, turns
our fans, draws water for us and works our
radio machine and performs many other
functions at various places. It is, however, a
settled fact that wherever any such activity
takes place, it is all due to the electrical
power; in fact, it is a manifestation of a
fraction of the electric current. Even so,
whatever speciality we observe in a particular
living being or object should be regarded as
a part manifestation of the divine glory.
The word Г«SattvamГӯ, qualified by
the adjectives Г«VibhВөutimatГӯ, Г«вҖәrвҲӮmatГӯ and
Г«ВӘUrjitamГӯ covers all living beings or inanimate
objects that are possessed of virtues like
glory, elegance and comeliness etc., and
endowed with strength, energy, valour or
any other power. And regarding every living
being or object endowed with all or even one
of those qualities as a fraction of the divine
glory is what is meant by knowing it as a
part manifestation of the divine effulgence.

* Chapter X *
519
The indeclinable Г«AthavГҘГӯ introduces an
alternative suggestion or something different
from what has been stated above. It is intended
to convey that the Lord is now going to say
something different from, and more remarkable
than, what has already been confided to him,
viz., a description of His outstanding glories
as contained in verses 20 to 39 and of the
various media of His manifestation enumerated
in verse 41.
Although at the request of Arjuna the
Lord described His conspicuous glories in
the foregoing verses, it is not all that he
should know. The most important thing is
that which He is now going to reveal, and
which Arjuna is now called upon to grasp
thoroughly. Everything else will then be
automatically clear to him, and nothing more
will remain still to be known by him.
The word Г«JagatГӯ, qualified by the
adjectives Г«IdamГӯ and Г«KГҰtsnamГӯ, stands for
the entire universe consisting of the different
worlds and all animate and inanimate beings
inhabiting them with their mind, senses
and body, objects and places of enjoyment
etc. That the Lord holds countless such
universes in a fragment of His being
through His own Yogic power is what is
sought to be conveyed by the latter half of
this verse.
Having thus referred to the manifestation of a fraction of His glory or Yogic power in
all principal objects and beings, the Lord now reveals that He holds the entire universe by
a mere fraction of His Yogic power.

520
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Title of the
Chapter
Summary of
the Chapter
Лҷ
вҖәrвҲӮmadbhagavad-GвҲӮtГҘ
Chapter XI
At the request of Arjuna, the Lord reveals to him His Cosmic Body in this chapter.
A major part of the chapter is solely devoted to a description of the Cosmic
Body and praises offered by Arjuna to the Lord manifested in that
form; hence it has been given the title of Г¬The Yoga of the Vision of the
Cosmic Body.Г®
Verses 1 to 4 of this chapter contain tributes of praise to the Lord and His teaching, and
ArjunaГӯs prayer for a vision of His Cosmic Body, From the fifth to the eighth He calls upon
Arjuna to behold in His body the whole universe consisting of gods, men,
beasts, birds and all other animate and inanimate beings with many wonderful
sights never seen before, and finally offers to vouchsafe the divine eye to him.
Reporting the revelation by the Lord of His Cosmic Body to Arjuna in the ninth, SaВӨjaya
describes from the tenth to the thirteenth the form as seen by Arjuna. The fourteenth
describes how Arjuna bowed to the Lord with reverence, full of wonder and joy at the sight
of that form. From the fifteenth to the thirtyfirst, Arjuna offers praises to the Cosmic Body
and describes what he beheld in that form, and finally requests the Lord to reveal His identity
to him. From the thirty-second to the thirtyfourth the Lord declares Himself as KГҘla, the
destroyer of the worlds, and BhвҲӮВ¶ma, DroЖ’a and all other brave warriors as already killed
by Him, and finally encourages and enjoins Arjuna to fight. Thereafter, verses 35 to 46
describe how Arjuna, wonderstruck and terrified at the words of the Lord, offers his praises
and obeisance to Him and begging His forgiveness prays Him to reveal His four-armed
divine form. Revealing the glory and rarity of His Cosmic Body in the fortyseventh and
fortyeighth, the Lord consoles Arjuna in the fortyninth and directs him to behold the four-
armed divine form. In the fiftieth, SaВӨjaya describes how the Lord first revealed the four-
armed form, and then resumed the human semblance. In the fiftyfirst, Arjuna tells the Lord
how he was composed and returned to his normal state at the sight of the LordГӯs delightful
human form. Thereafter, pointing out in the fiftysecond and fiftythird the rarity of the vision
of His four-armed Divine Form, the Lord declares in the fifty-fourth that the same can be
easily seen, known and entered into through single-minded devotion. The chapter is
concluded after revealing in the fiftyfifth the true nature and reward of exclusive devotion.
Having briefly described His glory and power of Yoga and the value of knowing them
in the first seven verses of Chapter X, the Lord devoted verses 8 to 11 of that
chapter to a discussion of Bhaktiyoga (the path of Devotion) and its fruit.
Thereupon, from the twelfth to the eighteenth, Arjuna extolled the Lord and
requested Him to describe in detail His divine glories and power of Yoga. Then, having
described His glories up to the fortieth, the Lord finally revealed the glory of His power of
Yoga and concluded the chapter by declaring that the entire universe is held in a mere
Link of the
Discourse

* Chapter XI *
521

Arjuna said: Thanks to the most profound words of spiritual wisdom that You
have spoken out of kindness to me; this delusion of mine has entirely disappeared. (1)
through the grace of God Himself he comes
to recognize the divine grace, and when
he perceives as if with his own eyes that
whatever is happening is happening through
the grace of God, he is filled with gratitude
and exclaims from the bottom of his heart,
Г¬O Lord, I am worth nothing at all, and am
wholly unqualified. All this is an exhibition
of Your grace alone.Г® With a heart full of
gratitude, Arjuna likewise says in this verse,
Г¬Lord, I am hardly qualified to hear all that
You have  said about Your own power and
glory. It is out of sheer kindness that You
have 
revealed 
this 
closely 
guarded
secret of Yours to me.Г® That is the implication
of the word Г«MadanugrahГҘyaГӯ, used in this
verse.
The word Г«VacaВЁГӯ qualified by the
adjectives 
Г«ParamaвүҲГӯ, 
Г«GuhyaвүҲГӯ 
and
Г«AdhyГҘtmasaвүҲjВӨitamГӯ, covers the supreme
words of wisdom referred to in X.1 and
acutally contained in verses 2 to 11, the
subsequent description of His glories and
power of Yoga given in verses 20 to 42 in
response to ArjunaГӯs further query, and lastly
the exposition of the truth and inner meaning
of His virtues, glory, power and identity
embodied in Chapters VII to IX under the
ArjunaГӯs heart was filled with joy, love
and gratitude when he heard the divine glory
of the Lord, who is an ocean of love, revealed
by Him in the beginning of Chapter X with
the remark that he was confiding all that to
him out of solicitude for him, knowing his
intense love for Him. Arjuna thought how
kind it was of the Lord, who is the supreme
Ruler of all the worlds, to acknowledge an
insignificant creature like him as his endearing
lover and go on openly revealing to him the
closely guarded secrets of His own glory. He
now remembered the words of the sages, and
singing His praises with utmost faith, he
renewed his loving prayer to the Lord for a
detailed description of His power of Yoga
and glories. The Lord responded to his prayer
and gave him a brief description of His
glories and Yoga. ArjunaГӯs heart now bore
the stamp of divine grace on it. He was
beside himself with joy at this unusual
exhibition of divine grace on him.
The supreme benefit or divine grace is
practically denied to the SГҘdhaka so long as
he remains conscious of his own effort, spiritual
practice or capacity. He cannot easily climb
to the higher rungs of spiritual ladder without
the help of the divine grace. When, however,

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
name of Г«JВӨГҘnaГӯ and Г«VijВӨГҘnaГӯ that is,
Knowledge of both NirguЖ’a Brahma and
qualified Divinity. In the course of these
teachings, the Lord has fully revealed His
identity. This is what is sought to be conveyed
by the use of the adjectives Г«ParamamГӯ
Г«GuhyamГӯ and Г«AdhyГҘtma-saвүҲjВӨitamГӯ with
the noun Г«VacaВЁГӯ in the present verse.
The Lord has Himself characterized as
most secret all those teachings in which He
has dealt with His own reality, virtues and
glory and exhorted Arjuna to take refuge in
Him, and in which he has plainly told Arjuna
that his friend вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a standing before him
was no other than the Almighty God Himself,
the creator and destroyer of the whole universe,
who represents both the NirguЖ’a and SaguЖ’a
aspects and is both embodied and disembodied,
who lies entirely beyond MГҘyГҘ, and is the
substratum and support of all.
ArjunaГӯs delusion or ignorance consisted
in his lack of full knowledge of the virtues,
glory, power and being of God. Through the
above teachings of the Lord he has now
partially realized these virtues, glory, power,
mystery and reality of God and come to
know that вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a is God Himself. This is
what is meant by his disillusionment.
Г·п¬ӮГҠГҘГҝГҝГҠГ’ ГҒвҖһU Г·ГҚГғГҠЕёГҠВў ГјГҠГҲГғГҠГ’ ГҒп¬ӮSГғвҒ„UвүҘГҠГҠпЈҝ в—ҠГҝГҠГ”вҖ“

For, KГҰВ¶Ж’a, I have heard from You in detail an account of the evolution and
dissolution of beings, and also Your immortal glory.                                   (2)
is altogether unconcerned; though all-pervasive,
He remains wholly untouched by the virtues
and vices of things; though dispensing the
fruits of good and evil deeds in the shape of
joys and sorrows, He is free from the faults
of cruelty and discrimination; and manifested
in the forms of PrakГҰti (Matter), KГҘla (Time)
and all regional gods, He is the almighty
Ruler of all. This is the immortal glory of the
Lord referred to in the latter half of the verse.
It is from God that all animate and
inanimate beings emanate; it is by Him that
they are maintained and into Him that they
all enter. Arjuna has repeatedly heard this in
detail from the lips of the Lord in Chapters
VII to X. This is what he means by the first
half of this verse.
Though creating, maintaining and
destroying the whole universe, God is in
reality a non-doer; though controlling all, He

=== PAGE 524 ===

* Chapter XI *
523
By addressing the Lord as Г«ParameвҖ№vara.
Arjuna seeks to convey that вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a was
the supreme Lord of the entire creation, and
thus capable of doing everything. He could,
therefore, easily reveal to Arjuna the divine
form which he longed to see. Again, by
addressing Him as Г«PuruВ¶ottamaГӯ he shows
that вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a was the Supreme Person,
beyond both the perishable and the
imperishable. Arjuna, accordingly hoped that
He would condescend to fulfil his desire for
a vision of the divine form.
Whatever has been spoken by the Lord
about Himself in the course of the description
of His virtues, glory, reality and divine powers
is wholly true; Arjuna had not the least doubt
about it. This is what he means by the first
half of this verse.
The word Г«RВөupamГӯ, qualified by the
adjective Г«AiвҖ№varam,Гӯ stands for that divine
form of the Lord which exhibits, in a
pronounced form, all divine virtues and powers
such as unbounded and undying knowledge,
energy, strength, valour and effulgence etc.,
and a fraction of which holds the entire
universe. ArjunaГӯs longing to see that form
showed that he had never seen it before.
Hearing of it from the Lord (X.42) he had
developed an irresistible yearning to see that
form, and he now felt and believed that the
object of his existence would be easily
acomplished by a vision of that form.
It may be contended here that ArjunaГӯs
longing to see that form betrays lack of
faith on his part in the words of вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a;
for if he had implicit faith in His words
and entertained not the least doubt about it,
why should he need an ocular proof ?
We shall, however, presently see that the
objection is wholly untenable. Suppose a
man who always spoke the truth and
possessed a philosopherГӯs stone or any other
wonderful object, confided the secret to a
friend, and the latter was fully convinced of
the presence of that wonderful object with
the former. If, however, this report excites
his curiosity and stimulates in him the desire
to see it with his own eyes simply because he
had not seen it before, the desire itself or his
giving vent to the same does not necessarily
imply lack of faith in the words of that
truthful man. Even so, since Arjuna had
never seen that wonderful form before, the
desire on his part to see it and his giving
expression to that desire cannot be interpreted
as having proceeded from lack of faith. On
the other hand, the desire to see it presupposes
his faith.
в—ҠГЈГҝвҖҡпЈҝ ГҝГҒЕ’ ГғГ«ВҝUГ„ГҝВў в—ҠГҝГҠ ВәЛқcвүҲГҲГҒв—ҠГҒГғ В¬ЛқГ·ГҠпЈҝГ”вҖ“
ГҝГҠпЈҝВӘпЈҝEвҒ„U ГғГғГҠпЈҝ в—ҠпЈҝ Г ГёВў Е’вүҘГҠВёГҝГҠГ в—ҠГҠЕёв—ҠвҲҡГҝГҝв—ҠЛңГ”H 4H
в—ҠГЈГҝвҖҡпЈҝ think; ГҝГҒЕ’ if; ГғГғЛңГ” that (form); вүҘГҠГ„Гҝв—ҠЛңГ” в—ҠГҝГҠ ВәЛқcвүҲГҲв—ҠЛңГ” is capable of being seen by me; ГҹГҒГғГ”
so; В¬ЛқГ·ГҠпЈҝ O Lord; ГҝГҠпЈҝВӘпЈҝEвҒ„UГ” O Lord of Yoga; ГғГғвҖ” then; в—ҠпЈҝГ” to me; Г п¬Ӯв—ҠЛңГ” You; Е’вүҘГҠВёГҝ reveal; вҖўГҠГ в—ҠГҠЕёв—ҠЛңГ”
вҖўвҲҡГҝГҝв—ҠЛңГ” that imperishable form.
KГҰВ¶Ж’a, if You think that it can be seen by me, then, O Lord of Yoga, reveal to
me Your imperishable form.                                                             (4)
that divine form, the Lord could easily render
him fit for the same by His own grace and
power. And by addressing вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a as
Г«YogeвҖ№varaГӯ Arjuna seeks to convey that
being the Master of all Yogas He could, if
He liked, easily enable Arjuna to see His
The use of the vocative Г«PrabhoГӯ with
reference to вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a indicates that being
the creator, preserver and destroyer of the
whole creation, and the inner controller of
all, He is all-powerful. Therefore, even if
Arjuna was not duly qualified for a vision of

=== PAGE 525 ===

524
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
divine form. Indeed, when even an ordinary
YogвҲӮ is capable of revealing his glory in
ways more than one, it should be much
easier for вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a to do so.
As stated above, Arjuna believed that
the glory of вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a was exactly as it had
been depicted by Himself, and he entertained
not the least doubt about it. He was also
convinced that even if the Lord failed to
reveal that form before him, such failure on
His part would neither prove that the Lord of
all Masters of Yogas was incapable of doing
it, nor would it detract in the least from his
faith in His words. This much was, however,
certain that his yearning for a vision of that
divine form was irresistible. And as the knower
of all hearts, it was open to the Lord to
examine and ascertain whether that desire
was genuine and intense. If it was found
true, it established his eligibility for that vision.
For, being a veritable wish yielding tree to
His devotees, the longing of the devoteeГӯs
heart was all that He cared for, and no other
qualifications. Arjuna, therefore, prayed that
if the Lord deemed fit he should reveal that
form to him.
Thus requested by Arjuna, His supremely loving and faithful devotee, the Lord
proceeds to describe His Cosmic Body in the next three verses, and enjoins Arjuna to behold
the same.
ГјГҠГӢГ·ВӘп¬ӮГҠЕёГҲUп¬ӮГҠГё

species, such as gods, human beings and
other lower forms of beings, both animate
and inanimate.

brought forth by His wornderful power of
Yoga.

The word Г«NГҘnГҘvidhГҘniГӯ indicates that
