and which have been enumerated in Chapter
VII above under the names of Г«BhВөumiГӯ, (earth),
Г«Г…paВЁГӯ (water), Г«AnalaГӯ (fire), Г«VГҘyuГӯ (air)
Г«KhaГӯ (ether).
Г«AhaвҲҶkГҘraГӯ or the Ego is an aspect of
the cosmic Mind. It is the cause of the five
TanmГҘtrГҘs, the mind and all the senses, and
is an effect of the Mahat-Tattva. It is also
known as the principle of Г«I-nessГӯ (the feeling
of Г«IГӯ).
Г«BuddhiГӯ, also known as the Mahat-
Tattva and Cosmic Intelligence, is an aspect
of the cosmic Mind, and represents the faculty
of determination or judgment.
The word Г«AvyaktaГӯ (Unmanifest) stands
for Primordial Matter, which is the cause of
all categories from Mahat-Tattva onwards,
and is known by the name of Г«PradhГҘnaГӯ in
SГҘвҲҶkhya philosophy, and which has been
referred to as Г«Mahad BrahmaГӯ in Chapter
XIV and as Г«PrakГҰtiГӯ in verse 19 of the
present chapter.
The tongue, hands, and feet, the
generative organ and the organ of defecationГі
these are the five organs of action; while the
senses of hearing, touch, sight, taste and
smell constitute the five senses of perception.
These are the ten Indriyas referred to in the
latter half of this verse. The principle of Ego
or AhaвҲҶkГҘra is the cause of them all.
The word Г«EkamГӯ stands for the mind,
which represents the cogitative faculty of the
cosmic Mind, and whose function is to reflect
and speculate. This is also an evolute of the
Ego.
Sound, touch, colour, taste and smell,
which are the objects of the five senses of
perception, are denoted by the words Г«PaВӨca
IndriyagocarГҘВЁГӯ.
other effect. Of these the eleven Indriyas are the effects of the ego, while the five gross elements are the
evolutes of the five TanmГҘtrГҘs. PuruВ¶a or the Spirit, however, is neither the cause nor the effect of anything
else. He is altogether unattached.
In the Yoga-SВөutras we read: ГҒп¬ӮвүҘГҠпЈҝВ·ГҠГҒп¬ӮвүҘГҠпЈҝВ·ГҒвҲӮUXГ”Uв—ҠГҠГІГҠГҠГҒвҲӮUXГ”UГҠГҒЕё ВӘГҲГЎГҠВ¬п¬ӮГҠВёГҒГЎГҠ (II.19) Г¬The ViвҖ№eВ¶as, that is, the five
senses of perception, the five organs of action, the mind and the five gross elements; the AviвҖ№eВ¶as, that
is, the ego and the five TanmГҘtrГҘs, the LiвҲҶgamГҘtra, that is, Mahat-Tattva; and the AliвҲҶga or Primordial

or objective existencesГ®.
What has been termed as Г«DГҰвҖ№yaГӯ in the Yoga-Sutras has been referred to as the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ in the GвҲӮtГҘ.

The word Г«IcchГҘГӯ in this verse denotes a
passionate longing for the acquisition of an
object which one regards as a source of
gratification and as relieving oneГӯs pain. There
are variours phases of this longing, which are
covered by such expressions as desire, thirst,
hope, craving, yearning etc. It is a modification
of the mind; hence it has been reckoned as
an evolute of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ.
Antipathy towards a thing which one
conceives to be a source of pain or as interfering
with oneГӯs pleasure or comfort is called DveВ¶a.
Animosity, jealously, hatred, anger etc., are
some of its gross forms. This is also a
modification of the mind. Hence it has been
counted as an evolute of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ.
The pleasurable feeling aroused in the
mind by the presence of that which is agreeable
and the disappearance of that which is
disagreeable is called Sukha. Being a
modification of the mind, it is also regarded
as an evolute of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ.
The agony caused in the mind by the
presence of that which is disagreeable and
the disappearance of that which is agreeable
is called Г«DuВЁkhaГӯ. This too represents a
modification of the mind and as such has
been recognized as an evolute of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ.
Г«SaвҲҶghГҘtaГӯ here stands for the corporeal
or physical body, composed of the five gross
elements, which is left behind, open to the
view of all, even on the departure of the
subtle body after death. Being a modification
of the five gross elements stated above, it has
also been regarded as an evolute of the
Г«KВ¶etraГӯ.
The word Г«CetanГҘГӯ signifies consciousness
or the cognizing faculty of the mind, by
which we feel pleasure and pain and cognize
all other objects, and which has already been
mentioned in X.22. Being a faculty of the
mind, it has also been counted as an evolute
of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ.
Г«DhГҰtiГӯ denotes Г«firmnessГӯ. It is the same
quality which has been mentioned in XVIII.33,
34 and 35 as of three kinds, viz., SГҘttvika,
RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika, and the SГҘttvika
type of which has been included in the divine
virtues enumerated in XVI.3. Being a
modification of the mind, it has also been
recognized as an evolute of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ.
Thus far have the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and its evolutes
been briefly mentioned, the former in verse 5
and the latter in the present one. This is what
the Lord seeks to convey through the last
quarter of the above verse.
Speaking thus of the nature of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and its evolutes, the Lord now enumerates
in the next five verses, under the name of Г«JВӨГҘnaГӯ the virtues which make for JВӨГҘna or
spiritual wisdom, which has been declared in verse 2 above as consisting in the knowledge
of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and the Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ.

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Г«MГҘnitvaГӯ or self-esteem consists in
looking upon oneself as an eminent person,
and an object of reverence and worship or a
towering personality, and coveting honour,
fame, respect and adoration from others, or
feeling delighted when all these come unasked
for. And absence of all this is Г«AmГҘnitvaГӯ or
absence of pride. Far from rejoicing when
honour, respect and worship etc., are offered
to him, the man who has fully developed this
virtue feels disgusted with all these and grows
indifferent towards them.
Г«DambhitvaГӯ consists in exhibiting oneself
as a devout or charitably-disposed man, a
devotee, 
one 
possessed 
of 
spiritual
enlightenment, or a saint, and in simulating
piety, generosity, liberality, devotion to God,
practice of Yoga and observance of religious
vows and fasts etc., or any other virtue for
the sake of honour, prestige and worship
etc., out of greed for wealth or with the
object of deceiving others. And Г«AdambhitvamГӯ
denotes complete absence of Г«DambhitvaГӯ.
Being entirely free from the desire for honour
and fame, the man who has fully developed
this virtue feels shy even in making known
to others his genuine pious sentiments, noble
virtues and devotional practices. It is, therefore,
out of question for him to display virtues
which he does not acutally possess.
Г«AhiвүҲsГҘГӯ means total abstenation from
violence or infliction of pain in any form or
shape on any creature whatsoever through
body, speech or mind, e.g., harbouring ill-
will towards another in oneГӯs mind; abusing
or vilifying another, using harsh language, or
uttering unpleasant and harmful words with
reference to another; and dealing a physical
blow or causing hurt to another or inflicting
injury in any form whatsoever on others. The
SГҘdhaka who has fully developed Г«AhiвүҲsГҘГӯ
ceases to bear enmity or ill-will towards
anyone; therefore no harm is ever done by
him to any creature whatsoever, nor is anyone
eventually aggrieved by him, nor again does
he actually cause fear to anyone. The great
sage PataВӨjali goes so far as to say that even
carnivorous animals living near him shed
their natural animosities.*
Г«KВ¶antiГӯ means forbearance. He who has
developed this quality never entertains the idea
of punishing a wrong-doer even indirectly.
He nurses no thought of revenge, nor would
he have the evil-doer punished for his wrongs
here or hereafter. In fact, he refuses to recognize
his faults as such, and wholly forgets them.
The idea has been explained at some length
in the commentary on X.4.
Г«Г…rjavaГӯ means straightness of mind,
speech and behaviour. The SГҘdhaka who has
fully developed this quality has straight dealings
with all; crookedness is entirely absent in
him. In other words, his dealings are wholly
free from artifice, duplicity or dishonesty. He
is ever straight and above board.
He who imparts knowledge and teaches
good precepts is called an Г…cГҘrya. Living
with such a teacher and devoutly striving in
every way with body, speech and mind to
bring him happiness, making obeisance to
him, obeying his commands and behaving in
conformity with his wishesГіall this is included
in Г«Г…cГҘryopГҘsanaГӯ.
Г«вҖәaucaГӯ means purity. Purity of money
is secured by honest and truthful dealings,
while purity of diet is ensured by food procured
through well-earned money. Conduct is purified
by correct and appropriate behaviour, and
the body is purified by cleaning it with water,
earth etc. All this is external purity. Internal
Absence of pride, freedom from hypocrisy, non-violence, forbearance, uprightness
of speech and mind etc., devout service of the preceptor, internal and external purity,
steadfastness of mind and control of body, mind and the senses.

purity comes when the mind gets purged of

Г«SthairyaГӯ denotes firmness, i.e., remaining
unshaken even in the face of the greatest
trials and tribulations, danger or sorrow,
swerving not an inch in any case from oneГӯs

The word Г«Г…tmanГӯ, forming part of the

Г«Г…tmavinigrahaГӯ, therefore, means control over

acquired complete mastery over his self act
as his obedient servants; they can no longer
entangle him into cobweb of sensuous
pleasures, and remain constantly engaged in
spiritual practice according to his will.

Dispassion towards the objects of enjoyment of this world and the next, and also
absence of egotism, pondering again and again on the pain and evils inherent in birth,
death, old age and disease.                                                                (8)
species of beings. Death also involves terrible
sufferings to the JвҲӮva. One has to leave under
compulsion the body and the home which one
has regarded as oneГӯs own for a whole life
time. An idea of the agonies of death can be
formed from the despondent looks and bodily
suffering of a dying person. The sufferings
of old age too are not negligible; the organs
get enfeebled, the senses lose their power, the
body becomes worn out; the mind is ever tossed
by longings of various kinds, and one is reduced
to a helpless condition. The hardships
undergone in such a state are indeed terrible.
Even so the agonies of ailment too are extremely
painful. The body gets emaciated, sufferings
of various kinds become unbearable and one
has to depend on others for so many things.
It is indeed a helpless state. These are the various
forms of suffering inherent in birth, death, old
age and disease.
Г«IndriyГҘrtheВ¶u 
VairГҘgyamГӯ 
denotes
complete absence of attachment for all the
objects of enjoyment of this world and the
next in the form of sound, touch, colour,
taste and smell, which are relished by the
mind and the senses, and which though
ignorantly regarded as a source of delight
are really productive of sorrow.
Г«AnahankГҘraГӯ means complete absence
of identification with the mind, intellect and
the body, which though other than the self
are ignorantly regarded as the self.
The pangs of birth are no ordinary pangs.
First of all, the helpless JвҲӮva undergoes various
forms of suffering, for a considerable length
of time, in the womb of the mother. Then,
during the process of birth, it has to suffer
unbearable pain while coming out of the womb.
The JвҲӮva has to go through these pangs in
the course of repeated births in the various

=== PAGE 597 ===

596
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Birth, death, old age and disease are
undergone by JвҲӮvas as a result of their sins.
Hence they are all full of evils. Constantly
dwelling on this idea is what is meant by
seeing evils in them.
As a matter of fact with the exception of
the conscious Self there is nothing in this
world which is free from these four evils.
A house is built, this constitutes its birth;
a part of it is broken, this represents its
diseased condition, the broken part is repaired,
the evil is remedied; the house becomes old,
this represents its old age; it is now past
repairs. Reaching the state of dilapidation it
collapses; this represents its death. All things,
great and small, go through this process.
Thus observing all objects of the world as
subject to birth, death, old age and disease,
one should cultivate dispassion towards
them.
identification can be set aside only by
overcoming the feeling of mineness. Hence the
sense in which we have interpreted the word
Г«AnabhiВ¶vangaГӯ seems to be the correct sense
of the term.
Contact 
with 
agreeable 
persons,
actions, events and objects and the
disappearance of what is disagreeable is
welcome (IВ¶Вўa) to all. Similarly, the
disappearance of what is agreeable, and contact
with what is disagreeable is Г«unwelcomeГӯ
(AniВ¶Вўa) to all. Absence of joy in the presence
of what is agreeable and on the disappearance
of what is disagreeable, and absence of grief,
fear and anger in the presence of what is
disagreeable and on the disappearance of
what is agreeable, that is, remaining unmoved,
even-tempered and equipoised under all
circumstancesГіthis is what is meant by
Г«equipoise of mind both in favourable and
unfavourable circumstancesГӯ.
Close association with oneГӯs wife and
children, home and wealth etc., generally
makes for special attachment to them. Even
after one has developed dispassion towards
ordinary objects of senses, this attachment
for wife, son etc., often remains lurking behind.
That is why absence of attachment for these
has been separately and specially mentioned
in this verse over and above dispassion towards
objects of enjoyment, mentioned in verse 8
above, which ordinarily covers the absence
of all forms of attachment.
The word Г«AnabhiВ¶vangaГӯ cannot be taken
to mean Г«absence of egotismГӯ as the same has
already been referred to in the preceding verse
by the term Г«AnahankГҘraГӯ. It is the feeling of
mineness which is responsible for close
identification with oneГӯs wife and children etc.
That is why the joys and sorrows, as well as
the gain and loss, of the latter make one
personally 
happy 
and 
unhappy.

* Chapter XIII *
597
Г«AnanyayogaГӯ means getting exclusively
united with God as the highest of all beings
and as oneГӯs sole lord, worth resorting to, the
supreme goal, the highest refuge, oneГӯs father,
mother, brother and friend, the greatest
benefactor, the nearest relation and everything,
and treating nobody else as oneГӯs own.
And cultivating steady, consummate and
unadulterated love for God conceived as above
and practising constant remembrance of and
meditation on Him is what is meant by
unflinching devotion to Him through exclusive
attachment.
The man who practises such devotion
ceases to have any trace of selfishness or
egotism in him and he no longer claims
anything belonging to this world as his own.
He is connected with the world only through
God, he ceases to have any direct relation
with anybody. He views everything as
belonging to God, and is constantly engaged
in meditating on God in a disinterested spirit
with reverence and love. Whatever he does,
he does for the sake of God.
Any secluded, lonely and consecrated
place, e.g., a temple or seat of religious
austerity, that bank of a holy river like the
Ganges, a sacred woodland or mountain cave
etc., which is free from bustle and noise and
is uninhabited by others, which when used
as a residence evokes no objection and causes
no vexation to others, which is free from dirt,
refuse, pebbles and thorns, which has lovely
natural surroundings which has a salubrious
climate and a holy atmosphere, which is free
from infectious diseases, and is not infested
by carnivorous animals, and which is free
from violence and surcharged with SГҘttvika
vibrations, is called Г«ViviktadeвҖ№aГӯ; and residing
in such a place with the object of carrying on
practices for Self-realization is what is referred
to here as Г«ViviktadeвҖ№asevitvamГӯ.
The word Г«JanasaвүҲsadiГӯ in present context
means the company of men steeped in error
and attached to worldly enjoyments. And
abhorring such company as a positive hindrance
to spiritual life is what is meant by finding no
enjoyment in the company of men. Association
with saints, exalted souls and aspirants, on the
other hand, is helpful to spiritual practice;
therefore, Г«JanasaвүҲsadiГӯ should not be taken
to refer to the company of such men.
в—ҠГҒГҝГ” to Me (the supreme Lord); Гё and; вҖўЕёГЈГҝГҝГҠпЈҝВӘпЈҝЕёГ” through exclusive attachment of mind;

598
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Self has absolutely no relation with them.
Г«AdhyГҘtma-JВӨГҘnaГӯ or self-knowledge consists
in thus realizing the truth of the Self through
the scriptures and teachings of the preceptor.
And conclusively determining this truth by
the intellect, and dwelling on it constantly
with the mind is what is meant by Г«constancy
in Self-knowledge.Гӯ
The object of true knowledge is the
integral Brahma or God, who is Truth,
Consciousness and Bliss solidified; for it is He
who is realized through such knowledge.
Constantly visualizing that transcendent Reality
as equally present everywhere is what is meant
by Г«seeing the object of true knowledge.Гӯ
The virtues enumerated in the preceding
verses, from Г«AmГҘnitvamГӯ in verse 7 to
Г«TattvajВӨГҘnГҘrthadarвҖ№anamГӯ in the present one,
are all conducive to the attainment of
Knowledge; therefore they have also been
given the name of Knowledge. The intention
of this is to make it clear that the statement of
the Lord contained in verse 2 above, viz.,
that the knowledge of Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ
is what He considers as true knowledge, should
not lead the reader to think that having
understood that Г«KВ¶etraГӯ stands for the body
and Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ denotes the indwelling subject
or Self, he has attained true knowledge. On
the other hand, true knowledge is that which
follows the realization of the truth about
Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ attained through the
practice of the twenty virtues mentioned above.
It is in order to reveal this truth to the reader
that these virtues have been declared as
synonymous with Knowledge. Thus an
enlightened soul must possess these virtues
from the very start. It is, however, not
indispensable that all these qualities should
appear simultaneously in all SГҘdhakas. Of
course, many of these virtues, which are useful
to all, e.g., absence of pride, freedom from
hypocrisy etc., are invariably present in every
case. Other virtues, such as Г«unflinching
devotionГӯ, Г«living in seclusionГӯ, Г«constancy in
Self-knowledgeГӯ, Г«seeing God as the object
of true knowledgeГӯ etc., may be optional
according to the mode of discipline followed
by a SГҘdhaka.
The opposites of these virtues, viz., lust
for honour and glory, hypocrisy, violence,
anger, duplicity, crookedness, malice, impurity,
fickleness, covetousness, attachment, egotism,
the sense of mineness, differentiation, impiety,
association with evil man and other such evil
propensities are conducive to the growth of
ignorance, which is the cause of birth and
death, and degrade the  soul.  Hence they are
the same as ignorance and as such should be
totally abandoned.
Hearing thus of the means to knowledge under the appellation of Knowledge, one may
ask: What is that which should be known through the Knowledge acquired by such means,
and what is the result of that Knowledge? Anticipating this question, the Lord undertakes
to describe the object of Knowledge and, holding out the Г¬attainment of immortalityГ® as its
reward, devotes the next six verses to a description of God, the object of Knowledge.
ГҙГҠпЈҝГҝВў ГҝГ»ГҠГ В¬Лқп¬ӮЛҮГҝГҠГҒв—Ҡ ГҝГ–ГҙГҠГҠГ п¬ӮГҠв—ҠГҺГғв—ҠoAГҲГғпЈҝвҖ“
вҖўЕёГҠГҒЕ’в—ҠГ В¬вҒ„ВўU вҖҷЛқrГҠГ” Её вҖҡГ»ГҠГӣГҠГҠвҖҡЕ’ГҲГ«ГҝГғпЈҝH 12H
ГҙГҠпЈҝГҝв—ҠЛңГ” (is) worth knowing; ГҝГғЛңГ” which; ГғГғЛңГ” that; В¬Лқп¬ӮЛҮГҝГҠГҒв—Ҡ I shall speak at length about; ГҝГғЛңГ”
(and) which; ГҙГҠГҠГ п¬ӮГҠ having known; вҖўв—ҠГҺГғв—ҠЛңГ” supreme bliss; вҖўoAГҲГғпЈҝ one attains; вҖўЕёГҠГҒЕ’в—ҠГғЛңГ” ruling over

=== PAGE 600 ===

* Chapter XIII *
599
The word Г«JВӨeyamГӯ in this verse stands
for Brahma or God, both absolute and
qualified, who is all truth, all consciousness
and all bliss; for, in this very context, the
Lord speaks of this object of knowledge as
both attributeless and as the enjoyer of attributes
or modes of PrakГҰti.
The word Г«AmГҰtaГӯ denotes God who is
an embodiment of supreme bliss. The intention
is to show that through the knowledge of the
supreme Brahma or God, who is the only
object worth knowing, man gets freed for all
time from the bondage of mundane existence
in the form of repeated births and deaths, and
attains supreme Bliss. It is this supreme Bliss
which is also spoken of as attainment of the
supreme goal or the supreme state.
In verse 19 of this very chapter, the Lord
speaks of both PrakГҰti (Matter) and the individual
soul as beginningless. Being the Lord of both,
the supreme Brahma or the supreme PuruВ¶a
has been referred to here as Г«AnГҘdimatГӯ (holding
sway over beginningless entities).
The use of the word Г«BrahmaГӯ qualified
by the adjective Г«ParamГӯ, is intended to show
that the object of Knowledge referred to here
is no other than the absolute, formless Brahma
or God, who is Truth, Consciousness and
Bliss solidified. The word Г«BrahmaГӯ can also
be taken to denote the Vedas, BrahmГҘ (the
Creator) and PrakГҰti; hence it has been qualified
by the adjective Г«ParamГӯ in order to show
that the object of Knowledge spoken of in
this verse is something different from all these.
That which is established by proof is
called Г«SatГӯ (being). The existence of God,
who is eternal and imperishable and stands
self-proved, cannot be established by any proof;
for all derive their existence from God, who
is beyond all proof. вҖәruti also says: Г¬How
can that knowing subject be known?Г® He is
entirely different from objects which can be
known through various means of cognition;
hence God cannot be called Г«SatГӯ. And that
which has no existence in reality is spoken of
as Г«AsatГӯ (non-being); but it is not true to say
that the supreme Brahma or God has no
existence. He surely exists; nay, the existence
of all else is proved by His existence. Hence
He cannot be called Г«AsatГӯ (non-being) either.
That is why God is beyond both Г«SatГӯ (being)
and Г«AsatГӯ (non-being).
In IX.19 the Lord said that He was
Г«beingГӯ and Г«non-beingГӯ both; whereas in the
present verse He says that God, who is the
only object worth knowing, is neither Г«SatГӯ
(being) nor Г«AsatГӯ (non-being). Although the
above two statements appear as contradictory,
the contradiction is more apparent than real.
For when a positive assertion is made with
reference to God, it is stated that all that
exists is Brahma; and when an attempt is
made to describe Him by the negative method,
it is said that He is Г«neither this nor thatГӯ, but
that He certainly is. In the previous statement
a positive assertion is made with regard to
God; hence it was but reasonable for the
Lord to say, Г¬I am being and non-being
both.Г® But, truly speaking, words cannot
describe the real character of God either by
the positive or by the negative method.
Whatever is said about Him is only intended
to serve as a hint just as we try to locate the
new moon with the help of a bough which
appears adjacent to it; words are utterly
incapable of representing His true character.
вҖәruti also says:Гі
ГҝГғГҠпЈҝ п¬ӮГҠГёГҠпЈҝ ГҒЕёп¬ӮГғВёГЈГғпЈҝ вҖўВ¬ЛқГҠГҘГҝ в—ҠЕёвҖҡГҠ вҖҡвҖһUвҖ“
I shall speak to you at length about that which ought to be known, and knowing
which one attains supreme Bliss. That supreme Brahma, who is the lord of the (two)
beginningless entitiesГі(PrakГҰti and JвҲӮva)Гіis said to be neither Sat (being) nor Asat
(non-being).
(12)

600
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Г¬Failing to reach Whom, speech together
with the mind returneth (that is Brahma).Г®
(Tait up. II.9) It is in order to bring this out
clearly that in the present verse the Lord
negatively describes God as neither Г«SatГӯ
(being) nor Г«AsatГӯ (non-being). He means to
say that the true character of the object of
knowledge sought to be described by Him is
beyond the ken of mind or speech; therefore,
whatever will be said about Him should be
taken to be only a faint indication of His
character and nothing more.
Undertaking thus to describe the reality of what is worth knowing (JВӨeya), the Lord
briefly described that truth; but the truth of that object of Knowledge is deeply profound.
Hence, in order to give an idea of that truth to the SГҘdhaka the Lord further describes it
in detail through such attributes as Г«all-pervasivenessГӯ, etc.
вҖҡп¬ӮВёГғвҖ”В¬ГҠГҒГЎГҠВ¬ГҠЕ’Вў ГғГ вҖҡп¬ӮВёГғГҠпЈҝЛҳГҒЛҮГҠГҒвүҘГҠвҒ„UГҠпЈҝв—ҠГҲПҖв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“

It has hands and feet on all sides, eyes, head and mouth in all directions, and ears
all-round; for It stands pervading all in the universe.                                (13)
of food wherever His devotees offer it to
Him; there is no place where His mouth does
not exist. In other words, God, who is the
only object worth knowing, is the Witness of
all, is all-seeing, and is capable of accepting
the homage and offerings of all.
The statement that Г¬He has ears all
roundГ® likewise describes the all-pervasiveness
of God. It means that He can hear everywhere.
Wherever His devotees offer Him praises or
prayers or make entreaties to Him, He clearly
hears them all.
The concluding assertion that Г¬He stands
pervading all in the universeГ® also proves in
a comprehensive sense the all-pervasive
character of that which is worth knowing.
Just as ether, being the cause of air, fire,
water and earth, stands pervading all those
elements, even so, God, being the cause of
the entire universe consisting of the animate
and inanimate creation, stands pervading them
all. Therefore, everything is full of God.
The assertion that God has hands on all
sides signifies that He is capable of accepting
an offering wherever it is offered to Him.
Similarly, the statement that He has feet every-
where implies that He accepts the prostrations
made at His feet by a devotee wherever he offers
them. For, omnipotent as He is, He can perform
the function of all His senses and other faculties
everywhere. His grasping faculty and moving
faculty are all-pervasive.
The statement that Г¬He has eyes, head
and mouth in all directionsГ®, also shows the
all-pervasive character of that which is worth
knowing. Since he has eyes everywhere,
there is no space where His eyes do not
function; hence nothing is hidden from His
view. Inasmuch as He has His Head
everywhere, flowers etc., placed on His head
by the devotees as a mark of honour reach
His head wherever they are offered; there is
no place devoid of His head. Having His
mouth everywhere, He can accept an article

=== PAGE 602 ===

* Chapter XIII *
601
The first half of this verse shows that even
the qualified aspect of God, who is the only
object worth knowing, is exceptionally
marvellous and transcendent in character. That
is to say, the assertion made in verse 13 above
that he has hand and feet and all other senses
functioning everywhere, should not lead one
to think that He has hands and feet and other
organs analogous to those of other living beings.
Though altogether devoid of such senses, He
is capable of perceiving the respective objects
of those senses. That is why He has been
declared as having all the senses everywhere
and yet devoid of all senses. вҖәruti also says:Гі
Г¬He (God) grasps and rapidly moves
without hands and feet; he sees without eyes
and hears without ears.Г® (вҖәve. Up. III.19.)*
Hence He is possessed of a transcendent
character; this is what is sought to be conveyed
in the present and the following two verses.
The assertion that Г¬though unattached,
He is the sustainer of allГ® shows that, unlike
worldly parents who maintain and support
their family out of attachment for the latter,
God or the supreme Brahma sustains and
nourishes all without any attachment. That is
why He has been spoken of as the disinterested
friend and benefactor of all beings (V.29). In
other words, God, who is all-pervading and
the only object worth knowing, is entirely
free from the taint of attachment. Yet He
sustains and nourishes all due to His contact
with PrakГҰti. Herein lies His transcendence.
The concluding statement that Г¬though
really beyond all attributes, God is the enjoyer
of qualitiesГ® also establishes His transcendent
character. The intention is to show that, though
enjoying all qualities or modes of PrakГҰti,
God is never tainted by the modes of PrakГҰti
even as other beings are. Though in reality
He is entirely beyond the modes of PrakГҰti,
He is yet the enjoyer of all qualities by
reason of His contact with PrakГҰti. This is
another example of His transcendence.
Speaking of God as having His hands and feet and all other senses capable of
functioning everywhere, the Lord now proceeds to establish the transcendent character of
His being.
вҖҡп¬ӮпЈҝВёГҒГЈВәЛқГҝВӘГҲГЎГҠГҠГ·ГҠвҖҡВў

Though perceiving all sense-objects, It is, really speaking, devoid of all senses.
Nay, though unattached, It is the sustainer of all nonetheless; and though attributeless,
It is the enjoyer of GuЖ’as (the three modes of PrakГҰti).                                   (14)

вҖўВ¬ГҠГҒГЎГҠВ¬ГҠЕ’ГҠпЈҝ ВЎп¬ӮЕёГҠпЈҝ ВӘЛқвҖһUГӢГғГҠ В¬вүҘГҝГ ГҝГёЛҮГҠГҲвҖ” вҖҡ oГҺГЎГҠГҠпЈҝГ ГҝвҲ‘В§ГЎГҠВёвҖ”вҖ“

=== PAGE 603 ===

602
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Just as an iceberg floating in the sea is
pervaded, both inside and outside, by water,
even so all animate and inanimate beings are
pervaded, both inside and outside, by God,
who is the only object worth knowing.
The above statement that God exists
both within and without all animate and
inanimate beings should not lead anyone to
imagine that the moving and unmoving
creation itself is something different from
Him. In order to make this clear, the Lord
further says that the animate and inanimate
creation too constitutes His being. That is to
say, just as a block of floating ice has water
both within and without, and the ice is really
the same as water and nothing but water,
even so the whole animate and inanimate
creation is the same as God and nothing
but God.
One may ask here: If everything is God,
how is it that all people do not know Him?
Anticipating this query, the Lord further says
that just as the common people cannot perceive
the molecules of water existing in the sunГӯs
rays,Гіthey find it hard to perceive those
molecules,Гіeven so the all-pervading supreme
Brahma or God, being far subtler even than
the 
molecules 
of 
water, 
beats 
the
comprehension of the common people and is
thus incomprehensible.
There is no space within or without this
entire universe, where, God is not. Hence He
is too near and far as well; for God, who is
Consciousness and Bliss solidified, ever fills
all places which man regards as distant and
near. Hence He is too near to those reverent
soul who know this truth; while He is far
away to the irreverent.
of Its subtlety; ГғГғЛңГ” It; вҖўГҒп¬ӮГҙГҠпЈҝГҝв—ҠЛңГ” (is) incomprehensible; Е’ГҚвҒ„USГ•в—ҠЛңГ” standing afar; Гё and (finally);
вҖўГҒГЈГғвҲ‘пЈҝВ§ close at hand; Гё as well as; ГғГғЛңГ” (is) That.
It exists without and within all beings, and constitutes the moving and unmoving
creation as well. And by reason of Its subtlety, It is incomprehensible; It is close at
hand, and stands afar too.*                                                                       (15)
вҖўГҒп¬ӮГ·Г„ГғВў Гё Г·ГҚГғпЈҝВ·ГҲ ГҒп¬ӮГ·QВ§ГҒв—Ҡп¬Ӯ Гё ГҒSГ•Гғв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“

* Chapter XIII *
603
The first line of this verse establishes
the unity of God, who is the only object
worth knowing. Just as the all pervading
space, though really one and indivisible,
appears divided as it were because of its
being enclosed by different pots, even so
God, though really undivided, appears divided
as it were in the form of the Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ,
residing in all beings, both animate and
inanimate. This diversity is only apparent; in
reality, God is one, and He fills all space.
The latter half of this verse shows that
Almighty God, who is the only object worth
knowing, is the creator, sustainer and destroyer
of the entire creation, both animate and
inanimate, and performs these functions in
three different forms. In other words, He
alone is BrahmГҘ, ViВ¶Ж’u and вҖәiva.
Г–ГҝГҠпЈҝГҒГғВ·ГҠв—ҠГҒВ¬ ГғГ®ГҝГҠпЈҝГҒГғSГғв—ҠвҖҡвҖ” В¬вҒ„Uв—ҠГҲГ«ГҝГғпЈҝвҖ“

Г–ГҝГҠпЈҝГҒГғВ·ГҠв—ҠЛңГ” of lights; вҖўГҒВ¬ even; ГғГғЛңГ” that (supreme Brahma); Г–ГҝГҠпЈҝГҒГғвҖ” the light; Гғв—ҠвҖҡвҖ” В¬вҒ„Uв—ҠЛңГ” (and)
entirely beyond MГҘyГҘ (Illusion); В©UГ«ГҝГғпЈҝ is said (to be); ГҙГҠГҠЕёв—ҠЛңГ” (It is) Knowledge itself; ГҙГҠпЈҝГҝв—ҠЛңГ”
worth knowing; ГҙГҠГҠЕёВӘГӘГҝв—ҠЛңГ” (and) worth attaining through real wisdom; NUГҒЕ’ in the heart; вҖҡп¬ӮВёSГҝ
of all; ГҒп¬ӮГҒDГ”UГғв—ҠЛңГ” (is) particularly abiding.
That supreme Brahma is said to be the light of all lights and entirely beyond
MГҘyГҘ. That godhead is Knowledge itself, worth knowing, and worth attaining through
real wisdom, and is particularly abiding in the hearts of all.                          (17)
The word Г«TamasaВЁГӯ in this verse stands
for darkness and ignorance. And the godhead
referred to here is self effulgent and Knowledge
itself; Darkness and ignorance cannot exist
near Him. Hence He has been spoken of as
entirely beyondГіaltogether fromГіMГҘyГҘ.
The word Г«JВӨГҘnamГӯ (Knowledge) denotes
the essential nature of God. The use of this
attribute shows that God is consciousness or
Knowledge itself.
Again, the qualification Г«JВӨeyaГӯ used
with reference to It is intended to reveal that
the highest duty of man is to know God, the
only object worth knowing, the discussion of
which has been commenced from verse 12
above. Hence it behoves all to exert themselves
fully to know Its truth, rather than fritter
away their precious lives in pursuit of worldly
enjoyments.
The word Г«JВӨeyamГӯ indicates that it is
essential to know God. Here it may be asked:
The godhead referred to above is the
illuminator of all external lights such as the
sun, moon, lightning and stars, of all psychic
lights viz., the intellect, mind and senses etc.,
and of all celestial lights in the shape of
deities presiding over the different worlds
and objects. Even the power of illumination
inherent in all these is only a fraction of God.
That is why He is the light of all lights, i.e.,
the illuminator of all, the source of all light;
He is illuminated by none. вҖәruti also says:Гі
Г¬Neither the sun nor the moon, nor stars
shine in that realm; nor again does lightning
flash there. How, then, can this fire shed its
lustre ? All these reflect His light; all this
borrows its light from His.Г® (KaВўhopaniВ¶ad

says in XV.12 that Г¬the light in the sun,
which illumines the entire universe, and that
which exists in the moon and in the fire,
know it to be My effulgence.Г®

604
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
How is He to be known? Anticipating this
query, the Lord says that He can be known
by means of true wisdom acquired through
the practice of virtues like Г«absence of prideГӯ
etc., referred to in the foregoing verses as
conducive to Knowledge. God should be
known by acquiring true wisdom through the
practice of the above virtues.
Although God is equally present
everywhere, it is in the heart that He is
particularly manifested. To take a homely
illustration, the light of the sun, though diffused
everywhere in a uniform degree, is particularly
reflected in a mirror etc.; while it emits fire
when reflected in a sun-stone. The reflection
is not so strong in other objects. Even so the
human heart is the seat of GodГӯs manifestation.
In the heart of an illumined soul, of course,
He stands directly manifested. It is in order to
bring this out clearly that God has been
spoken of as particularly seated in the hearts
of all.
Thus briefly describing the true nature of the KВ¶etra. Knowledge and the object worth
knowing, the Lord now points out the reward, of knowing this subject.

the only object worth knowing; Гё and; В©UQВ§в—ҠЛңГ” has been discussed; вҖҡв—ҠГҠвҖҡГғвҖ” in a nutshell; в—ҠjQВ§вҖ”

* Chapter XIII *
605
The word Г«PrakГҰtiГӯ in this verse stands
for GodГӯs own primoridal Nature, that has
existed from time without beginning. It is no
other than what has been spoken of as Г«Mahad-
BrahmaГӯ in Chapter XIV. Again, it is this
very PrakГҰti that has been referred to in
VII.4-5 under the name of Г«AparГҘ PrakГҰtiГӯ or
lower Nature, and as Г«KВ¶etraГӯ in verse 5
above. The only difference is that whereas
the above verses speak of this Primordial
PrakГҰti with her evolutes, viz., mind, intellect,
ego and the five gross elements etc., the
present verse treats of primordial Nature alone.
The use of the particles Г«CaГӯ and Г«EvaГӯ
is intended to convey that PrakГҰti and PuruВ¶a
stand on the same footing so far as
Г«beginninglessnessГӯ is concerned, or that the
two are identical in this respect. And the
object of declaring both as Г«without beginningГӯ
is to show that the JвҲӮvahood of the JвҲӮva, i.e.,
his association with PrakГҰti is not something
adventitious, but has existed from time without
beginning, and even so this PrakГҰti, which is
GodГӯs own energy or potency, should be
recognized as beginningless.
The 
word 
Г«VikГҘrГҘnГӯ 
stands 
for
modifications such as desire and aversion, joy
and sorrow etc., enumerated in verse 6 of this
very chapter; while Г«GuЖ’ГҘnГӯ stands for the
three GuЖ’as, or modes of PrakГҰti, viz., Sattva,
Rajas, Tamas, and all material objects evolved
therefrom. The intention of the Lord in declaring
both these to be Nature-born is to show that
PrakГҰti is not synonymous with the three
GuЖ’asГіSattva, Rajas and Tamas; it is
something without beginning. In the beginning
of creation, the three GuЖ’as are born of PrakГҰti
(SrвҲӮmad BhГҘgavata II.v.22 and XI.xziv.5). It
is in order to make this point clear that in
XIV.5 the Lord mentions the three GuЖ’as by
name and declares them as Nature-born. Again,
in verse 5 of Chapter III, verse 40 of Chapter
XVIII, and verse 21 of the present Chapter the
Lord speaks of the three GuЖ’as as Nature-
born. Verses 27 and 28 of Chapter III as well
refer to the three GuЖ’as as evolutes of PrakГҰti.
Therefore, the three GuЖ’asГіSattva, Rajas and
TamasГіwith their evolutes, and likewise all
modifications should be recognized as born of
PrakГҰti or Nature.

606
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The five subtle elements, ether, air, fire,
water and earth, and the five objects of
senses, viz., sound, touch, colour, taste and
smellГіthese ten constitute what has been
referred to as Г«KГҘryaГӯ in this verse. And the
three internal or psychic organs, viz., the
intellect, the ego and the mind, the five
senses of perception, viz., the sense of hearing,
the tactile sense, the senses of sight and taste
and the olfactory sense, and the five organs
of action, viz., the tongue, hands and feet,
and the organs of generation and defecationГі
these thirteen are covered by the term Г«KaraЖ’aГӯ.
It is from PrakГҰti that these twentythree
categories are evolved; PrakГҰti alone is their
material cause; hence PrakГҰti has been declared
to be the cause of their evolution.
The order of their evolution is as follows.
PrakГҰti gives rise to the Mahat-Tattva (the
principle of Cosmic Intelligence), from the
Mahat-Tattva follows AhaвҲҶkГҘra (the Cosmic
Ego), AhaвҲҶkГҘra brings forth the five TanmГҘtrГҘs,
mind and the ten organs of perception and
action; and the five TanmГҘtrГҘs in their turn
are believed to evolve the five gross elements.
This is borne out by the following verse
(verse 22) of the SГҘвҲҶkhya-KГҘrikГҘ:Гі
В¬ЛқвҲ‘ГҺВ§ГғпЈҝв—ҠВёвҖһUГҠВўSГғГғГҠпЈҝЛҳвҖһВўUвҲ‘В§ГҠвҒ„USГғSв—ҠГҠЕ’ЛңГ” ВӘГЎГҠp В·ГҠпЈҝВ«UвүҘГҠвҲ‘В§вҖ”вҖ“
ГғSв—ҠГҠЕ’ГҒВ¬ В·ГҠпЈҝВ«UвүҘГҠвҲ‘В§ГҠГғЛңГ” В¬@ГЁГҝвҖ” В¬@Г·ГҚГғГҠГҒЕёH
The GвҲӮtГҘ speaks of the five subtle elements
in place of the five TanmГҘtrГҘs, while in place
of the five gross elements it mentions the five
objects of the senses. This is the only difference
between the two versions.
In some editions of the GвҲӮtГҘ, we find the
word Г«KГҘraЖ’aГӯ substituted for Г«KaraЖ’aГӯ in the
present verse. If we accept this variant, the
word Г«KГҘryaГӯ should be understood to stand
for the following sixteen categories, viz., the
five senses of perception. The five organs of
action, mind and the five objects of senses;
for they are all effects of some cause, and are
not themselves the cause of others. And the
word Г«KГҘraЖ’aГӯ should be taken to cover the
intellect, the ego and the five subtle elements.
For the intellect is the cause of the ego; the
ego is the cause of the mind, senses and the
five subtle elements; and the five subtle
elements in their turn are the causes of the
five objects of senses.
Although other scriptures recognize four
aspects of the AntaВЁkaraЖ’a or inner sense,
viz., Buddhi (Intellect), AhaвҲҶkГҘra (Ego), Citta
(the faculty of cogitation or reflection) and
Manas (Mind), the Lord does not recognize
Citta and Manas as two diverse faculties; he
reckons them as only two names of one and
the same faculty. The SГҘвҲҶkhya and Yoga

вҲ‘В§ГҠГҝВёвҲ‘В§вҒ„UГЎГҠвҲ‘В§ГғГҺВёГ п¬ӮпЈҝ

PrakГҰti is said to be responsible for bringing forth the evolutes and the
instruments; while the individual soul is declared to be the cause for the experiences
of joys and sorrows.                                                                                  (20)

* Chapter XIII *

systems also uphold the same view. That is
why, instead of four, the Lord speaks here of

Here it may be urged that the word
Г«PuruВ¶aГӯ stands for the conscious Self or
Г…tmГҘ, and the Self is regarded as stainless

cannot possibly have any experience of

between PuruВ¶a and PrakГҰti has no beginning,
hence the PuruВ¶a has been recognized as
responsible for the experiences of joys and
sorrows. It is in order to make this point clear
that the next verse explicitly says that it is the
PuruВ¶a, seated in PrakГҰti, who senses objects
of the nature of the three GuЖ’as evolved
from PrakГҰti. That is why the PuruВ¶a, weaned
from PrakГҰti, has no semblance of such
experiences.
В¬ГҲLВ§В·вҖ” В¬ЛқвҲ‘ГҺВ§ГҒГғSГ•ГҠпЈҝ ГҒвҖһU Г·ГҲГұпЈҝ В¬ЛқвҲ‘ГҺВ§ГҒГғВЎГҠГЈВӘГҲГЎГҠГҠЕёЛңГ”вҖ“

Only the PuruВ¶a in association with PrakГҰti experiences objects of the nature of
the three GuЖ’as evolved from PrakГҰti. And it is attachment with these GuЖ’as that is
responsible for the birth of this soul in good or evil wombs.                                               (21)
under the category of Г«evilГӯ wombs. Contact
of the JвҲӮva or embodied soul with the three
GuЖ’as, Sattva, Rajas and Tamas, existing,
from time without beginning, and his attachment
for worldly objects, which are the evolutes of
those GuЖ’as, is what is referred to as
Г«GuЖ’asaвҲҶgaВЁГӯ. The latent desires of a man
are determined by his attachment for a particular
GuЖ’a or its evolutes; and it is these latent
desires which decide the nature of his rebirth.
That is why contact with the GuЖ’as has been
declared as responsible for oneГӯs birth in
good or evil wombs.
In this connection it may be noted that
in IV.13 the Lord claimed to have brought
into being the four orders of society dividing
people according to their qualities (GuЖ’as)
and actions. In VIII.6, He declared that
The substantive Г«GuЖ’ГҘnГӯ, qualified by
Г«PrakГҰtijГҘnГӯ, denotes the three qualities or
modes, Sattva, Rajas and Tamas, born of
PrakГҰti, as well as their evolutes, viz., all
mundane objects of the nature of sound,
touch, colour, taste and smell. An individual
soul is said to be seated in PrakГҰti so long as
it is invested with any of the three bodiesГі
gross, subtle and causalГіborn of PrakГҰti.
That is why the Self or Spirit senses the
Nature-born qualities so long as it is associated
with PrakГҰti. It ceases to be the enjoyer on its
being dissociated with PrakГҰti; for, in reality,
the PuruВ¶a is eternally detached in character.
The human and all other higher species,
such as the celestial species and so on, are
Г«goodГӯ wombs; while all subhuman species,
e.g., beasts, birds, trees and creepers etc., fall

608
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
whatever a man thinks of at the last moment
before leaving his body, that very object he
attains in his next life. And in the present
verse He attributes oneГӯs birth in good or evil
wombs to contact with the GuЖ’as. How are
these versions to be reconciled?
Our submission in this connection is that
there is no incongruity whatsoever between
these three statements. On closer examination
all the three statements will be found in one
way or other to attribute oneГӯs birth in good
and evil wombs to contact with the GuЖ’as. (1)
God evolves the four orders of society on the
basis of the GuЖ’as and Karmas of each
individual. In this division contact of the JвҲӮvas
with the GuЖ’as naturally operates as the
determining factor. (2) Man develops special
attachment for one of the three GuЖ’as according
to the nature of his pursuits and the kind of
company he keeps; and the inclinations or
tendencies formed as a result of those actions
are also of a cognate type. Again, his thought
at the last moment of his life is determined by
those proclivities and his birth in good or evil
wombs is, in its turn, decided by his thought at
the last moment. Hence contact with the GuЖ’as
is the ultimate cause even of oneГӯs birth in
good or evil wombs. (3) In the present verse,
of course, contact with the GuЖ’as has been
expressly stated to be the cause of such birth.
Therefore, all the three statements bring out
the same truth.
Having thus described the character of the PuruВ¶a seated in PrakГҰti, the Lord now
delineates the transcendent character of the Self, establishing identity between God and the
individual soul at the same time.

the knowing subject. The diversity which

 (23)
to show that he who knows the PuruВ¶a and

Since evils like lust and anger wholly

Knowledge of the PuruВ¶a consists in

as delineated in this chapter. In other words,
true realization of the fact that all knowing

Consciousness and Bliss once for all, is what
is meant by Г«knowing the PuruВ¶a in realityГӯ.
And grasping the truth that the three GuЖ’as
are evolutes of PrakГҰti, that the whole universe
is nothing but a projection of PrakГҰti, and that
it is perishable, unconscious, momentary and
transient, is what is meant by Г«knowing PrakГҰti
together with the GuЖ’as in realityГӯ.
The use of the indeclinable Г«ApiГӯ with
the words Г«SarvathГҘ VartamГҘnaВЁГӯ is intended

The PuruВ¶a ceases to have anything
to do with PrakГҰti the moment he comes
to know the truth about both; for their
contact has been held to be imaginary, unreal
and brought about by mere ignorance.
The contact of the PuruВ¶a with PrakГҰti and
its GuЖ’as lasts only so long as they are
not fully known and it is only till then that
he continues to be born again and again
in various species (XIII.21). That is why
one is not reborn after knowing their true
nature.
On hearing the glory of the Knowledge of PrakГҰti with the three GuЖ’as and of the
PuruВ¶a, one may be tempted to enquire how this knowledge may be gained. Therefore, in
the next two verses, the Lord mentions various disciplines to be followed by men of different
aptitudes for the realization of Truth.

Some by meditation behold the supreme Spirit in the heart with the help of their
refined and sharp intellect; others realize It through the Yoga of Knowledge, and still
others, through the Yoga of Action i.e., Karmayoga.                                        (24)
formless Brahma viewed as identical with
oneГӯs Self, and it is realization of God as

The words Г«SГҘвҲҶkhyenaГӯ and Г«YogenaГӯ,

all material objects are nothing but illusion,

are born of PrakГҰti, that react on the GuЖ’as, a

Г«DhyГҘnaГӯ denotes constant meditation
on God, practised in a clean and secluded spot
in accordance with the procedure laid down in
verses 11 to 13 of Chapter VI, i.e., firmly
squatting on an appropriate seat, withdrawing
the senses from their objects, controlling the
mind and forgetting everything else than God.
The intellect gets purified through such practice
of meditation; and realizing the supreme Eternal,
who is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss
solidified, through such refined and sharp
intellect within the heart is what is meant by
beholding the Self by meditation in the Self
and through the Self.
Verse 22 above has established identity
between the soul and the Oversoul, and it is
in the light of this identity that the Lord
discusses the different forms of discipline for
the attainment of the ultimate goal in the
form of Realization of the Self. Therefore,
judging from the context, the meditation referred
to in this verse is meditation on the absolute,

=== PAGE 612 ===

* Chapter XIII *
611
the sense of doership in respect of all activities
of the mind, senses and the body, and ever
established in identity with the all-pervading
God, who is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss
solidified, refuses to recognize the separate
existence of anything other than God. And
directly perceiving, through such practice, the
identity of the soul with the Oversoul and thus
realizing the Absolute, is what is meant by
beholding the Self in the Self through the
practice of SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga.
SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga can be easily practised
only by a qualified soul who is endowed
with the four equipments, viz., (1) Viveka or
Discrimination, (2) VairГҘgya or Dispassion,
(3) В®aВў Sampatti or the sixfold riches, and (4)
MumukВ¶utva, the quest for freedom.
1. VIVEKA
Discrimination between the real and the
unreal, as well as between the eternal and the
fleeting is called Viveka. Discriminaiton sifts
the one from the other. Viveka is the same as
realization of Truth. This consummation of
Viveka is attained by constantly sifting the
Self from the non-self, under all circumstances,
and in every object. When Viveka actually
dawns, difference between the real and the
unreal, between the eternal and the transient,
begins to be directly experienced as that between
milk and water. The next equipage is:Гі
2. VAIRГ…GYA
Once the real is sifted from the unreal,
and the eternal from the passing, with the
help of discrimination, attraction for the unreal
and the passing naturally ceases. This is what
they call VairГҘgya. If the hankering for
enjoyments persists in the mind and we make
a show of aversion to and disgust for the
world, that is no dispassion. There is total
negation of RГҘga or attachment in dispassion;
VairГҘgya, really speaking, is another name
for mental detachment. The mind of those
who develop true dispassion entirely cease to
have any thirst or attachment for the various
forms of enjoyment extending up to the abode
of BrahmГҘ (the Creator). Turning their back
upon the unreal and the transient they remain
constantly intent on the real and the eternal.
This is what constitutes dispassion. So long
as this  type of dispassion does not appear
Viveka should be regarded as deficient. When
discrimination is complete, the awakening of
dispassion is inevitable.
3. THE SIXFOLD RICHES
As a result of such discrimination and
dispassion, the SГҘdhaka comes to acquire
sixfold riches of the highest value. So long as
this wealth is not fully acquired, discrimination
and dispassion should be regarded as positively
deficient; for when the SГҘdhaka is fully
endowed with discrimination and dispassion,
it is easy for him to acquire this wealth. This
wealth is known by the name of Г«В®aВўsampattiГӯ
(the sixfold riches) and the following are the
six factors constituting it:Гі
1.
вҖәama. 
Complete 
subjugation,
quiescence and stillness of the mind is called
вҖәama. When discrimination and dispassion
are fully developed, the mind naturally becomes
quiescent and still.
2.
Dama. Complete mastery over the
senses and their finding no relish in the
objects of enjoyment is called Г«DamaГӯ.
3. Uparati. Withdrawal of the mind
from all worldly objects is known as Г«UparatiГӯ.
When the mind and the senses cease to have
any relish for the objects of enjoyment, the
SГҘdhaka will naturally turn his back on them.
This indifference must be cultivated with
respect to every form of enjoyment,Гіnot
simply externally but internally. The mind
should never think of even the rare pleasures
of Brahmaloka or the seventh heaven with
intent to enjoy them. This is what is known
as Г«UparatiГӯ.

=== PAGE 613 ===

612
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
4.
TitikВ¶ГҘ. TitikВ¶ГҘ ordinarily means
maintaining oneГӯs balance of mind in the
face of Dvandvas or pairs of opposites, such
as heat and cold, pleasure and pain, honour
and ignominy; but the Г«TitikВ¶ГҘГӯ which follows
in the wake of Viveka, VairГҘgya, вҖәama,
Dama and Uparati must be something different
from ordinary endurance as indicated above.
The Dvandvas can neither cease in this world
nor can any one wholly avoid them. Putting
up with them anyhow is also quite good; but
the best thing is to rise above the realm of
contraries and to look on them as a dispassionate
observer. Such indeed is TitikВ¶ГҘ in the real
sense of the term. When this state is reached,
heat and cold or honour and ignominy can
no longer touch a man.
5.
вҖәraddhГҘ. Unceasing faith in the
existence of the Self as in something directly
perceived is called вҖәraddhГҘ. вҖәraddhГҘ, first of
all, centers round the вҖәГҘstras or sacred books,
the personality of the religious preceptor and
spiritual practice etc., faith in the Self develops
thereby. But so long as complete faith is not
developed in the spiritual Self, the intellect
cannot get established in the one integral,
stainless, formless and attributeless Brahma
as its goal.
6.
SamГҘdhГҘna. SamГҘdhГҘna means total
absorption of the mind and intellect in God.
In other words, just as Arjuna, while
demonstrating his skill in archery in the
presence of Г…cГҘrya DroЖ’a, beholds only the
neck of the dummy bird placed on a tree,
even so when oneГӯs mind and intellect
constantly perceive God alone as their sole
objective, that is Г«SamГҘdhГҘnaГӯ.
4. MUMUKВ®UTVA
In this way when discrimination,
dispassion and the sixfold virtues are acquired,
the SГҘdhaka naturally seeks absolute freedom
from the bondage of ignorance, and
withdrawing his mind from everything else,
and without looking in any other direction,
runs exclusively after God. This running after
God with the utmost speed on his part, i.e.,
his intense practice itself is an indication of
his keenest desire to realize God. This is
what they call Г«MumukВ¶utvaГӯ  or the quest of
liberation.
*
*
*
The word Г«KarmayogaГӯ in this verse
stands for the course of discipline which has
been discussed, along with its reward in
Chapter II from verse 40 up to the end of the
Chapter. In other words, Karmayoga consists
in performing all oneГӯs prescribed duties,
appropriate to the VarЖ’a (grade in society)
and Г…вҖ№rama (stage in life) to which one
belongs, according to the scriptural injunctions
in a disinterested spirit, renouncing all
attachment and reward, and treating success
and failure alike. And realizing the supreme
eternal Spirit, who is Truth, Consciousness
and Bliss solidified, as oneГӯs own Self through
this discipline is what is meant by beholding
the Self in the Self through Karmayoga.
Here it must be remembered that, even
though maintaining this identity apart from
God during the period of his SГҘdhanГҘ, the
SГҘdhaka who aims at unity in the end realizes
Brahma as his own Self. And the subject
under discussion in this verse is to ascertain
the practices through which God can be
realized as oneГӯs own Self. That is why this
verse speaks of realizing the supreme eternal
Spirit as oneГӯs own Self even through the
practice of Karmayoga.

* Chapter XIII *
613
The Particle Г«TuГӯ in this verse indicates
that the Lord now proceeds to speak of other
SГҘdhakas different from those mentioned in
the preceding verse. In other words, the present
verse points out the way to the redemption of
those who cannot fully grasp the spirit of the
practices mentioned above.
The pronoun Г«AnyeГӯ, qualified by the
adjectival phrase Г«Evam AjГҘnantaВЁГӯ, refers to
those SГҘdhakas who are too dullwitted to grasp
in full the spirit of any one of the practices
mentioned in the preceding verse, viz.,
DhyГҘnayoga, SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga and Karmayoga.
SatyakГҘma, son of JabГҘlГҘ, approached
the great sage HГҘridrumata, who claimed his
descent from Gautama, in quest of the
knowledge of Brahma. Having had a talk
with him, the teacher selected from his herd
four hundred weak and extremely emaciated
cows and asked SatyakГҘma to follow them.
Driving them towards the forest, as enjoined
by his preceptor, with great reverence,
enthusiasm and joy, SatyakГҘma said, Г¬I shall
return when their number swells to a thousand.Г®
Taking them to a safe retreat abounding in
herbage and water, he came back only when
their number had swollen to a thousand. The
result was that he attained God-realization
while he was yet on his way back to his
preceptorГӯs hermitage (ChГҘndogya, IV.iv.9).
Receiving instructions from such men of
wisdom, who have realized the Truth, and
carrying them out with extreme reverence
and love is what is meant by Г«worshipping
even as they have heard from others.Гӯ
The compound adjective Г«вҖәrutiparГҘyaЖ’ГҘВЁГӯ
refers to those who take with reverence and
love to the diligent practice of what they have
heard. The use of the indeclinable Г«ApiГӯ is
intended to show that when even such men of
small understanding are doubtless able to
conquer death by worshipping as they have
heard from others, there can be no doubt about
the redemption of SГҘdhakas who follow one
of the three disciplines stated above.
The word Г«MГҰtyumГӯ in this verse stands
for mundane existence in the shape of repeated
births and death. The use of the verb Г«TarantiГӯ,
with Г«AtiГӯ prefixed to it, is intended to show
that those who worship as indicated above
attain lasting union with the supreme eternal
Spirit, who is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss
solidified, and are never born again. In short,
the words Г«MГҰtyum AtitarantiГӯ in this verse
convey the same idea as has been brought
out by the words Г«he is not born againГӯ in
verse 23 and Г«beholding the Self in the Self
in verse 24 above.

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The adjectives Г«YГҘvatГӯ and Г«KiВӨcitГӯ signify
totality. Beings possessing locomotion, e.g.,
the celestials, human beings, beasts and birds
etc., fall under the category of Г«JaвҲҶgamaГӯ
(animate); 
while 
creatures 
devoid 
of
locomotion, such as trees, creepers, rocks
etc., are known as Г«SthГҘvaraГӯ (inanimate).
Therefore, the substantive Г«SattvamГӯ qualified
by the three adjectives Г«YГҘvatГ®, Г«KiВӨcitГӯ and
Г«SthГҘvarajaвҲҶgamamГӯ covers the entire creation
of animate and inanimate beings.
The term Г«KВ¶etraГӯ denotes the aggregate
of twentyfour categories enumerated in verse
5 of this chapter and which have been, referred
to under the name of Г«AparГҘ PrakГҰtiГӯ or the
lower Nature in VII.4 and 5. And the conscious
principle which is the knower of this KВ¶etra,
and which has been spoken of as Г«ParГҘГӯ
PrakГҰtiГӯ (the higher Nature) in verse VII.5, is
called Г«KВ¶etrajВӨa.Гӯ Contact of this Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ,
i.e., of the PuruВ¶a seated in PrakГҰti, with the
gross bodies evolved from Nature has been
referred to in this verse as the union of
KВ¶etra and the KВ¶etrajВӨa; and the emanation
of beings in different shapes and forms through
various types of wombs or species is what
has been spoken of in this verse as their birth
from such union.

even though seeing, they do not see at all.

And he who sees that all actions are performed in everyway by nature (PrakГҰti)
and the Self as the non-doer, he alone truly sees.                                             (29)
Thus the same thing has been stated in all the
three places, there is hardly any difference in
the three statements. They are all intended to
show the non-doership of the Self.
The Self is eternal, pure, awakened,
free and devoid of all modifications; it has no
connection whatsoever with PrakГҰti. That is
why it is neither the doer of any action nor
the experiencer of the fruit of actions. The
direct perception of this truth is what is
meant by seeing the Self as the non-doer.
And the assertion that such realization is the
only true realization is intended to glorify this
vision. The intention is to show that the
vision of those who view the Self as the
doer of all actions and the experiencer of
their fruit, due to its contact with the mind,
intellect and body, is mistaken and therefore
incorrect.
Verse 27 and 28 of Chapter III and
verse 19 of Chapter XIV speak of all actions
being performed by the GuЖ’as or modes of
PrakГҰti, and verses 8 and 9 of Chapter V
speak of the senses acting on sense-objects;
whereas the present verse indirectly exhorts
us to see all actions being done by PrakГҰti.
These three statements, though apparently
differing from one another, point to the same
conclusion, as we shall see below.
The three GuЖ’asГіSattva, Rajas and
TamasГіare all evolutes of PrakГҰti; and the
senses, mind and intellect etc., as well as the
objects of sensesГіall these are extensions of
the GuЖ’as alone. Therefore, speaking of the
senses as acting on sense-objects, of the GuЖ’as
as acting on the GuЖ’as, and of all actions
being performed by the GuЖ’as is the same as
declaring all actions being done by PrakГҰti.
Thus glorifying the realization of the Self as the non-doer, the Lord now points out the
reward of perceiving Its unity.

The compound word Г«BhВөutapГҰthagbhГҘ-
vamГӯ in this verse stands for the manifold
variety of animate and inanimate beings who
are said to emanate from the union of Г«KВ¶etraГӯ
and the Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ (XIII.26), and in whom
the SГҘdhaka has been indirectly advised to
see God as equally present (Ibid.,27). And
just as a man who has just woken from a
dream perceives the motley show of beings
seen in his dreaming state in his own self and
also recognizes that they were all projections
of his being, that, in fact there was nothing in
that dream-world other than himself, that he
alone saw himself in divergent forms, even
so the man of right vision sees all creatures
as existing in God, and everything as emanating
from Him.
The indeclinables Г«YadГҘГӯ and Г«TadГҘГӯ
signify immediate sequence of time. The use
of these two words is intended to show that
the realization of Brahma or attainment of
oneness with God follows immediately after
the perception of the above truth, that the
process involves no amount of delay. It is
this attainment of oneness with Brahma, who
is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss solidified,
that is otherwise known as attainment of
the supreme state, attainment of liberation,
and realization of supreme peace and lasting
Bliss.

вҖўЕёГҠГҒЕ’Г п¬ӮГҠГғЛңГ” being without beginning; ГҒЕёВӘГҲВёГЎГҠГ п¬ӮГҠГғЛңГ” (and) being attributeless; В¬вҒ„Uв—ҠГҠГ в—ҠГҠ the
supreme Spirit; вҖўГҝв—ҠЛңГ” this; вҖўвҲҡГҝГҝвҖ” indestructible; вүҘГҠвҒ„UГӢвҒ„USГ•вҖ” вҖўГҒВ¬ though dwelling in the body;
вҲ‘В§ГҠГ’ГЈГғпЈҝГҝ O son of Kunti, Arjuna; Её вҲ‘В§вҒ„UГҠпЈҝГҒГғ does nothing (in fact); Её nor; ГҒвҲӮUГҘГҝГғпЈҝ gets contaminated.
Arjuna, being without beginning and without attributes, this indestructible Supreme
Spirit, though dwelling in the body, in fact does nothing, nor gets contaminated. (31)

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
points to that which has been discussed
immediately before. The use of this adjective
along with the word Г«ParamГҘtmГҘГӯ, therefore,
shows that the supreme Spirit referred to in
this verse is the same as has  been spoken of
as Г«ParameвҖ№varaГӯ (the supreme Lord) in verse
27, as Г«вҲ«вҖ№varaГӯ (Lord) in verse 28, as the
Г«AtmГҘГӯ (Self) in verse 29, and as Г«BrahmaГӯ
(the Supreme) in verse 30 above. In other
words the use of Г«AyamГӯ is intended to show
that all these terms are identical in meaning.
It may be asked here: If all these terms
refer to the same entity, why was it necessary
to use different words for the same thing?
Our submission in this connection is that in
verse 3 above the Lord undertook to reveal
to Arjuna the character and glory of the
Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ (the knowing Self). Therefore,
the Lord has deliberately used different words
expressive of the divine principle in order to
reveal the essential character of the KВ¶etrajВӨa,
which is identical with the supreme eternal
Spirit.
In reality, the Self has no connection
whatsoever with the GuЖ’as of PrakГҰti and
their evolutes in the form of the intellect,
mind, senses and the body; it exists entirely
beyond the GuЖ’as. Just as ether, though
present in the clouds, does not become their
originator, nor is it affected by them, even so
the Self is never the doer of actions nor does
it get contaminated by the bodies. The Lord
Himself elucidates this point in the next two
verses with the help of illustrations.
In support of his statement that the Self, though residing in the body, does not get
contaminated by it, the Lord further says:
ГҝГ•ГҠ вҖҡп¬ӮВёВӘГғВў вҖҡГҠГ’ЛҮГӘГҝГҠЕ’ГҠвҲ‘В§ГҠвүҘГҠВў ЕёГҠпЈҝВ¬ГҒвҲӮUГҘГҝГғпЈҝГ”вҖ“

ГҝГ•ГҠ as; вҖҡп¬ӮВёВӘГғв—ҠЛңГ” the all-pervading; вҖҡГҠГ’ЛҮГӘГҝГҠГғЛңГ” by reason of its subtlety; вҖўГҠвҲ‘В§ГҠвүҘГҠв—ҠЛңГ” ether; Её
В©UВ¬ГҒвҲӮUГҘГҝГғпЈҝ is not tainted; вҖҡп¬ӮВёГІГҠ every where; вҖўп¬ӮГҒSГ•ГғвҖ” seated; Е’пЈҝвҖһпЈҝU in the body; ГғГ•ГҠ so; вҖўГҠГ в—ҠГҠ the
Self; Её В©UВ¬ГҒвҲӮUГҘГҝГғпЈҝ is not affected (by the attributes of the body owing to its attributeless
character).
As the all-pervading ether is not tainted by reason of its subtlety, though
permeating the body, the Self is not affected by the attributes of the body due to Its
subtle character.                                                                  (32)
even so though permeating the entire body, and
because it is extremely subtle and exists entirely
beyond the GuЖ’as, the Self is not affected in
the least by the good and evil qualities of the
intellect, mind, senses and the body.
By the analogy of ether, the verse proves
the stainless character of the Self. The idea is
that just as, though equally present in the air,
fire, water and earth, ether is in no way
contaminated by their good and evil qualities,
The Lord now explains how the Self is not the doer, though dwelling in the body.
ГҝГ•ГҠ В¬ЛқвҲ‘В§ГҠвүҘГҠГҝГ ГҝпЈҝвҲ‘В§вҖ” вҲ‘ГҺВ§Г FВў вҲӮUГҠпЈҝвҲ‘В§ГҒв—Ҡв—ҠВў вҒ„UГҒп¬ӮвҖ”Г”вҖ“

O descendant of Bharata, Arjuna.

* Chapter XIII *
619
The illustration of the sun has been used
in this verse to demonstrate the non-doership
of the Self, and the qualification Г«EkaВЁГӯ to bring
out its non-dual character. That is to say, just
as the one sun lights up the entire universe, so
does the one Self infuse life and activity into
the whole world of matter referred to in verses
5 and 6 above under the name of Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and
its evolutes. And through different mental
apparatuses the Self appears to manifest itself
differently in different bodies. Nevertheless, like
the sun, the Self neither performs the actions
pertaining to the bodies nor does it cause them
to be performed, nor is it tainted by evils like
duality or partiality etc. That immortal Self ever
remains pure, conscious, actionless, immutable,
unchanged, impartial and stainless under every
circumstance.
Arjuna, as the one sun illumines this entire universe, so the one Г…tmГҘ (Spirit)
illumines the whole KВ¶etra (Field).                                                         (33)
Having thrown light on the six points raised in verse 3 above, the Lord now winds up
the chapter, holding out God-realization as the reward of fully grasping the whole teaching
contained therein.
ЛҮГҠпЈҝГІГҠЛҮГҠпЈҝГІГҠГҙГҠГҝГҠпЈҝвҒ„пЈҝUп¬Ӯв—ҠГЈГғвҒ„ВўU

Those who thus perceive with the eye of wisdom the difference between the
KВ¶etra (field) and KВ¶etrajВӨa (knower of the field), and the phenomenon of liberation
from PrakГҰti with her evolutes, reach the Supreme, eternal Spirit.

unattached, pure, of the same stuff as

apparent identity of the two is rooted in

This is what is meant by perceiving with the

Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and the Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ.

Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ (the Soul) is its knower, conscious,

620
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
realizing in the aforesaid manner the difference
between the KВ¶etra and the KВ¶etrajВӨa, when
the soul gets disunited from Г«PrakГҰtiГӯ and is
established in identity with its real Self, the
supreme Spirit, one is said to have realized
liberation from PrakГҰti with her evolutes.
The dreaming man, on being reminded
anyhow of his waking state, comes to know
that he was dreaming, therefore, he wakes up
the moment it strikes him that waking in his
real body is the only means of escape from
the woes of the dream state. Even so the
JВӨГҘnayogвҲӮ or the SГҘdhaka following the path
of Knowledge, while perceiving the difference
between the KВ¶etra and the KВ¶etrajВӨa, comes
to realize at the same time that his apparent
connection with the KВ¶etra was due to his
having erroneously taken it to be real, therefore,
riddance from such connection consists in
getting established in identity with oneГӯs real
or divine Self, who is Truth, Consciousness
and Bliss solidified. Such a man is said to
have come to know his liberation from PrakГҰti
with her evolutes.
The moment this truth is realized the
entire realm of objective reality disappears
along with ignorance, and God-realization
takes place immediately.

* Chapter XIV *
621
Title of the
Chapter
Summary of
the Chapter
Лҷ
вҖәrвҲӮmadbhagavad-GвҲӮtГҘ
Chapter XIV
The present chapter discusses matters relating to the three GuЖ’as,ГіSattva, Rajas and
TamasГіviz., their essential character, evolutes, cause and power, how and under what
circumstances they throw the embodied soul into bondage, as well as the
nature of that bondage, and, finally, the marks of the soul who has risen
above the GuЖ’as and realized God. In order to inculcate the advisability, in
the first instance, viz., during the period of oneГӯs SГҘdhanГҘ or spiritual discipline, of shaking
off Rajas and Tamas and adopting Sattva, and finally of giving up all connection with the
GuЖ’as, the three GuЖ’as have been separately dealt with. Hence this chapter has been given
the title of Г«The Yoga of Division of the three GuЖ’as.Г®
In verses 1 and 2 of this chapter the Lord glorifies Wisdom, the discussion of which
follows later, and introduces the subject. Detailing in the third and fourth the process of
evolution of all beings from the union of PrakГҰti and PuruВ¶a, in the fifth the
Lord declares the three GuЖ’as,ГіSattva, Rajas and Tamas,Гіas the cause of
bondage of the embodied soul. Verses 6 to 8 discuss in their order of
sequence the nature of the three GuЖ’as and the way in which they bind the JвҲӮvГҘtmГҘ.
Mentioning in the ninth which GuЖ’a drives the soul to what, and showing in the tenth how
one GuЖ’a prevails over the other two, the Lord discusses in verses 11 to 13 the marks
indicating the preponderance of Sattva, Rajas and Tamas respectively. Discussing in the
fourteenth and the fifteenth the fate of the man who dies during the preponderance of each
of the three GuЖ’as, the Lord details in the sixteenth the corresponding fruits of actions of the
SГҘttvika, RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika Types. Declaring in the seventeenth that the quality of
Sattva gives rise to knowledge, the quality of Rajas begets greed and the quality of Tamas
breeds error, stupor and ignorance, the Lord details in the eighteenth the corresponding fate
of the JвҲӮvГҘtmГҘ established in each of the three GuЖ’as. Verses 19 to 20 proclaim the reward
of viewing actions as being performed by the GuЖ’as, and the Г…tmГҘ as standing beyond the
GuЖ’as and a non-doer, as well as of transcending the three GuЖ’as. In verse 21, Arjuna
inquires of the Lord about the marks and conduct of one who has risen above the GuЖ’as,
and about the means of rising above them. In response to this, the Lord discusses in verses
22 to 25 the marks and conduct of the man who has risen above the three GuЖ’as, and points
out in the twentysixth the means as well as the reward of rising above them. The Lord then
concludes the chapter by declaring Himself in the last or twentyseventh verse as the
foundation of Brahma, immortality, and so on inasmuch as they are all identical with Him.
In the course of Chapter XIII, the Lord first of all defined Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ and
declared their knowledge alone as true Knowledge, and accordingly discussed the denotation,
character, and evolutes of the Г«KВ¶etraГӯ and the order of evolution of its
constituents and the essential character and glory of the Г«KВ¶etrajВӨaГӯ Introducing
the topic of the GuЖ’as under the name of PrakГҰti and PuruВ¶a in verse 19, the
Link of the
Discourse

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Lord declared the three evolutes of PrakГҰti, and added in the twenty-first that attachment to
the three GuЖ’as alone was responsible for oneГӯs repeated birth in good and evil wombs. In
this connection one is naturally tempted to know the diverse characteristics of the three
GuЖ’as, how they bind the JвҲӮvГҘtmГҘ to the body, the type of birth one attains through
attachment to a particular GuЖ’a, the means of ridding oneself of the three GuЖ’as, and the
marks and conduct of the soul who has been rid of them. Chapter XIV has, therefore, been
introduced to elucidate all these points. The same JВӨГҘna or Wisdom that has been dealt with
in Chapter XIII has to be clearly brought out and elaborately discussed in this chapter;
therefore, glorifying that Wisdom in two verses, the Lord first of all undertakes to resume
its discussion.
ГјГҠГӢГ·ВӘп¬ӮГҠЕёГҲUп¬ӮГҠГё

В¬вҒ„в—ҠЛңГ” supreme; Г·ГҚГҝвҖ” again; В¬Лқп¬ӮЛҮГҝГҠГҒв—Ҡ I shall expound; ГҙГҠГҠЕёГҠЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” of wisdoms; ГҙГҠГҠЕёв—ҠЛңГ” wisdom;

вҖәrвҲӮ BhagavГҘn said: I shall expound once more the supreme wisdom, the best of
all kinds of wisdom, acquiring which all sages have attained highest perfection, being
liberated from this mundane existence.                                                                (1)
order that it may be fully understood. The
method of exposition, of course, is different
this time.
The word Г«MunayaВЁГӯ stands for those
wise men who have attained the supreme
state through the practice of JВӨГҘnayoga or the
discipline of Knowledge; and the highest
perfection reached by these wise men is the
same which is spoken of as Г«the attainment
of supreme BrahmaГӯ, and which has been
variously described as Г«supreme PeaceГӯ,
Г«everlasting happinessГӯ and the Г«state from
which there is no returnГӯ etc.
The word Г«ItaВЁГӯ (hence) signifies mortal
existence. The use of this word shows that
the sages referred to in this verse have ceased
for all time to maintain any connection with
this woeful world consisting of death.
The word Г«JВӨГҘnГҘnГҘmГӯ in this verse covers
the entire body of teachings, varied in  character,
contained in the Vedas, SmГҰti texts, PurГҘЖ’as
and other sacred literature, and intended to
throw light on different subjects. Of these,
the Lord undertakes in this verse to discuss
that true wisdom, which, while differentiating
between PrakГҰti and PuruВ¶a, reveals the true
character of PuruВ¶a. This wisdom brings direct
realization of God and, freeing the embodied
soul from the bondage of PrakГҰti, liberates it
for ever; hence it has been declared as the
best and highest of all wisdom.
The use of the indeclinable Г«BhВөuyaВЁГӯ is
intended to show that this wisdom has been
discussed even before; but being most profound
and abstruse, it is difficult to understand.
Hence it is taken up for discussion again in
ГҹЕ’Вў ГҙГҠГҠЕёв—ҠГҲВ¬ГҠГҒГјГҠГ Гҝ в—Ҡв—Ҡ вҖҡГҠЕ“ГӘГҝВёв—ҠГҠВӘГғГҠвҖ”Г”вҖ“
вҖҡВӘпЈҝВёЛҳГҒВ¬ ЕёГҠпЈҝВ¬ВЎГҠГҝГЈГғпЈҝ В¬ЛқвҲӮUГҝпЈҝ Её вҲҡГҝГ•ГҒГЈГғ ГёH 2H

* Chapter XIV *
623
The demonstrative adjective Г«IdamГӯ has
been used before Г«JВӨГҘnamГӯ in order to make
it clear that whatever has been stated in this
verse is in praise of that very wisdom which
has already been discussed in Chapter XIII,
and is going to be discussed in the present
Chapter as well. And those who, having
realized in the light of the wisdom referred to
in this context the true nature of PrakГҰti and
PuruВ¶a, have reached entirely beyond PrakГҰti
and her GuЖ’as, and remain established in
identity with the absolute, formless supreme
Spirit, who is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss
solidified, are said to have taken recourse to
or practised this wisdom.
The words Г«Mama sГҘdharmyamГҘgatГҘВЁГӯ
in the present verse convey the same idea as
has been expressed by Г«ParГҘm Siddhim GatГҘВЁГӯ
(attained highest perfection) in the preceding

aspect of God as oneГӯs own Self is what is
meant by attaining His Being.
The latter half of this verse is intended
to convey that those who, taking recourse to
and practising the wisdom taught in this chapter,
have realized the supreme eternal Spirit as
their very Self, cease to have any connection
with the creation or dissolution of the universe.
For the principal factor determining oneГӯs birth
in good or evil wombs in contact with the
GuЖ’as, and liberated souls stand entirely
beyond the GuЖ’as; hence there is no return
for them. And when there is no birth, the
question of death does not arise at all.

My primordial Nature, known as the great Brahma, is the womb of all creatures;

The word Г«GarbhamГӯ in this verse stands

The demonstrative adverb Г«TataВЁГӯ (thence)
refers to the union of Matter and Spirit
brought about in this way by God; while
Г«SarvabhВөutГҘnГҘmГӯ stands for the totality of living
beings appearing in various forms, e.g.,

Г«SambhavaВЁГӯ signifies the appearance of these
creatures in various subtle or astral forms
through this conjunction of Matter and Spirit.
The first offspring of this union, appearing at
the dawn of creation, is HiraЖ’yagarbha (the
astral form of BrahmГҘ, the Creator) and the
subtle bodies of other creatures follow next.
By the latter half of this verse the Lord
conveys that the subtle and gross froms of these
beings are all constituted of Matter, while the
conscious spirit dwelling in them is a particle
of God. It is the union of PrakГҰti and God
that brings forth all embodied beings; hence
PrakГҰti is their Mother, and God, their Father.
The word Г«MВөurtayaВЁГӯ stands for the

belonging to different species, such as celestials,
human beings, RГҘkВ¶asas or demons, beasts,
birds and so on; and Г«SambhavantiГӯ denotes
their incarnation in the aforesaid species.

Of all embodied beings that appear in all the species of various kinds, Arjuna,

In verse 21 of Chapter XIII it was stated that attachment to the GuЖ’as is responsible
for the birth of the embodied soul in good or evil wombs. Following this line of thought, the
Lord spoke in verses 3 and 4 above of embodied souls being born in species of various
kinds; but the verses contain no reference to the GuЖ’as. Therefore, while introducing this
topic in order to make it clear what these GuЖ’as are, what is meant by getting attached to
them, attachment to what particular GuЖ’a causes good birth or otherwise and so on, the
Lord in course of the next four verses first of all declares that the GuЖ’as are evolved from
PrakГҰti, then mentions them separately by name and lastly discusses their nature as well as
the way in which they bind the JвҲӮva.

reference to the same shows that in reality

therewith.
The use of the words Г«SattvamГӯ, Г«RajasГӯ

The quality of Sattva is absolutely pure
in character and untainted by any form of
evil; hence it is illuminating and free from

The quality of Rajas manifests itself in

of Rajas develops through desire and
attachment, while cupidity and attachment in
their turn are developed through RajoguЖ’a.
They are interdependent like the seed and the
tree. Of these, Rajas is analogous to the seed;
while desire, attachment etc., correspond to
the tree. The seed is a product of the tree,
and yet it is the cause of the tree as well. It

Fruitless activity of the mind and senses

The quality of Tamas (inertia) stupefies
