694
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The word Г«DevaГӯ stands here for BrahmГҘ
(the Creator), вҖәiva (the god of destruction),
the sun-god, the moon-god, DurgГҘ (вҖәivaГӯs
Consort), Agni (the god of fire), VaruЖ’a (the
god presiding over waters), Yama (the god
of judgment), Indra (the lord of paradise) and
all other gods mentioned in the вҖәГҘstrasГіwhose
worship has been enjoined in the вҖәГҘstras. The
word Г«DvijaГӯ, though denoting in its wider
application all the three higher castes, viz.,
the BrГҘhmaЖ’a, (the priestly class), the KВ¶atriya
(the warrior class) and the VaiвҖ№ya (the trading
class), stands exclusively for the BrГҘhmaЖ’a;
for the latter alone is an object of worship for
all. The word Г«GuruГӯ in this verse covers oneГӯs
parents, preceptor, aged folk and those who
are superior in VarЖ’a (grade in society),
Г…вҖ№rama (stage in life) and age, etc. And the
word Г«PrГҘjВӨaГӯ denotes saintly and wise men
who possess adequate knowledge of God.
Treating all these with due respect and attention,
bowing to them, prostrating oneself at their
feet, washing their feet, offering them
sandalpaste, flowers, incense, light, food etc.,
rendering them appropriate services etc., and
making reasonable attempts to gratify themГі
all these are included in their worship.
The word Г«вҖәaucaГӯ has been used here in
the restricted sense of mere external purity.
For purity of speech has been mentioned
separately in verse 15 and purity of mind in
verse 16. Г«вҖәaucaГӯ consists in cleaning oneГӯs
body with water, earth etc., and keeping all
oneГӯs bodily activities pure (XVI.3).
Г«Г…rjavaГӯ means straightness. Having been
mentioned as a part of bodily penance, it
signifies absence of crookedness in the form
of stiffness and twisting of the body.
Г«BrahmacaryaГӯ consists in abstaining from
sexual commerce in all its bodily forms and
carefully preserving oneГӯs seminal fluid.
Г«AhiвүҲsГҘГӯ means taking a vow never to
inflict with oneГӯs body the least suffering in
any form on any creature whatsoever.
The above virtues and practices are mainly
connected with the body, or in other words,
the body plays the principal part in them; and
they wash away all the impurities of the
body and the senses and purify them. Hence
they are known as bodily penance.
Having thus described the characteristics of the threefold YajВӨa, the Lord now takes up
the subject of Tapas, and in order to point out the characteristics of SГҘttvika penance in the
following four verses, first discusses the nature of bodily penance.

* Chapter XVII *
695
Words which cause no annoyance to
others and are absolutely untainted by
calumny and slander etc., are called
Г«AnudvegakaraГӯ. Similarly, truthful words are
those which state the bare truth in order to
convey to others a correct idea of what one
has actually seen, heard or experienced. Loving,
sweet, artless and gentle words, which are
pleasing to hear and are altogether free from
acerbity, pungency, coldness, sarcasm,
insolence and other faults, are known as
Г«PriyaГӯ. And words which are ultimately
beneficial to all, which are altogether free
from violence, malice, jealousy and animosity,
and are full of love, compassion and good
wishes are called Г«HitaГӯ.
The uttering of only, such words as
combine in themselves all the four virtues,
viz., unoffensiveness, veracity, agreeability
and wholesomeness, and are free from all
impurities of speech mentioned in the вҖәГҘstras,
can be recognized as the austerity of speech;
words which have the least tinge of these
impurities and lack in any of the aforesaid
virtues do not fall under the category of
complete austerity of speech. This is what is
sought to be conveyed by the use of the
particle Г«CaГӯ after Г«PriyahitamГӯ.
Reciting the Vedas and their auxiliaries
viz., works on grammar, phonetics etc., the SmГҰti
texts, the PurГҘЖ’as and Stotras or panegyrics to
God etc., according to oneГӯs qualifications,
uttering the praises, glory and names of God
and offering prayers etc., to GodГіall these
are included in Г«SvГҘdhyГҘyГҘbhyasanamГӯ.
All the above virtues and practices are
connected with speech; they eradicate all the
impurities of speech and purify it along with
the mind; hence they have been spoken of as
the austerity of speech.
вҖўЕёГҲmпЈҝВӘвҲ‘В§вҒ„Uв—ҠЛңГ” causing no annoyance; п¬ӮГҠГ„Гҝв—ҠЛңГ” speech; вҖҡГ Гҝв—ҠЛңГ” truthful; ГҒВ¬ЛқГҝГҒвҖһUГғв—ҠЛңГ” agreeable and

taptamГӯ.
The use of the adjectives Г«TatГӯ and
Г«TrividhamГӯ with Г«TapaВЁГӯ is intended to convey
that the threefold penance relating to the
body, speech and mind, mentioned in the
preceding three verses alone, can be SГҘttvika
in character. Other forms of penance, such as
those referred to in verse 5 of this very
chapter as Г«not sanctioned by the scripturesГӯ
(AвҖ№ГҘstravihitam) and Г«dreadfulГӯ (Ghoram), are
never SГҘttvika. It further shows that although
the forms of penance enumerated in verses
Г«AphalГҘkГҘвҲҶkВ¶вҲӮГӯ is he who never and on
no account entertains the least craving for any
return whatsoever in the shape of enjoyments
of this world or the next or the cessation of
suffering; and he whose mind, intellect and
senses, being disciplined and untainted, can
never be excited by enjoyment of any kind
whatsoever, and who is entirely free from
attachment, 
is 
called 
Г«YuktaГӯ. 
Thus
demonstrating by the use of the above two
epithets the need of a disinterested spirit, the
Lord thereby shows that the threefold penance
described in the above three verses assumes
a wholly SГҘttvika character only when it is
performed by such disinterested persons.

Austerity, whether of a profane or sacred

It should be noted in this connection

to the RГҘjasika level; it is much more so if it
is accompanied with ostentation.
There is no surety about our gaining the
object sought through such penance; hence
such austerity has been declared as Г«AdhruvaГӯ,
and it has been spoken of as Г«CalaГӯ because
whatever return we get for such penance
does not last for ever, it is bound to perish.
The use of the adjective Г«YatГӯ with

The reputation that one gains in the

The penance which is performed for the sake of renown, honour or adoration as

The word Г«TГҘmasamГӯ in this verse refers
to the type of penance mentioned in verses 5

oneГӯs head downwards, sitting on iron spikes

by way of ostentation or through ignorance.
This is what is sought to be conveyed by the
use of the adjective Г«YatГӯ with Г«TapaВЁГӯ.
Obstinately clinging to any practice
whatsoever as an act of penance without
knowing the true characteristics of austerity

PвҲӮЕ’ayГҘГӯ. Therefore, an act of penance which

Г«ParasyotsГҘdanГҘrtham TapaВЁГӯ.

the good of others to the best of his ability.
Failing to do so he falls from the human state

Therefore, that gift alone is wholly SГҘttvika
in character, which is bestowed with a pure

Only that place and time are fit and
suitable for gratifying others through the gift
of a particular object, at which that object is
particularly needed. For instance, that place
and time alone are fit and suitable for the gift

prevalence of famine or draughtГіno matter

Besides these, ordinarily speaking, KurukВ¶etra,

MathurГҘ,

He who is in need of a particular object
at 
a 
particular 
place 
and 
time 
is
a Г«PГҘtraГӯ or fit person to receive a gift of that
object at that very place and time. For instance,
all living beings who are hungry, thirsty, naked,
poor, sick, afflicted, forlorn and terror-stricken
are worthy of receiving gifts of food, water,
cloth, wherewithal to make both ends meet,
medicine, consolation, shelter and assurance
of safety respectively. The fitness of afflicted
creatures is not qualified by considerations of
caste, nationality or time. Their affliction alone
is a test of their fitness. Besides these, learned
BrГҘhmaЖ’as of excellent conduct, ideal
BrahmacГҘrвҲӮs or religious students, VГҘnaprasthas
or hermits living in a forest and SaвүҲnyГҘsвҲӮs or

those who have taken a vow of service are fit
to receive, according to their respective
qualifications, gifts of money and other articles
which are within the means of the donor and
have been recommended for them by the
вҖәГҘstras.
It is certainly obligatory to render good
offices to, and try to gratify to oneГӯs level
best, those to whom one owes a debt of
gratitude. Nay, men of a noble disposition
cannot help obliging their benefactor. They
know that an attempt to return a true obligation
is to undervalue it; for a real obligation cannot
be repaid at all. Hence they render good
offices to him only for their own satisfaction;
and whatever they do, appears inadequate to
them. They remain overwhelmed with gratitude.
In вҖәrвҲӮ RГҘmacaritamГҘnasa of GosvГҘmвҲӮ
TulasвҲӮdГҘsa, BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ RГҘma says to His
devotee, HanumГҘn:Гі
Г¬O monkey-god, there is none among
celestials, human beings and sages, who has
rendered such valuable services to Me as
you have done. How should I requite your
services? My mind is unable even to stand
your looks.Г®
In вҖәrвҲӮmad BhГҘgavata, BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ
KГҰВ¶Ж’a proclaims His indebtedness to the
cowherd damsels of Vraja. Under such
circumstances, offering anything to a benefactor
can never be unjustifiable or a RГҘjasika act;
of course, it does not come within the purview
of Г«DГҘnaГӯ or charity. It is rather a natural
gesture expressive of oneГӯs gratitude. Those
who regard it as an act of charity really offer
insult to the benefactor; while those who are
unwilling to oblige a benefactor belong to
the category of ungrateful creatures. Hence
one must render good offices to oneГӯs
benefactor.
By insisting that a gift should be bestowed

The Lord now defines a RГҘjasika gift.
ГҝГ»ГҠГҲ В¬ЛқГ ГҝГҲВ¬вҲ‘В§ГҠвҒ„UГҠГ•Z В»В§вҲӮUв—ҠГҲГҒgвүҘГҝ п¬ӮГҠ В¬ГҲЕёвҖ”вҖ“

expecting; п¬ӮГҠ or; В¬ГҲЕёвҖ” then; Е’ГӢГҝГғпЈҝ is given; Гё and; В¬ГҒвҒ„UГҒГ„вҖ№cвүҲв—ҠЛңГ” in a grudging spirit; ГғГғЛңГ” that; Е’ГҠЕёв—ҠЛңГ”
gift; вҒ„UГҠВЎвҖҡв—ҠЛңГ” RГҘjasika (in character); Sв—ҠГҺГғв—ҠЛңГ” has been proclaimed.
A gift which is bestowed in a grudging spirit and with the object of getting a
service in return or in the hope of obtaining a reward, is called RГҘjasika.        (21)
Similarly, a gift which is bestowed on an
individual or institution with the object of gaining
honour, fame, prestige and enjoyments, either
of this world or the next, or for curing some
disease, is said to have been given in the hope
of obtaining a reward. Some people seek to
derive a number of benefits all at once from
a single gift, e.g., they anticipate that:Гі
(a) The donee or recipient of the gift
will feel obliged and will take sides with
them in a good or evil cause when the time
comes for it;
(b) They will gain reputation, which
will add to their prestige and bring honour to
them;
(c) Publicity through the press will create
an impression on the minds of the general
public that the donors are very rich; this will
give them a number of advantages in business
and they will be able to make maximum
profits;
(d) Good reputation will enable them to
secure high connections for their sons and
daughters, which will serve a number of their
ends;
(e) Their gift as a matter of course will
yield in the next world the maximum return
promised in the вҖәГҘstras.
The disjunctive particle Г«TuГӯ in this verse
is intended to distinguish the RГҘjasika from
the SГҘttvika type of gifts.
A donation which is given in a reluctant
and helpless mood under threat or coercion,
due to pressure exerted by respectable and
influential men, in response to an importunity
or with a covert feeling of discomfiture and
sorrow, is said to have been given in a
grudging spirit.
A gift bestowed on someone who is of
permanent utility or who is expected or
calculated to render some useful service, great
or small, later on, is no true gift, really
speaking that is something like a sum advanced
to ensure a service. For instance, on sacred
occasions like a SomavatвҲӮ AmГҘvasyГҘ (the
last day of a dark fortnight synchronizing
with a monday, which is a rare phenomenon)
it is customary these days to bestow a gift on
such BrГҘhmaЖ’as as are useful to ourselves or
to our friends or relatives, and who are expected
to render some service in future, or to donate
a sum to institutions or organizers of institutions
who are expected in return to serve our ends
in ways more than one. This is what is meant
by bestowing a gift with the object of getting
a service in return.

701
Calculations of this kind detract much
from the value of a gift.
The use of the particles Г«VГҘГӯ, Г«PunaВЁГӯ
and Г«CaГӯ is intended to convey that a gift is
RГҘjasika if it possesses any of the three traits
mentioned in the verse.
The Lord now defines a TГҘmasika gift.

A gift which is made without good grace and in a disdainful spirit, out of time and
place, and to undeserving person or persons, is said to be TГҘmasika.                          (22)
place paves the way for hell, hence it has
been denounced as Г«TГҘmasikaГӯ.
Those who need no gift and who have
been precluded by the вҖәГҘstras from receiving
a gift, e.g., he who marks a show of piety, a
hypocrite, an imposter, a cruel man, a
caluminator, he who is intent on achieving
his own selfish ends by depriving others of
their means of subsistence, he who affects
humility, he who takes forbidden articles
such as meat and wine, he who indulges in
mean pursuits such as thieving, adultery etc.,
a swindler, a gambler, an unbeliever and so
on, all these are unfit for receiving a gift. A
gift bestowed on them is fruitless and leads
the donor to hell; hence it is TГҘmasika in
character. This should, not, however, be taken
to debar anyone giving food, water, clothing
and medicine etc., to the hungry, thirsty,
naked and sick according to their respective
needs.
A gift which is given in a cold manner
without due attention to a deserving man,
who comes to receive it, i.e., without greeting
him, enquiring after his health, addressing
sweet and polite words to him and offering
him a seat etc., is known as Г«AsatkГҰtamГӯ.
Similarly, a gift which is made with an
adverse criticism and unpleasant remarks,
which is accompanied with a threat and a
strict warning not to appear again, and which
is given with words of ridicule or with an
insult offered through speech, body or gesture,
is called Г«AvajВӨГҘtamГӯ.
That time and place which are not suitable
for a gift, that is to say, in which a gift is
unnecessary or forbidden by the scriptures
(e.g., the gift of a cow in a place where
barberous or Mlecchas live or giving away
girl in marriage during an eclipse), are referred
to here by the compound word Г«AdeвҖ№akГҘleГӯ.
A gift which is made at such a time and
Thus, with a view to declaring sacrifice, austerity and charity etc., of the SГҘttvika type
as worth practising and those of the RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika types as worth renouncing,
sacrifice etc., have been classified under three heads each. The next topic is now introduced
in order to show that connection they have with God and how a flaw or defect in the
practice of the SГҘttvika types of sacrifice, austerity and charity is set right, and so on.
Лҷ ГғГ вҖҡГҒЕ’ГҒГғ ГҒЕёЕ’пЈҝВёвүҘГҠГҠпЈҝ вҖҷЛқrГҠГ”ГЎГҠГҒSГІГҠГҒп¬ӮЕ“вҖ” Sв—ҠГҺГғвҖ”вҖ“
вҖҷЛқГҠrГҠГ”ГЎГҠГҠSГғпЈҝЕё п¬ӮпЈҝЕ’ГҠp ГҝГҙГҠГҠp ГҒп¬ӮГҒвҖһUГғГҠвҖ” В¬ГҲвҒ„UГҠH 23H

=== PAGE 703 ===

702
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Although there are countless names of
Brahma or Almighty God, the appellations,
OГҖ, TAT and SAT have been recognized
as the foremost in the Vedas and they are
specially connected with sacred pursuits like
sacrifice, austerity and charity. Hence these
three alone have been mentioned here.
The pronoun Г«TenaГӯ refers to Brahma
who bears the three appellations, rather than
to the appellations (NirdeвҖ№aВЁ) themselves.
Here it may be urged that while in
Chapter III above the entire creation including
sacrifices has been spoken of as having
emanated from BrahmГҘ, the Lord of creation
(III.10), the present verse declares the
BrГҘhmaЖ’as etc., as having evolved from God
Himself; how are these two statements to be
reconciled? In this connection it may be
submitted that BrahmГҘ, the lord of creation,
has taken his descent from God, while the
BrГҘhmaЖ’as, the Vedas and sacrifices etc.,
have all emanated from BrahmГҘ. Therefore,
at some places these have been spoken of as
having emanated from God Himself, while at
others they have been declared as having
evolved from BrahmГҘ, the lord of creation. It
is, however, just the same.
The word Г«BrГҘhmaЖ’ГҘВЁГӯ in this verse
should be taken to cover all created beings
from the BrГҘhmaЖ’a downwards; and Г«YajВӨГҘВЁГӯ
should be understood to include sacrifice,
austerity, charity and all other duties of an
obligatory nature enjoined by the scriptures.
The idea underlying this verse is that
the syllables OГҖ, TAT and SAT are
appellations of God, from whom this entire
creation consisting of the sacrificer, the act of
sacrifice and the body of rules governing
such sacrificial performances has emanated;
hence the utterance etc., of these appellations
corrects the flaws and defects of all these
performances. Utterance of the names of God
is, therefore, most essential at the beginning
of each undertaking.
Лҷ the syllable OГҖ; ГғГғЛңГ” the syllable TAT; вҖҡГғЛңГ” the syllable SAT; ГҹГҒГғU this; ГҒЕёЕ’пЈҝВёвүҘГҠвҖ”
appellation; вҖҷЛқrГҠГ”ГЎГҠвҖ” of the Absolute (who is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss solidified); ГҒГІГҠГҒп¬ӮЕ“вҖ”
threefold; Sв—ҠГҺГғвҖ”Г” has been declared; вҖҷЛқГҠrГҠГ”ГЎГҠГҠвҖ” the BrГҘhmaЖ’as; ГғпЈҝЕё by that; п¬ӮпЈҝЕ’ГҠвҖ” the Vedas; Гё and;
ГҝГҙГҠГҠвҖ” sacrifices etc.; Гё as well as; ГҒп¬ӮГҒвҖһUГғГҠвҖ” were created; В¬ГҲвҒ„UГҠГ” at the cosmic dawn.
OГҖ, TAT and SATГіthis has been declared as the threefold appellation of the
Absolute, who is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss. By That the BrГҘhmaЖ’as and the
Vedas as well as sacrifices were created at the cosmic dawn.
 (23)
Here it may be asked: What connection do these three names of GodГіOГҖ, TAT and
SATГіhave with sacrifice, austerity and charity etc.? Anticipating this query, the Lord first
tells us about the use of OГҖ.
ГғSв—ҠГҠЕ’ГҠпЈҝГҒв—ҠГ ГҝГҲЕ’ГҠNUГ Гҝ 
ГҝГҙГҠЕ’ГҠЕёГғВ¬вҖ”ГҒвҲ‘ЛқВ§ГҝГҠвҖ”вҖ“
В¬Лқп¬ӮГғВёГЈГғпЈҝ ГҒп¬ӮЕ“ГҠЕёГҠпЈҝQВ§ГҠвҖ” вҖҡГғГғВў вҖҷЛқrГҠГ”п¬ӮГҠГҒЕ’ЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ”H 24H
ГғSв—ҠГҠГғЛңГ” therefore; вҖўГҠпЈҝв—ҠЛңГ” the syllable OГҖ; ГҹГҒГғ this; В©UЕ’ГҠNUГ Гҝ uttering; ГҝГҙГҠЕ’ГҠЕёГғВ¬вҖ”ГҒвҲ‘ЛқВ§ГҝГҠвҖ”U acts of
sacrifice, charity and austerity; В¬Лқп¬ӮГғВёГЈГғпЈҝ proceed; ГҒп¬ӮЕ“ГҠЕёГҠпЈҝQВ§ГҠвҖ” enjoined by sacred precepts; вҖҡГғГғв—ҠЛңГ”
always; вҖҷЛқrГҠГ”п¬ӮГҠГҒЕ’ЕёГҠв—ҠЛңГ” of noble persons given to the recitation of Vedic chants.
Therefore, acts of sacrifice, charity and austerity as enjoined by sacred precepts
are always commenced by noble persons, given to the recitation of Vedic chants with
the invocation of the divine name, Г«OГҖГӯ.
 (24)

=== PAGE 704 ===

* Chapter XVII *
703
The present verse is primarily intended
to reveal the glory of the Divine Name. What
the Lord seeks to convey through this verse
is that, being a name of God from whom
acts of sacrifice etc., have proceeded, the
syllable OГҖ corrects by its very utterance the
flaws and defects of all such performances
and lends them a sacred and blessed
character. Such is the infinite glory of the
Divine Name. The word Г«BrahmavГҘdinГҘmГӯ
refers to those learned BrГҘhmaЖ’as, KВ¶atriyas
and VaiвҖ№yas who are qualified to perform
sacrifices and other sacred acts with reciting
the Vedic chants. They never and in no
case undertake any sacred performance
whatsoever without uttering the holy name
Г«OГҖГӯ. Therefore, everyone should emulate
their noble example.
Having thus spoken about the use of the syllable OГҖ, the Lord now tells us about the
use of another name of GodГіTAT.
ГғГҒЕ’Г ГҝЕёГҒГ·вҖҡГЈЕ“ГҠГҝ

Г«TATГӯ, acts of sacrifice and austerity as well as acts of charity of various kinds are
performed by the seekers of blessedness, expecting no return for them.                         (25)
mere instrument.

the injunctions of the scriptures and are

The compound word Г«SadbhГҘveГӯ in this

which lasts for ever, and such is the essential

Г«SATГӯ.
The guileless and noble disposition

Г«SadbhГҘvaГӯ.
A noble deed which is worth performing

Г«SATГӯ, is used with reference to it, that is to

as is performed according to the behest of

The words Г«YajВӨeГӯ, Г«TapasiГӯ Г«DГҘneГӯ in

In this way the Lord pointed out the value of actions such as sacrifice, austerity and
charity etc., enjoined by the scriptures and performed with reverence. This naturally tempts
one to enquire about the result of those acts of sacrifice etc., which, though enjoined by the
вҖәГҘstras, are performed without faith. Anticipating this query, the Lord now concludes the
chapter with the following remarks.

=== PAGE 706 ===

* Chapter XVII *
705
An offering into the sacred fire, a gift
and an act of penance and other noble deeds
are able to purify the heart and bestow rewards
in this world or the next only when they are
accompanied with faith. Noble deeds done
without faith are fruitless; that is why they
have been declared as naught (Asat) and of
no avail here or hereafter.
There is no need of faith in perpetrating
forbidden acts and their fruit too does not
depend on oneГӯs faith. Moreover, they are
perpetrated by those men alone who do not
possess full faith in the вҖәГҘstras, exalted souls
and God Himself, and who do not believe
that sinful acts bear fruit; nevertheless they do
reap the painful consequences of such acts.
Therefore, the words Г«Yat KГҰtamГӯ do not include
sinful deeds. Besides this, having been
mentioned along with noble acts such as
sacrifice, charity and austerity, Г«Yat KГҰtamГӯ
can only denote actions of the same category.
Hence the statement that these actions are of
no avail here or hereafter cannot apply to sinful
deeds; for since they are purely productive of
sorrow, there is no possibility whatsoever of
their yielding any good results. Hence the
present verse speaks only of noble deeds
performed without faith, and not of vile deeds.
вҖўГјГҠhГҝГҠ вҖһГҲUГғВў Е’Г»ГҠВў ГғВ¬SГғ#Вў вҲ‘ГҺВ§ГғВў Гё ГҝГғЛңГ”вҖ“
вҖўвҖҡГҒЕ’Г ГҝГҲГ«ГҝГғпЈҝ В¬ГҠГ•Вё Её Гё ГғГ В¬ЛқпЈҝГ Гҝ ЕёГҠпЈҝ ГҹвҖһUH 28H
вҖўГјГҠhГҝГҠ without faith; вҖһГҲUГғв—ҠЛңГ” that which is offered as an oblation; Е’Г»ГҠв—ҠЛңГ” that which is given;
ГғВ¬вҖ” Гғ#в—ҠЛңГ” austerity practised; вҲ‘ГҺВ§Гғв—ҠЛңГ” (noble act) is performed; Гё and; ГҝГғЛңГ” whatever; вҖўвҖҡГғЛңГ” naught;
ГҹГҒГғ so; В©UГ«ГҝГғпЈҝ is declared; В¬ГҠГ•Вё O son of PГҰthГҘ (KuntвҲӮ), Arjuna;  Её Гё nor; ГғГғЛңГ” that (is of avail);
В¬ЛқпЈҝГ Гҝ hereafter; ЕёГҠпЈҝ neither; ГҹвҖһU here.
An oblation which is offered, a gift given, an austerity practised, and whatever
good deed is performed, if it is without faith, it is all termed as naught i.e., Г«AsatГӯ;
therefore, it is of no avail here or hereafter.                                                                     (27)
Лҷ ГғГ вҖҡГҒЕ’ГҒГғ ГјГҠГӢв—ҠjВӘп¬ӮeГӢГғГҠвҖҡГҚВ¬ГҒЕёВ·Г вҖҡГҲ вҖҷЛқrГҠГ”ГҒп¬ӮlГҠГҝГҠВў
ГҝГҠпЈҝВӘвүҘГҠГҠSГІГҠпЈҝ ГјГҠГӢвҲ‘ГҺВ§cГЎГҠГҠВЎГҲВёЕёвҖҡВўп¬ӮГҠЕ’пЈҝ ГјГҠhГҠГІГҠГҝГҒп¬ӮГ·ГҠВӘГҝГҠпЈҝВӘГҠпЈҝ
ЕёГҠв—Ҡ вҖҡ#Е’вүҘГҠГҠпЈҝЛҳГӨГҝГҠГҝвҖ”H 17H
Thus, in the UpaniВ¶ad sung by the Lord, the science of Brahma, the scripture
of Yoga, the dialogue between вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a and Arjuna, ends the seventeenth
chapter entitled Г¬The Yoga of the Division of the Threefold FaithГ®.

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Title of the
Chapter
Summary of
the Chapter
Лҷ
вҖәrвҲӮmadbhagavad-GвҲӮtГҘ
Chapter XVIII
MokВ¶a consists in securing lasting freedom from the bondage of mundane existence in
the form of birth and death and realizing God, who is no other than supreme Bliss. Summing
up the substance of all previous chapters, the present one discusses, under the
names of Г«SannyГҘsaГӯ and Г«TyГҘgaГӯ respectively, the Paths of Knowledge and
Action, both of which are means to the attainment of MokВ¶a, in all their
details; and secondly, the teaching of the GвҲӮtГҘ has been wound up (XVIII.66) with an
exhortation to offer all actions to God, who is the same as MokВ¶a. It is for these reasons that
the chapter has been given the title of Г«MokВ¶a-SannyГҘsa-YogaГӯ.
In the opening verse of this chapter Arjuna expresses his desire to know the truth about
SannyГҘsa and TyГҘga: in the second and third the Lord records the views of other thinkers
on the subject; in the fourth and fifth He invites Arjuna to hear His own
conclusion about TyГҘga, and establishes the advisability of not relinquishing
oneГӯs obligatory duties, while in the sixth He gives His own considered
opinion in the matter of TyГҘga, and declares it as superior to the other views. Thereafter,
defining the TГҘmasika, RГҘjasika and SГҘttvika forms of TyГҘga in the seventh, eighth and ninth
respectively, He describes the marks of a TyГҘgвҲӮ (man of renunciation) of the SГҘttvika type in
the tenth and eleventh. Demonstrating the greatness of men of renunciation the Lord winds
up the topic of TyГҘga in the twelfth. Thereafter, inviting Arjuna to hear His dissertation on
SГҘвҲҶkhya, the Lord discusses up to the fifteenth the five factors which contribute to the
accomplishment of actions, viz., AdhiВ¶ВўhГҘna etc., according to the viewpoint of SГҘвҲҶkhya.
Denouncing in the sixteenth the man who regards the pure spirit as the doer, the Lord praises
in the seventeenth one who performs actions without claiming the doership. Mentioning the
threefold incentive to action as well as the three constituents of action in the eighteenth, the
Lord introduces the threefold division of knowledge and action as well as of the doer in the
nineteenth, and actually divides them under three heads, viz., SГҘttvika, RГҘjasika, and TГҘmasika
from the twentieth to the twenty-eighth. Introducing in the twenty-ninth the threefold division
of Buddhi (reason) and DhГҰti (firmness), the Lord divides them under three heads, viz., SГҘttvika,
RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika, from the thirtieth to the thirty-fifth. Dividing Sukha (joy) under three
heads, viz., SГҘttvika, RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika, from the thirty-sixth to the thirty-ninth, the
Lord winds up the topic of GuЖ’as and declares the whole world as made up of the three
GuЖ’as in the fortieth. Thereafter, introducing the topic of the natural functions of the four
grades of society in the forty-first, the Lord enumerates the natural functions of the BrГҘhmaЖ’as
in the forty-second, of the KВ¶atriyas in the forty-third and of the VaiвҖ№yas and вҖәВөudras in the
forty-fourth. Declaring in the forty-fifth that men can attain highest perfection through the
performance of their respective duties, the Lord tells us in the forty-sixth the method of attaining
it. Extolling the performance of oneГӯs own duty in the forty-seventh, He discourages renunciation
of the same in the forty-eighth. Thereafter, resuming the discussion of SannyГҘsayoga from
the forty-ninth and declaring that one can attain the highest perfection through SannyГҘsa, the

=== PAGE 708 ===

* Chapter XVIII *
707
Link of the
Discourse
Lord undertakes in the fiftieth to speak of the culmination of the Yoga of Knowledge and
discusses from the fifty-first to the fifty-fifth the Yoga of Knowledge with its fruit. Thereafter,
from the fifty-sixth to the fifty-eighth the Lord points out the glory and fruit of Karmayoga
which lays special emphasis on Bhakti and commands Arjuna to practise the same, warning
him at the same time of the risk involved in not obeying Him. Declaring in the fifty-ninth and
sixtieth the inability of Arjuna to abandon his natural duties due to his inherent nature, the
Lord commands him in the sixty-first and sixty-second to take refuge with all his being in
God, who is the Lord and inner controller of all. Winding up the topic in the sixty-third and
asking Arjuna to do as he pleased after weighing everything, the Lord invites him in the
sixty-fourth to hear once more the most esoteric truth, which is the quintessence of the whole
GвҲӮtГҘ. And imparting in the sixty-fifth and sixty-sixth that most esoteric gospel of exclusive
surrender along with its fruit, the Lord winds up the teaching of the GвҲӮtГҘ by asking Arjuna to
take refuge in Him. Thereafter, warning Arjuna in the sixty-seventh not to impart the teaching
of the GвҲӮtГҘ to four types of unqualified persons, the Lord stresses the importance of popularizing
the GвҲӮtГҘ among qualified persons in the sixty-eighth and sixty-ninth, of studying the GвҲӮtГҘ in
the seventieth, and of merely listening with faith to the recitation of the GвҲӮtГҘ in the seventy-
first. In the seventy-second the Lord asks Arjuna if he listened to the GвҲӮtГҘ with one-pointed
attention and whether his delusion was dispelled; and in the seventy-third Arjuna assures the
Lord that his delusion was dispelled, wisdom gained and all his doubts removed, and agrees
to do His bidding. Thereafter, from the seventy-fourth to the seventy-seventh SaВӨjaya glorifies
the teaching of the GвҲӮtГҘ in the form of a dialogue between вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a and Arjuna, and tells
DhГҰtarГҘВ¶Вўra how the thought of that dialogue as well as of the cosmic form of the Lord filled
him with wonder and joy again and again. And he winds up the chapter by proclaiming in
the seventy-eighth that the side with which BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a and Arjuna had ranged
themselves was sure to win and attain glory etc.
The gospel of the GвҲӮtГҘ actually commenced from verse 11 of Chapter II. In verses 11
to 30 of that chapter the Lord taught JВӨГҘnayoga and, incidentally establishing the
advisability of waging war from the viewpoint of the code of morality laid
down for a KВ¶atriya, taught Karmayoga from verse 39 right up to the end
of the chapter. Thereafter, from Chapter III to Chapter XVII, the Lord
detailed a number of disciplines for God-realization, some from the viewpoint of SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga
and others from that of Karmayoga. Having heard of all these, Arjuna now expresses the
desire to know separately and clearly the truth of SannyГҘsa or JВӨГҘnayoga and of TyГҘga or
Karmayoga which consists in relinquishing the attachment for fruit, in order to have the
essence of the teaching of all chapters in the present chapter.
вҖўВЎВёГҲЕё В©Uп¬ӮГҠГё
вҖҡГӣГҝГҠвҖҡSГҝ в—ҠвҖһUГҠвҖҷГҠвҖһUГҠпЈҝ ГғГ»п¬ӮГҒв—ҠГ«ВҝUГҠГҒв—Ҡ п¬ӮпЈҝГҒЕ’ГғГҲв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“

severally; вҲ‘пЈҝВ§ГҒвүҘГҠГҒЕёВ·ГҚЕ’ЕёГ” O slayer of the demon KeвҖ№вҲӮ (who had taken the form of a mighty horse
in order to kill вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a).

708
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
confused with each other.
A critical survey of this chapter will
show that the points mentioned above have
all been touched by the Lord in the course of
it. Thus the essential character of SannyГҘsa
(JВӨГҘnayoga) has been discussed in verses 13
to 17. The SГҘttvika virtues and actions
mentioned in verses 19 to 40 are helpful to
the practice of this discipline, while the RГҘjasika
and TГҘmasika traits and actions are hostile to
it. Verses 50 to 55 describe in detail the
mode as well as the fruit of the practice of
SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga with worship, while verse 17
gives the process of pure SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga.
Even so verse 6 reveals the essential
character of Karmayoga (which consists in
relinquishing the attachment for fruit). Verse
9 gives us under the name of Г«SГҘttvika TyГҘgaГӯ
the mode of practice of the pure or unmixed
type of Karmayoga. Verses 47 and 48
pronounce the performance of oneГӯs own
duty as helpful to the pursuit of this discipline
and the TГҘmasika and RГҘjasika types of
TyГҘga as mentioned in verses 7 and 8 as
impediments to it. Verses 45 and 46 discuss
Karmayoga blended with devotion, while
verses 56 to 66 speak of Karmayoga dominated
by Devotion. Verse 46 teaches us how to
practise Karmayoga blended with Bhakti;
while verse 57 tells us how to practise
Karmayoga with special emphasis on Bhakti.
The use of the vocatives Г«MahГҘbГҘhoГӯ,
Г«HГҰВ¶ikeвҖ№aГӯ and Г«KeвҖ№iniВ¶ВөudanaГӯ is intended to
convey that вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a being Almighty God
Himself, the Indweller of all and the destroyer
of all evils, knows full well what Arjuna,
seeks to learn from Him. Arjuna, therefore,
expects that the Lord would attend to his
prayer and enlighten him on the subject in
such a way as to enable him to grasp it fully
and correctly and to resolve all his doubts
completely.
In the present verse Arjuna seeks light on
the following points; what is the essential
character of SannyГҘsa? What type of ideas and
actions are helpful to it and what are prejudicial
to it? How is SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga practised with
worship and how is it practised without
worship? Even so, what is the essential character
of TyГҘga (i.e., Karmayoga, which consists in
relinquishing the attachment for fruit). How do
they practise Karmayoga of the unmixed type?
What practices are helpful to it and what hinder
it? What is Karmayoga mixed with Bhakti or
Devotion? What is the nature of Karmayoga
dominated by Bhakti? And how do they practise
Karmayoga mixed with Bhakti and Karmayoga
dominated by Bhakti while performing their
secular and sacred duties? Arjuna further
requests that the points of difference between
the two may be clearly brought out, so that
they may not be promiscuously blended or
Arjuna said: O mighty-armed вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a, O HГҰВ¶вҲӮkeвҖ№a, O Slayer of KeвҖ№вҲӮ, I wish to
know severally the truth of SannyГҘsa and TyГҘga.
 (1)
Thus addressed by Arjuna, the Lord cites the diverse views of other thinkers on the subject
of SannyГҘsa and TyГҘga in two verses before expressing His own opinion in the matter.

=== PAGE 710 ===

* Chapter XVIII *
709
Particular acts of sacrifice, charity,
penance and worship and other noble acts
that have been recommended in the вҖәГҘstras
for the attainment of agreeable objects such
as wife, progeny, wealth and an abode in
heaven etc., and for getting rid of that which
is disagreeable, such as disease and danger
etc., are referred to here as Г«KГҘmyГҘnГҘm
KarmaЖ’ГҘmГӯ. The word Г«KГҘmyГҘnГҘmГӯ further
indicates that such acts are optional and not
obligatory. That is to say, they may be
undertaken if one seeks some return for them;
but their omission on the part of those who
seek no return will bring no harm to them.
The first half of this verse is intended to
show that according to some sages SannyГҘsa
consists in merely giving up such optional
acts. In the opinion of these sages SannyГҘsвҲӮs
are those who duly perform only such duties
as must be performed daily or require to
be performed on special occasions, and
have given up the optional acts referred
to above.
Devotion to God, worship of other deities,
service of oneГӯs parents and other elders,
sacrifice, charity and penance and vocational
duties peculiar to oneГӯs grade in society and
stage in life and bodily functions such as taking
food, drink and so on, and all other duties of
an obligatory nature enjoined by the scriptures,
the omission of which interferes with morality,
religion and tradition are included in
Г«SarvakarmaГӯ. And Г«SarvakarmaphalatyГҘgaГӯ
consists in giving up the desire for all
enjoyments of this world as well as of the next,
such as wife, progeny, wealth, honour, fame,
prestige and heavenly bliss etc., obtained
through the performance of such acts, that is
to say, in associating no such action with the
idea of any reward.
By the latter half of this verse the Lord
seeks to convey that, in the eyes of those
who use their critical faculty in distinguishing
the eternal from the evanescent. TyГҘga consists
in relinquishing the fruit of all actions as
indicated above, and performing oneГӯs
obligatory duties alone; hence they perform
all their duties in such a spirit.
вҖәrвҲӮ BhagavГҘn said: Some sages understand SaвүҲyГҘsa as the giving up of all actions
motivated by desire; and other discerning thinkers declare that TyГҘga consists in
relinquishing the fruit of all actions.                                                      (2)
Г ГҝГҠГ–ГҝВў Е’ГҠпЈҝВ·п¬ӮГҒЕ’Г ГҝпЈҝвҲ‘пЈҝВ§ вҲ‘В§в—ҠВё В¬ЛқГҠвҖһГҲUв—ҠВёЕёГӢГҒВ·ГЎГҠвҖ”Г”вҖ“
ГҝГҙГҠЕ’ГҠЕёГғВ¬вҖ”вҲ‘В§в—ҠВё Её Г ГҝГҠГ–ГҝГҒв—ҠГҒГғ ГёГҠВ¬вҒ„пЈҝUH 3H
Г ГҝГҠГ–Гҝв—ҠЛңГ” (and therefore) worth giving up; Е’ГҠпЈҝВ·п¬ӮГғЛңГ” contains some measure of evil; ГҹГҒГғU (say)
thus; вҲһвҲ‘пЈҝВ§ some; вҲ‘В§в—ҠВё (all) action; В¬ЛқГҠвҖһГҲUвҖ” declare (that); в—ҠЕёГӢГҒВ·ГЎГҠвҖ” men of wisdom; ГҝГҙГҠЕ’ГҠЕёГғВ¬вҖ”вҲ‘В§в—ҠВё
(that) acts of sacrifice, charity and penance; Её Г ГҝГҠГ–Гҝв—ҠЛңГ” (are) not to be given up; ГҹГҒГғГ” thus; ГёГ”
and; вҖўВ¬вҒ„пЈҝU others.
Some wise men declare that all actions contain a measure of evil, and are,
therefore, worth giving up; while others say that acts of sacrifice, charity and penance
are not to be given up.                                                                    (3)
hence even such duties as have been enjoined
on us are not wholly free from blemish. It is
in order to impress this idea that the Lord
By the first half of this verse the Lord
seeks to convey that every undertaking
invariably gets tainted with sin to some extent,

=== PAGE 711 ===

710
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
By addressing Arjuna as Г«BharatasattamaГӯ
and Г«PuruВ¶avyГҘghraГӯ the Lord seeks to convey
that of the three types of TyГҘga going to be
discussed by Him in the course of this chapter,
he is capable of practising the SГҘttvika form
of TyГҘga, which is only another name for
Г«KarmayogaГӯ or the Path of Action, and
should avoid the other two, viz., the RГҘjasika
and TГҘmasika types.
It will be remembered that in the opening
verse of this chapter, Arjuna requested the
Lord to reveal the truth of both SannyГҘsa and
TyГҘga; and of these two, the Lord proceeds
in this verse to discuss the Truth of TyГҘga in
the first instance. This is what is sought to be
conveyed by the use of the indeclinable Г«TatraГӯ
in this verse. Arjuna requested the Lord to
expound the truth of the two separately and
the Lord has hinted as His intention to discuss
the subject of TyГҘga alone without declining
his request. This shows that He will discuss
the subject of SannyГҘsa later on.
The use of the words Г«Me NiвҖ№cayamГӯ in
this verse is intended to convey that none of
the views so far expressed by Him fully
represents His own view, which is now
introduced by Him.
By referring to the three types of TyГҘga
mentioned in the вҖәГҘstras, the Lord indicates
that the view He holds on the subject is
supported by the scriptures. This He does in
order to show His regard for the scriptures.
According to other wise men, however,
acts of sacrifice, charity and penance are not
really tainted with blemish. They hold that
the sins of violence etc., with which
undertakings of all sorts are unavoidably found
tainted are really no sins; on the other hand,
having been enjoined by the scriptures, they
serve to purify the agent. Hence a man seeking
beatitude should shun only prohibited acts
and should not abstain from duties enjoined
by the вҖәГҘstras.
Himself says elsewhere in the course of this
very chapter that Г¬all undertaking, are involved
in sin as fire in smoke (XVIII.48).* Therefore,
some men of wisdom declare that the man
who seeks blessedness should actually give
up all kinds of actions including those that
must be performed daily, those that require to
be performed on special occasions and those
that are motivated by desire, or in other
words that he should enter the order of
SaвүҲnyГҘsa or Renunciation.
Having thus cited the divergent views of men of wisdom on the subject of SannyГҘsa and
TyГҘga, the Lord now proceeds to pronounce His own conclusion on the subject of TyГҘga.

has been declared to be.
Of SannyГҘsa and TyГҘga, first hear My conclusion on the subject of renunciation
(TyГҘga), Arjuna; for renunciation, O tiger among men, has been declared to be of
three kindsГіSГҘttvika, RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika.                                                         (4)
Thus calling the attention of Arjuna to His own view on the subject, and in order to
reveal the true nature of TyГҘga as understood by Him, the Lord first of all gives in two
verses His conclusion about the performance of commendable acts enjoined by the scriptures.
* вҖҡп¬ӮГҠВёвҒ„UГӘГ·ГҠ ГҒвҖһU Е’ГҠпЈҝВ·пЈҝГЎГҠ Е“ГҚв—ҠпЈҝЕёГҠГҒГҮЕёГҒвҒ„Uп¬ӮГҠп¬ӮГҺГғГҠвҖ”вҖ“

* Chapter XVIII *

tells us in what spirit they are to be performed.
The word Г«ManiВ¶вҲӮЖ’ГҘmГӯ in this verse
stands for those wise seekers of liberation
who duly and scrupulously perform, in a
disinterested spirit and according to the sacred
precepts, the duties that have been assigned
to them by the scriptures with due regard to
their grade in society and stage in life. Instead
of causing bondage, the acts of sacrifice,
charity and penance performed by them serve
to purify their heart: hence one must perform
these acts in a disinterested spirit. This is
what is sought to be conveyed by the latter
half of this verse.
The first half of this verse impresses upon
Arjuna the obligatory nature of duties enjoined
by the the scriptures. The idea is that the man
to whom a particular duty has been assigned
by the вҖәГҘstras with due regard to his grade in
society and stage in lifeГіwho has been
enjoined to perform sacrifices and penance
and bestow gifts at a particular time and in a
particular mannerГіshould not shun that duty:
in other words, he should not flout the
injunctions of the scriptures; for far from
yielding any good results, their omission brings
the sin of default on oneГӯs head. Hence one
must perform these duties. The following verse

performed; ГҹГҒГғ this (is); в—ҠпЈҝ My; В¬ГҠГ•Вё O son of PГҰthГҘ (KuntвҲӮ), Arjuna; ГҒЕёГҒpГғв—ҠЛңГ” well-considered;
в—ҠГғв—ҠЛңГ” verdict; В©UГ»ГҠв—Ҡв—ҠЛңГ” (and) supreme.
Hence these acts of sacrifice, charity and penance, and all other acts of duty too,
must be performed without attachment and expectation of reward: this is My well-
considered and supreme verdict, Arjuna.                                                                  (6)
food and drink and all other duties enjoined

Г«SaвҲҶgamГӯ means attachment, not only to
the action itself, but to its fruit as well; while
Г«PhalГҘniГӯ means the desire for fruit in the

=== PAGE 713 ===

712
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
should abstain from prohibited acts as well as
from acts which are motivated by desire.
The words Г¬Iti Me niвҖ№citam matam-
uttamamГ® signify that such is the LordГӯs own
conception of TyГҘga. That is to say, in the
opinion of the Lord, TyГҘga consists in
performing oneГӯs allotted duties without
attachment and without any hope of reward;
for, he who performs his duties in the aforesaid
manner is freed from the bondage of actions
and attains the supreme state. He ceases to
have anything to do with his actions.
Now let us examine the views of other
sages referred to by the Lord as well as that
of the Lord Himself on their own merits and
see for ourselves how the LordГӯs own view
is decidedly superior to the other views. It
will thus be seen that the definitions of TyГҘga
and SannyГҘsa as pronounced by other sages
are far from ideal or perfect. For, even if one
actually abstains from actions which are
motivated by desire, other actions of an
obligatory or occasional type will lead to
bondage if one continues to entertain a feeling
of mineness, attachment and desire with regard
to those actions and their fruit. Even if one
gives up the desire for the fruit of all actions,
they may lead to bondage if one continues to
entertain the feeling of mineness and attachment
with respect to them. Without renouncing the
feeling of Г«IГӯ and Г«mineГӯ, attachment and
desire we cannot be freed from the bondage
of actions even if we shun our obligatory
duties, regarding all action as containing some
measure of evil; for by doing so we incur the
sin of neglecting a prescribed duty. Even so,
acts of sacrifice, charity and penance will
lead to bondage if one continues to perform
them without renouncing attachment and the
desire for their fruit. Therefore, SannyГҘsa
and TyГҘga as defined by those sages cannot
secure complete freedom from the bondage
of actions. Complete renunciation or TyГҘga
consists in renouncing the feeling of mineness
and attachment in respect of all oneГӯs actions,
as well as the desire for their fruit, as advised
by the Lord. By doing so one secures complete
freedom from the bondage of actions. For
action in itself is not conducive to bondage;
it is the feeling of mineness and attachment
with respect to it and the desire for its
fruit that lead to bondage. Herein lies the
superiority of the LordГӯs view over the views
of others.
Thus expressing His considered opinion in the matter, the Lord now defines in an
ascending order the three types of TyГҘga in the following three verses, and begins with the
marks of the TГҘmasika type. His object in doing so is to point out that of the three types of
TyГҘga mentioned in the scriptures, viz., SГҘttvika, RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika, the SГҘttvika type
alone is real TyГҘga and worth practising, while the other two types of TyГҘga are no TyГҘga
in the real sense of the term and are not worth practising, as well as to show that His view
is in complete accord with the SГҘstras.
ГҒЕёГҝГғSГҝ ГғГҲ вҖҡГӣГҝГҠвҖҡвҖ” вҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГЎГҠГҠпЈҝ ЕёГҠпЈҝВ¬В¬lГғпЈҝГ”вҖ“
в—ҠГҠпЈҝвҖһUГҠГ»ГҠSГҝ В¬ГҒвҒ„UГ ГҝГҠВӘSГғГҠв—ҠвҖҡвҖ” В¬ГҒвҒ„UвҲ‘В§ГӢГҒГғВёГғвҖ”Г”H 7H
ГҒЕёГҝГғSГҝ of a prescribed; ГғГҲ but; вҖҡГӣГҝГҠвҖҡвҖ” (actual) renunciation; вҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГЎГҠвҖ” duty; ЕёГ” not; В©UВ¬В¬lГғпЈҝ
is advisable; в—ҠГҠпЈҝвҖһUГҠГғЛңГ” through ignorance; ГғSГҝ its; В¬ГҒвҒ„UГ ГҝГҠВӘвҖ” abandonment; ГғГҠв—ҠвҖҡвҖ” TГҘmasika in
character; В¬ГҒвҒ„UвҲ‘В§ГӢГҒГғВёГғвҖ”Г” has been declared.
(Prohibited acts and those that are motivated by desire should, no doubt, be given
up). But it is not advisable to abandon a prescribed duty. Such abandonment out of
ignorance has been declared as Г«TГҘmasikaГӯ.                                                (7)

* Chapter XVIII *
713
Sacrifice, charity, penance, study and
teaching, giving religious discourses, warfare,
governance of the people, rearing of cattle,
agriculture, trade, service, eating and drinking
and all other duties that have been enjoined
by the вҖәГҘstras as binding on a particular man
with due regard to his grade in society, stage
in life, temperament and circumstances, are
Г«Niyata KarmaГӯ for him. He who actually
gives up these duties fails to discharge
his obligation and thereby incurs sin; for
such neglect of duty causes a brake in the
continuity of action and brings about a
chaos in the whole world (III.23-24). Hence
it is not advisable to abandon oneГӯs allotted
duties.
The TyГҘga or renunciation on the part
of him who gives up his allotted duty,
erroneusly taking such renunciation to be a
means to liberation, is inspired by Tamas
inasmuch as it is actuated by ignorance; for
Moha or ignorance has been declared to be a
product of TamoguЖ’a (XIV.13,17). And it
has been pointed out by the Lord that men
possessing a TГҘmasika disposition fall in the
scale of spiritual evolution (XIV.18). Hence
the form of renunciation referred to above is
not such as enables one to secure freedom
from the bondage of Karma. On the other
hand, it brings about oneГӯs downfall inasmuch
as it involves one in the sin accruing from
neglect of duty.
Having characterized the TГҘmasika form of TyГҘga, the Lord now defines RГҘjasika
TyГҘga.
Е’ГҲвҖ”ПҖГҒв—ҠГ ГҝпЈҝп¬Ӯ ГҝГ вҲ‘В§в—ҠВё вҲ‘В§ГҠГҝГ„вҖ№пЈҝвүҘГҠГ·ГҝГҠГ»ГҝВЎпЈҝГғГ”ЛңГ”вҖ“

relaxation is said to abandon his duties for fear
of physical strain.
The latter half of this verse shows that he
who gives up his allotted duties and enters the
order of SannyГҘsa with such feelings, practises
what they call the RГҘjasika form of renunciation;
for attachment to the relaxation of mind, senses
and body is an offspring of RajoguЖ’a. Hence
he who practises this form of renunciation
fails to reap the fruit of true renunciation, viz.,
freedom from the shackles of Karma and
realization of God; for so long as there is the

be freed from the bondage of actions. Hence
The word Г«KarmaГӯ, qualified by the

undertaking sacred vows and fasts, and to
observe many restriction of various kinds.
Thinking thus, he who gives up actions
enjoined by the scriptures, such as sacrifice,

* Chapter XVIII *

austerity etc., and all other duties consistent

Performed in a disinterested spirit, these actions
are capable of wiping out manГӯs accumulated
sins of previous lives and freeing him from
the bondage of actions; hence they are spoken
of as Г«KuвҖ№alaГӯ (conducive to oneГӯs lasting

he performs them for the maintenance of the
world-order, renouncing the feeling of

and with a sense of duty.
The man of a SГҘttvika disposition who
performs or shuns actions with a sense of duty
and without any partiality or prejudice is
convinced that the SГҘttvika TyГҘga in the form
of Karmayoga is the only perfect means of
securing freedom from the bondage of actions
and attaining the supreme state. This is what
is meant by the adjective Г«ChinnasaвүҲвҖ№ayaВЁГӯ.
The word Г«KarmaГӯ, qualified by the

sinful acts prohibited by the вҖәГҘstras, as well
as for those that are motivated by desire; for,

human species of various kinds and throw
one into the infernal regions, actions, motivated
by desire too bring about rebirth in order to
enable one to reap their fruit. Being thus
conducive to bondage, both these types of
action are called Г«AkuвҖ№alaГӯ. The man who
practises the SГҘttvika form of renunciation is
entirely free from likes and dislikes; hence
the shunning of prohibited and desire-born
acts on his part is not actuated by hatred. He
shuns them in the interest of the world order
and with a recognition of the fact that it is his
duty to shun acts which are not conducive to
lasting good. This is what is meant by words

In the above verse the man of renunciation of the SГҘttvika type, i.e., the KarmayogвҲӮ
performing his duties in a disinterested spirit, has been called a man of true renunciation.
This raises the following question. The man who abstains from all sorts of action, as from
those that are prohibited or motivated by desire, can as well be a man of true renunciation;
how, then, does the Lord declare him alone who performs actions in a disinterested spirit
as a man of true renunciation? Anticipating this query, the Lord says:

Since all actions cannot be given up in their entirety by anyone possessing a body,
he alone who renounces the fruit of actions is called a man of renunciation.      (11)

=== PAGE 717 ===

716
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The word Г«DehabhГҰtГҘГӯ covers all human
beings who maintain and nourish their body.
The first half of this verse is, therefore, intended
to convey that no human being can remain
inactive; for without action it is not possible
even to maintain oneГӯs body (III.8). Therefore,
to whatever Г…вҖ№rama or stage in life a man
belongs, so long as he is alive, he must
satisfy his hunger and thirst, keep his body
either in a sitting or a lying posture, stir and
move about, speak and do other things
according to his circumstances. Hence it is
not possible to give up all actions in their
entirety.
The compound word Г«KarmaphalatyГҘgвҲӮГӯ
stands for the KarmayogвҲӮ who performs duties
enjoined by the вҖәГҘstras, renouncing the feeling
of mineness, attachment and desire. Since
everybody must do something, none can remain
inactive. A man of true renunciation is he
who totally abstains from prohibited actions
and those that are motivated by desire and
performs duties enjoined by the вҖәГҘstras
according to the needs of the occasion,
completely renouncing the feeling of mineness,
attachment and desire in respect of those
actions as well as for their fruit. This is what
is meant by the latter half of this verse.
He who dwells with his mind on the
objects of the world, outwardly restraining
the functions of the senses, is no man of
renunciation; and even so he who gives up
duties enjoined by the вҖәГҘstras such as sacrifice,
charity and austerity etc., even though
attachment and the feeling of Г«IГӯ and Г«mineГӯ
are present in him, is no TyГҘgвҲӮ either.
In the above verse it has been stated that a man of renunciation is he who renounces
the fruit of actions. Here it may be urged that even though one may not expect any return
for oneГӯs actions, the latter cannot be undone without yielding their fruit; just as a seed sown
in the soil automatically gives birth to a tree in course of time, even so the fruit of actions
once performed must be reaped by all in one life or another. Such being the case, how can
one be a TyГҘgвҲӮ i.e., Г«free from the bondage of actionsГӯ by merely renouncing the fruit of
action? In order to meet this objection the Lord says:
вҖўГҒЕёcвүҲГ”UГҒв—ҠcвүҲВўU ГҒв—ҠГјГҠВў Гё ГҒГІГҠГҒп¬ӮЕ“Вў вҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГЎГҠвҖ” В»В§вҲӮв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“
Г·п¬ӮГ ГҝГ ГҝГҠГҒВӘЕёГҠВў В¬ЛқпЈҝГ Гҝ Её ГғГҲ вҖҡГӣГҝГҠГҒвҖҡЕёГҠВў Г„п¬ӮГҒГёГғЛңГ”H 12H

The word Г«AtyГҘginГҘmГӯ refers to the

717
beasts, birds, insects, moths and trees etc.,
tortures in hell or sufferings of any other kind
brought about by their sinful deeds are spoken
of as the unwelcome fruit. Even so, being
born as a human being, he who obtains
welcome enjoyments now and undergoes
unwelcome experiences at another time is
said to reap a mixed fruit. This is the threefold
fruit yielded by oneГӯs actions.
The actions performed, by these
unrenouncing men cannot be neutralized until
they have borne fruit, and continue to yield
their welcome or unwelcome fruit life after
life; that is why such men go on revolving
with the cycle of births and deaths.
In his current life man generally reaps
his Г«PrГҘrabdhaГӯ or destiny carved out of his
doings in previous lives; the fruit of his current
actions is seldom reaped in this life. Therefore,
the fruit of actions performed in course of one
human life is bound to be reaped in many lives.
This is what is sought to be conveyed by the
use of the word Г«PretyaГӯ.
The particle Г«TuГӯ is intended to distinguish
those that have renounced the fruit from the
unrenouncing, and establish their superiority
over the latter.
The word Г«SannyГҘsinГҘmГӯ in this verse
stands for those KarmayogвҲӮs who have
completely renounced the feeling of mineness,
attachment and desire in respect of actions
and their fruit, who have been referred to
under the name of Г«TyГҘgвҲӮГӯ in verse 10 above,
and termed both as a Г«SannyГҘsвҲӮГӯ and Г«YogвҲӮГӯ
in the opening verse of Chapter VI, and who
are shown in verse 51 of Chapter II to attain
the supreme blissful state.
Whatever actions are performed by such
men of renunciation are like fried seeds; they
are incapable of bearing fruit. And such
disinterested actions performed for the sake
of sacrifice also neutralize all good and evil
actions performed in previous lives (IV.23).
Hence none of their actions performed in this
life or in lives that have gone by ever yield
any fruit either here or hereafter, under any
circumstances; they are completely rid of the
bondage of actions.
In the opening verse of this chapter Arjuna requested the Lord to expound the truth of
SannyГҘsa and TyГҘga separately. In response to this prayer the Lord stated in verses 2 and
3 the diverse views of different sages on this question and explained at full length the truth
of TyГҘga or Karmayoga as understood by Him in verses 4 to 12. Now, in order to expound
the truth of SannyГҘsa or SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga, the Lord first of all mentions five factors contributory
to the accomplishment of actions from the SГҘвҲҶkhya point of view.

In the branch of learning known as SГҘвҲҶkhya, which prescribes means for
neutralizing all actions, five factors have been mentioned as contributory to the
accomplishment of all actions; know them all from Me, Arjuna.

Mind, intellect and the ego are internal

These five are the contributory causes of whatever actions, right or wrong, man
performs with the mind, speech and body.                                            (15)
Similarly, acts which have been forbidden
by the вҖәГҘstras for some classes of men in
view of their grade in society etc., and all
other sinful acts which run counter to the
principles of morality and religion, such as
lying, thieving, adultery, violence, drinking,
and taking prohibited food, belong to the
category of unrighteous acts.
The words Г«KarmaГӯ, qualified by the
relative adjective Г«YatГӯ, covers all actions, both
good and evil, the fruits of which have to be
reaped here or hereafter. None of these actions
can be accomplished without the joint operation
of the above five factors. The co-operation of
all these is essential for the performance of
any action whatsoever. If any of these five is
missing, no action can be possible. That is
why it has been stated in verse 17 below that
action which is performed without any conscious
agent is no action at all.
The use of word Г«NaraВЁГӯ in this verse is
intended to convey that it is in the human
body alone that the soul is free to perform
fresh actions of a virtuous or sinful type. All
other species of life are meant for pleasurable
and painful experiences alone; in them one
has to reap the consequences of oneГӯs past
actions and is denied freedom of will.
The word Г«вҖәarвҲӮraГӯ forming part of the
compound word Г«SarвҲӮravГҘвҲҶmanobhiВЁГӯ stands
for the physical body including the senses,
while Г«ManasГӯ covers all the aspects of the
mind or the internal organ.
Sacrifice, charity, austerity, study, warfare,
agriculture, rearing of cows, trade service and
all other duties enjoined by the вҖәГҘstras as binding
on a man according to his grade in society,
stage in life, temperament and circumstances,
and performed in a righteous manner, are
covered by the adjective Г«NyГҘyyamГӯ.
Having thus enumerated the five factors, AdhiВ¶ВўhГҘna etc., contributory to the
accomplishment of all actions according to the SГҘвҲҶkhya point of view, the Lord now first
denounces those who recognize the Self as the doer. This He does in order to impress upon
Arjuna that, really speaking, the Self has nothing to do with actions and that it is absolutely
untainted, immutable and a non-doer.

As has been shown in the preceding

=== PAGE 721 ===

720
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
really nothing to do with them. Hence it is
quite absurd to regard the Self as the doer.
Nevertheless, people foolishly arrogate to
themselves the doership of actions; how strange
is that! This is what is sought to be conveyed
by the words Г«Evam SatiГӯ.
The ignorant man of the world, whose
intellect has not been purified through
association with holy men and the study of
scriptures, and through spiritual practices such
as discrimination, reflection and control of
mind and body etc. is referred to here as
Г«AkГҰtabuddhiГӯ. The use of the ablative form
Г«AkГҰtabuddhitvГҘtГӯ is intended to show why
people regard the Self as the doer. The idea
is that even though, really speaking, the Self
has nothing to do with actions, yet, through
lack of discrimination man ignorantly embraces
the belief that the Self is the doer.
The adjective Г«KevalamГӯ used with
reference to Г«Г…tmГҘnamГӯ, is intended to reveal
the true character of the Self, which is Г«absoluteГӯ,
i.e., absolutely pure, immutable and unattached.
вҖәrutis also proclaim that Г¬the Self is, really
speaking, altogether unattached.Г®* (BГҰhad.,
up., IV.iii.15-16). Hence it is quite preposterous
to connect the Self, which is unattached, with
actions and regard it as the doer.
The word Г«DurmatiВЁГӯ has been used to
reveal that the reason of the man who views
the Self as the doer is perverted, that he lacks
the capacity to realize the essential character
of the Self. The truly discerning man is he
who views all actions as a game of PrakГҰti and
the Self as a non-doer in every sense, as declared
in verse 29 of Chapter XIII. As against this, he
who regards the Self as the doer is deluded by
ignorance and egotism (III.27); hence his view
is not correct, but erroneous.
Here it will be seen that of the five
factors declared as contributory to the
accomplishment of actions, four are products
of PrakГҰti, while the fifth, viz., the agent, is
no other than the Self rooted in PrakГҰti. In the
above exposition however, it is stated that
the Self is not the doer, but unattached. How
are we to reconcile these two self-contradictory
statements? In this connection it should be
noted that, really speaking, the Self is eternal,
pure, enlightened, immutable and wholly
unattached; it has nothing to do with PrakГҰti,
objects born of PrakГҰti, or actions. But due to
nescience, that has existed from time without
beginning, the unattached Self has been
connected, as it were, with PrakГҰti; therefore,
falsely identifying itself with the acts performed
by PrakГҰti, it arrogates to itself the doership
of those actions. The Self which thus claims
to be the doer is known as Г«PrakГҰtistha PuruВ¶aГӯ
(self rooted in PrakГҰti); it is only when it
arrogates to itself the doership of actions that
they are known by the name of Г«KarmaГӯ and
bear fruit. That is why the PrakГҰtistha PuruВ¶a
has to take birth in good and evil wombs and
to reap the consequences of those actions
(XIII.21). Therefore, the Г«KartГҘГӯ or agent,
mentioned in verse 14 above as one of the
five factors contributory to the accomplishment
of actions, is the PrakГҰtistha PuruВ¶a; whereas
the present verse speaks of the absolute, i.e.
unattached and untainted Self. Hence by
declaring it as the non-doer the Lord has
stated its real character. The fifth factor, viz.,
the agent, is no longer present in the actions
of the man who has realized the true nature
of the Self. That is why his actions cease to
be known as Г«KarmaГӯ. It is this very idea that
has been brought out in the next verse.
* вҖўвҖҡXГ”UГҠпЈҝ sГ”ГҝВў В¬ГҲLВ§В·вҖ”вҖ“
Denouncing the man who views the Self as the doer, in order to inculcate the truth that
the Self is absolutely untainted, immutable and a non-doer, the Lord now extols him who,
having realized the true nature of the Self, views it as a non-doer.

the least connection with the actions performed
by them or with the fruit of those actions. It,
therefore, follows that the actions that are
performed by his mind, intellect and senses
in the interest of the world-order and due to
the impulse of his PrГҘrabdha or destiny are
all in accord with the вҖәГҘstras, and beneficent
to all. For the feeling of Г«IГӯ and Г«mineГӯ,
attachment and selfishness having disappeared,
there remains no incentive to the perpetration
of sinful acts. Therefore, just as when any
living being meets its death due to its PrГҘrabdha
or destiny through the agency of fire, air and
water etc., neither are the latter held responsible
for the death of that living being nor are they
bound by that action, even so the exalted
soul mentioned above, while discharging his
sacred obligations in the eyes of the world, is
not held responsible for his deeds nor gets
bound by their fruit even if he carries out the
hard-hearted duties of a KВ¶atriyaГіexterminates
the whole creation according to the exigencies
of the momentГіmuch less when he performs
noble acts such as sacrifice, charity and
austerity. That is to say, even though he
performs all sorts of actions, he remains
absolutely free from their binding effect.
The idea is that just as God, even though
carrying on the functions of creation,
maintenance and destruction etc. of the whole
universe, is really speaking not the doer in
respect of these functions (IV.13), and has
The relative pronoun Г«YasyaГӯ in this
verse refers to the SГҘвҲҶkhyayogвҲӮ (the SГҘdhaka
treading the path of Knowledge) who views
all actions as a game of PrakГҰti. No longer
regarding the body as his Self, such a man
altogether ceases to be the doer. That is to
say, he no longer entertains to the least degree
the sense of doership or duty in respect of
any action whatsoever performed by the mind,
senses and the body. This is what is meant
by the words Г«Na AhaвҲҶkГҰtaВЁ BhГҘvaВЁГӯ.
The SГҘвҲҶkhyayogвҲӮ further ceases to
entertain the feeling of mineness, attachment
and desire in respect of actions and their fruit
in the shape of wife, progeny, wealth, house,
honour, fame, heavenly bliss and other objects
of this world and the next. He disclaims
connection with any action whatsoever or its
fruit; and inasmuch as he comes to realize all
actions and objects as momentary, perishable
and unreal like the actions and enjoyments of
a dream, they hardly leave any impression on
his mind. This is what is meant by the clause
Г«BuddhiВЁ na lipyateГӯ.
Thus it is clear that, inasmuch as the
SГҘвҲҶkhyayogвҲӮ comes to realize in the aforesaid
manner the essential character of the Self,
egotism or the feeling of Г«IГӯ, which is born of
ignorance, entirely disappears from his mind;
and since he no longer entertains the feeling
of Г«IГӯ and Г«mineГӯ in respect of the mind,
intellect, senses and body, he ceases to have

He whose mind is free from the sense of doership, and whose reason is not
affected by worldly objects and activities, does not really kill, even having killed all
these people, nor does any sin accrue to him.                                                          (17)

nothing to do with them (IV.14; IX.9), even

in keeping with his grade in society, stage in

strain on him inasmuch as it becomes a habit
with him.
Having thus established the non-doership of the Self in order to expound the truth of

from the point of view of SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga.

through which man comes to regard the

The first half of this verse represents the

=== PAGE 726 ===

* Chapter XVIII *
725
recognise the Self as distinct from the body
or all-pervading, is no knowledge in the real
acceptance of the term. Hence the Lord has
not even mentioned the word 'JВӨГҘna' in this

is really the same as ignorance.
The use of the adjective Г«AhaitukamГӯ
(irrational) with this reference to this knowledge
is intended to convey that a reasonable man
will hardly entertain such a view; even a man
of small understanding is able to distinguish
between the material body and the conscious
Self by exercising his judgement; hence such
a perverted knowledge cannot exist where
there is reason and a critical sense.
That which is perceived or apprehended
through this knowledge is not real; that is to
say, this knowledge does not represent an
object in its real character. Hence it is perverted
form of knowledge and very insignificant;
that is why it is worth discarding. This is
what is sought to be conveyed by the use of
the adjective Г«AtattvГҘrthavatГӯ and Г«AlpamГӯ.
Such is the perverted form of knowledge
or belief entertained by men of a grossly
TГҘmasika disposition. That is but natural to
them inasmuch as ignorance has been said to
be an offspring of TamoguЖ’a.
The Lord now characterizes SГҘttvika action.
ГҒЕёГҝГғВў 
вҖҡXГ”UвҒ„UГҒвҖһUГғв—ҠвҒ„UГҠВӘmпЈҝВ·ГғвҖ”

ГҒЕёГҝГғв—ҠЛңГ” is ordained (by the scriptures); вҖҡXГ”UвҒ„UГҒвҖһUГғв—ҠЛңГ” is not accompanied with the sense of
doership; вҖўвҒ„UГҠВӘmпЈҝВ·ГғвҖ”Г” without any attachment or aversion; вҲ‘ГҺВ§Гғв—ҠЛңГ” has been done; вҖўВ»В§вҲӮUВ¬ЛқпЈҝГҘвҖҡГҲЕёГҠ by

That action which is ordained by the scriptures and is not accompanied by the
sense of doership, and has been done without any attachment or aversion by one who
seeks no return, is called SГҘttvika.                                                        (23)
The word Г«SaвҲҶgaГӯ should, therefore, be
interpreted to mean the feeling of identification
that one develops with regard to oneГӯs actions
by 
claiming 
their 
doership. 
Hence
Г«SaвҲҶgarahitamГӯ should be taken to refer to
such actions as are performed without the
sense of doership and without identifying
oneself with oneГӯs body. Hence the use of
this adjective is intended to show that even
the aforesaid duties that have been enjoined
by the вҖәГҘstras are SГҘttvika only when they
are Г«SaвҲҶgarahitaГӯ, and not otherwise.
The compound adjective Г«Aphala-
prepsunГҘГӯ, stands for the unselfish man who,
having ceased to entertain the feeling of
mineness and attachment in respect of all
enjoyments of this world and the next, obtained
as a reward for oneГӯs actions, no longer
cherishes the least craving for those enjoyments,
Sacrifice, charity and austerity, vocational
duties and bodily functions and all other noble
acts enjoined by the scriptures as binding on
a particular man with due regard to his grade
in society, stage in life, temperament and
circumstances, are referred to here as Г«Niyatam
KarmaГӯ. The use of the participial adjective
Г«NiyatamГӯ is intended to convey that only such
obligatory duties as have been enjoined by
the scriptures, and are required to be performed
either daily or on special occasions, can be
SГҘttvika in character; those that have been
prohibited or are motivated by desire cannot
belong to this category.
The noun Г«SaвҲҶgaГӯ, forming part of the
compound adjective Г«SaвҲҶgarahitamГӯ, has not
been used in the sense of attachment in this
verse; for absence of attachment has been
included in the phrase Г«ArГҘgadveВ¶ataВЁ KГҰtamГӯ.

=== PAGE 727 ===

726
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
who does not seek to attain any selfish end
of his through any action whatsoever, who
does not need any object for himself.
The present verse shows that that action
alone is perfectly SГҘttvika, which answers all
the qualifications mentioned above. If any of
these characteristics is missing in a particular
action, its SГҘttvika character should be regarded
as wanting to that extent. Besides this, it
should also be taken to mean that JВӨГҘna or
realization follows from the quality of Sattva
and SГҘttvika actions alone; hence men who
seek to know the truth about God should
perform SГҘttvika actions alone, and should
not court bondage by performing RГҘjasika
and TГҘmasika actions.
Here it should be noted that the present
verse defines SГҘttvika action from the point
of view of SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga; hence it precludes
the sense of doership by the term
Г«SaвҲҶgarahitamГӯ and the feeling of attraction
and aversion too by the word Г«ArГҘgadveВ¶ataВЁГӯ.
In verse 9, however, SГҘttvika TyГҘga has
been declared to consist merely in giving up
attachment and the craving for fruit of actions,
performed from the point of view of
Karmayoga. Hence there is no mention of
the absence of doership in that verse; on the
other hand, it advises the performance of
actions as a matter of duty. Here lies the
difference between SГҘttvika Karma and
SГҘttvika TyГҘga as defined in the two verses.
God-realization through Knowledge of Truth
is the reward of both; hence there is no real
difference between the two, the difference
lies only in their mode of practice.
The Lord now defines RГҘjasika action.
ГҝГ»ГҠГҲ вҲ‘В§ГҠв—ҠпЈҝГҘвҖҡГҲЕёГҠ вҲ‘В§в—ҠВё вҖҡГҠвҖһUVГ”UГҠвҒ„пЈҝUГЎГҠ п¬ӮГҠ В¬ГҲЕёвҖ”вҖ“
ГҒвҲ‘ЛқВ§ГҝГғпЈҝ 
вҖҷвҖһГҲUвҲӮUГҠГҝГҠвҖҡВў 
ГғВәЛқГҠВЎвҖҡв—ҠГҲЕ’ГҠNUГғв—ҠЛңГ”H 24H
ГҝГғЛңГ” which; ГғГҲ but; вҲ‘В§ГҠв—ҠпЈҝГҘвҖҡГҲЕёГҠ by a man seeking enjoyments; вҲ‘В§в—ҠВё the action; вҖҡГҠвҖһUVГ”UГҠвҒ„пЈҝUГЎГҠ by him
who is full of egotism; п¬ӮГҠ or; В¬ГҲЕёвҖ” and; ГҒвҲ‘ЛқВ§ГҝГғпЈҝГ” is performed; вҖҷвҖһГҲUвҲӮUГҠГҝГҠвҖҡв—ҠЛңГ” involving much strain
or exertion; ГғГғЛңГ” that; вҒ„UГҠВЎвҖҡв—ҠЛңГ” RГҘjasika; В©UЕ’ГҠNUГғв—ҠЛңГ” has been spoken of.
That action, however, which involves much strain and is performed by one who
seeks enjoyments or by a man full of egotism, has been spoken of as RГҘjasika.  (24)
do not involve any strain. The doer of RГҘjasika
actions, however, does identify himself with
the body, hence the toil and hardships involved
in his actions make him unhappy; that is why
he feels the strain while performing every action.
Besides this, the doer of SГҘttvika actions confines
himself to those duties alone which fall to his
lot from the sacred or secular point of view;
hence he does not expand his activities. But
the doer of RГҘjasika actions continues to
undertake new enterprises every day, hence
the scope of his activities gets much enlarged.
That is another reason why acts of weary toil
have been declared as RГҘjasika.
The compound word Г«KГҘmepsunГҘГӯ stands
The words Г«BahulГҘyГҘsam KarmaГӯ refer
to those desire-born and worldly undertakings
for which many activities of diverse kinds
have been prescribed, and which due to the
feeling of identification with oneГӯs body one
accomplishes with great exertion and grudge
regarding them as a burden. The use of the
compound adjective Г«BahulГҘyГҘsamГӯ is intended
to distinguish the RГҘjasika from the SГҘttvika
type of actions. The idea is that the doer of
SГҘttvika actions does not identify himself with
his body and has no sense of doership in
respect of his actions; hence he does not feel
the least exertion or hardship in performing
any action whatsoever. Therefore, his actions

* Chapter XVIII *
727
for the self-centred man who ever goes on
craving for enjoyments of various kinds,
entertaining as he does the feeling of mineness
and attachment in respect of them, and who
does everything for the sake of enjoyments of
this world and the next, such as wife, progeny,
wealth, house, honour, fame, prestige etc.
The use of the disjunctive particle Г«VГҘГӯ
in this verse is intended to convey that not
only those actions which aim at the acquisition
of objects of enjoyment but even those which,
though not motivated by the desire for
enjoyments, are prompted by egotism are
RГҘjasika in character. The idea is that even
those actions which are tainted by either of
these two evils viz., the craving for enjoyment
and egotism, are RГҘjasika, much more those
which are characterized by both.
The word Г«SГҘhaвҲҶkГҘreЖ’aГӯ stands for the
man who not only identifies himself with the
body but all of whose actions are prompted
by egotism, nay, who claims the doership of
actions, regards himself as nonpareil and makes
much of his capabilities and is also given to
bragging.
It has already been stated that RГҘjasika
actions yield a crop of sorrow (XIV.16) and
that the quality of Rajas binds man through
attachment of actions (XIV.7); hence a seeker
of liberation should never perform such actions.
This is what is meant by calling the above
actions RГҘjasika.
The Lord now characterizes TГҘmasika action.

That action which is undertaken through sheer ignorance, without regard for the
consequences or loss to oneself, injury to others and oneГӯs own capacity, is declared as
TГҘmasika.
(25)
possesses the requisite capacity. A TГҘmasika
action is that which does not take into account
all these considerations and is undertaken in
a reckless spirit. This is what the Lord seeks
to convey in the present verse.
Inspired as it is by ignorance, which is
a product of TamoguЖ’a, such a rash act is
called TГҘmasika. And it has already been
stated (XIV.18) that a TГҘmasika action leads
to AjВӨГҘna, i.e., birth in the species of stupid
creatures, such as swine, dogs, plants etc.,
or condemnation to hell. Hence those seeking
beatitude should never undertake such
an act.
Before launching an enterprise a man
should exercise his own judgment and foresee
its consequences, viz., whether it will lead to
happiness or sorrow. Again, he should consider
what will it cost him in the shape of money,
bodily strength and time, to what extent it
will interfere with Dharma or piety and what
other losses it will entail. Thirdly, he should
foresee in what way and to what extent it
will cause hardship to other human beings or
creatures and involve destruction of human
or other lives. And, fourthly, he should form
an estimate of the capacity that will be required
for accomplishing it and consider whether he
The Lord now defines a KartГҘ or doer of the SГҘttvika type.

Free from attachment, unegotistic, endowed with firmness and zeal and unswayed
by success and failureГіsuch a doer is said to be SГҘttvika.                          (26)
has achieved success or like one who seeks
some reward for his actions. A man who is
endowed with both these virtues, namely,
firmness and zeal, does not shirk his duty
even when faced with the heaviest odds, but
diligently pursues it through thick and thin,
surmounting all difficulties. These constitute
the distinguishing marks of such a man.
Ordinary men of the world are overjoyed
on the completion of an undertaking to which
they feel attached and which they look upon
as a means to the realization of their desired
end; even so, they feel very miserable when
it is interrupted and left half finished. Similarly,
the success and failure of an undertaking
awaken morbid feelings of various other kinds
in their heart. Therefore, that man of an
unruffled temperament who, having no feeling
of Г«IГӯ and Г«mineГӯ, attachment and the desire

under every circumstance and at all times, is
called Г«SiddhyasiddhyoВЁ NirvikГҘraВЁГӯ.
He alone who possesses all the above
qualifications is a perfectly SГҘttvika doer; his
SГҘttvika nature will be found wanting to the
extent he lacks these qualifications. Such
SГҘttvika traits manifest the hidden light of
God, hence a seeker of liberation should
imbibe the virtues of a SГҘttvika doer.
Г«MuktasaвҲҶgaВЁГӯ is he who has ceased to
have any connection whatsoever with
actions and their fruit. That is to say, he
no longer entertains the least feeling of
mineness, attachment and desire in respect of
whatever is done by the mind, senses and
body, or with regard to its fruit in the shape
of honour, fame, prestige, wife, progeny,
wealth, house and other enjoyments of this
world and the next.
Again, Г«AnahaвүҲvГҘdвҲӮГӯ is he who, having
ceased to identify himself with the mind,
intellect, senses and body, which are other
than the Self, does not claim the doership in
respect of any of his actions. He, therefore,
never boasts, like men possessing a demoniac
disposition, of his having realized a particular
end, nor advertises his future designs. He
does not claim to be Г¬the lord, the enjoyer, a
mighty and happy man, having no compeerГ®
nor proclaims his intention to perform sacrifices
and bestow gifts (XVI.13-15) but talks in an
unaffected and unegotistic strain.
He who does not lose his balance
of mind even when confronted with the
greatest obstacles and difficulties while
discharging any sacred obligation is called
Г«DhГҰtisamanvitaВЁГӯ; while Г«UtsГҘhasamanvitaВЁГӯ

The doer who is full of attachment, seeks the fruit of actions and is greedy, who
is oppressive by nature and of impure conduct, and is affected by joy and sorrow, has
been called RГҘjasika.

continues to oppress others for the sake of

Г«AyuktaВЁГӯ is he who has not been able
to subdue his mind and senses, but on the
other hand, who is under the control of his
mind and senses, and who is lacking in faith
and piety.
He who is naturally dull-witted and has
not received good education, who possesses
a childish nature, who has no knowledge of
his duty (XVI.7) and whose mind and senses
have not been improved by culture, is referred
