The arrogant man who is very
hardhearted by nature, who has no element
of humility in him, and who is ever intoxicated
with pride, is called Г«StabdhaВЁГӯ.
The crafty swindler who secretly harms
others, concealing his malicious intentions,
and is ever contriving plans to do them an ill
turn, is referred to here as Г«вҖәaВўhaВЁГӯ.
Г«NaiВ¶kГҰtikaВЁГӯ is he who deprives others
of their means of subsistence.
The slothful man who is wont to remain
lying down all the twenty-four hours, who
does not feel inclined or encouraged to perform
any sacred or secular duty, and whose mind
and senses are ever steeped in indolence, is
spoken of here as Г«AlasaВЁГӯ.
The down-hearted man who grieves day
and night and whose worries know no end
(XVI.11) is called Г«ViВ¶ГҘdвҲӮГӯ.
And he who having taken up some
work does not finish it for a long time,Гіwho
goes on putting it off from day to day, so that
a work which could be finished in one day
takes many days and is yet left unfinished,
such a man of sluggish habits is called
Г«DвҲӮrghasВөutrвҲӮГӯ.
The evil traits mentioned in the above
verse are all products of TamoguЖ’a; hence
whoever possesses all these characteristics or
many of them should be regarded as a doer
of the TГҘmasika type. And men of a TГҘmasika
disposition fall in the scale of spiritual evolution
(XIV.18); they are born in sub-human species
of various kinds, such as beasts and birds,
insects and moths etc. (XIV.15). Hence a
man seeking beatitude should not allow any
of these characteristics to stay in him.
Having thus classified, JВӨГҘna, Karma and KartГҘ in order of sequence out of the factors
which motivate action as well as the constituents of action in order that the SГҘttvika traits,
which are helpful in the realization of Truth, may be imbibed and the RГҘjasika and
TГҘmasika propensities, which are prejudicial to such realization, may be discarded, the
Lord now undertakes to classify in their order of sequence Buddhi and DhГҰti under three
heads, viz., SГҘttvika, RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika.
вҖҷГҲhпЈҝГ·пЈҝВёЕ’Вў Е“ГҺГғпЈҝpГ’п¬Ӯ ВӘГҲГЎГҠГғГҒSГІГҠГҒп¬ӮЕ“Вў oГҺГЎГҠГҲвҖ“

* Chapter XVIII *
731
The word Г«BuddhiГӯ in this verse denotes
the faculty of determination; it is also called
Г«AntaВЁkaraЖ’aГӯ or the internal organ. The JВӨГҘna
or knowledge which has been classified under
three heads in verses 20, 21 and 22 is a
product or function of Buddhi, which is the
source of it. Verse 18 mentions Г«JВӨГҘnaГӯ as
one of the factors that motivate action; while
Buddhi has been included in the constituents
of action under the name of Г«KaraЖ’aГӯ or
instrument.
This constitutes the difference between
Buddhi and JВӨГҘna. The following verses
classify Buddhi which is the chief KaraЖ’a or
instrument, in order to bring out clearly the
threefold division of KaraЖ’as, which form
part of the constituents of action.
Г«DhГҰtiГӯ is the faculty of holding on to a
particular pursuit, belief or idea; it is also a
function of the intellect. It is through this
faculty that a man firmly adheres to a particular
activity or idea. Hence it is included in the
category of Г«KaraЖ’aГӯ. The word occurs in
verse 26 above among the characteristics of
a SГҘttvika doer; this is likely to give the
impression that DhГҰti is SГҘttvika only. But
the truth is otherwise; DhГҰti also is of three
kinds. It is in order to bring out this truth that
Г«DhГҰtiГӯ has been classified under three heads
in the following verses.
The obvious intention of the Lord in
classifying both Buddhi and DhГҰti under three
heads is to point out that only the SГҘttvika
types of these are worth cultivating, while
the RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika types are to be
given up.
In keeping with His promise to discuss the threefold divisions of Buddhi and DhГҰti, the
Lord first of all defines SГҘttvika Buddhi.
В¬Лқп¬ӮГҺГҒГ»ГҠВў Гё ГҒЕёп¬ӮГҺГҒГ»ГҠВў Гё вҲ‘В§ГҠГҝГҠВёвҲ‘В§ГҠГҝпЈҝВё Г·ГҝГҠГ·ГҝпЈҝвҖ“
вҖҷГЈЕ“Вў в—ҠГҠпЈҝЛҮГҠВў Гё ГҝГҠ п¬ӮпЈҝГҒГ»ГҠ вҖҷГҲГҒhвҖ” вҖҡГҠ В¬ГҠГ•Вё вҖҡГҠГҒГ»п¬ӮвҲ‘В§ГӢH 30H

The intellect which correctly determines the paths of activity and renunciation,
what ought to be done and what should not be done, what is fear and what is
fearlessness, and what is bondage and what is liberation, that intellect is Г«SГҘttvikaГӯ. (30)
the craving for reward. This is known as the
path of activity. And right knowledge of this
path consists in forming a correct idea about
the same and following it according to the
ideals of the ancient kings Janaka and AmbarвҲӮВ¶a
and that of great sages VasiВ¶Вўha, YГҘjВӨavalkya
and so on.
The other path is the path of renunciation.
It consists in completely abstaining from all
activities and enjoyments both externally and
internally and remaining constantly engaged
in hearing, reflection and contemplation or in
The way to God-realization is twofold.
The first consists in practising worship of
God, performing noble deeds enjoined by
the scriptures such as sacrifice, charity and
austerity, vocational duties consistent with
oneГӯs grade in society and stage in life, and

the feeling of Г«IГӯ and Г«mineГӯ, attachment and

=== PAGE 733 ===

732
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
The compulsion under which a JвҲӮva or
embodied soul has to go through an unending
series of births and deaths from time without
beginning due to its connection with good or
evil actions is called Г«BandhaГӯ or bondage.
And when all the ties of good and evil
Karma that bind a soul are cut asunder by the
grace of God through the practice of
Karmayoga, Bhaktiyoga, JВӨГҘnayoga or any
other discipline undertaken as a result of
association with holy men, and the soul attains
God-realization, it is said to have achieved
MokВ¶a or liberation.
The correct knowledge of Bandha
consists in grasping the true nature of bondage
as defined above and ascertaining the causes
which have thrown the JвҲӮva into bondage, as
well as the factors which go to tighten this
bond and so on; while true knowledge of
MokВ¶a consists in correctly apprehending what
is meant by securing freedom from this
bondage and discovering how and by what
means it can be secured.
To sum up, the SГҘttvika type of intellect
is that which is capable of correctly determining
all these points, which neither errs nor wavers
in its judgment on any of these issuesГі
which pronounces its correct verdict on
whatever point it is sought. The SГҘttvika type
of intellect frees man from the bondage of
mundane existence and leads to the attainment
of the supreme state; hence a seeker of
blessedness should improve his intellect by
cultivation of the above traits and make it
SГҘttvika.
the adoration and remembrance of God and
chanting His names etc., alongwith the practice
of control of mind and the senses, endurance
etc., while staying in the fourth Г…вҖ№rama of
SannyГҘsa and remaining aloof from all kinds
of worldly distractions. Again, all this has to
be done for the sake of God-realization and
renouncing the feelings of Г«IГӯ and Г«mineГӯ as
well as attachment. And right knowledge of
this path consists in forming a correct notion
of it and following it according to the ideals
of Sanaka and his three brothers, NГҘrada,
Lord В§RВ¶abhadeva and вҖәukadeva.
Г«KГҘryaГӯ is that which a man ought to do
at a particular place and on a particular occasion
considering his grade in society, stage in life,
temperament and circumstances; and that which
should be shunned by him on a particular
occasion is Г«AkГҘryaГӯ for him. Right knowledge
of Г«KГҘryaГӯ and Г«AkГҘryaГӯ, therefore, consists in
correctly determining whether an action, one
is called upon to perform, is worth performing
or whether one should abstain from it.
The nervous excitement caused in oneГӯs
mind by the presence of some disagreeable
object or occurrence or by the apprehension
of such an occurrence is called Г«BhayaГӯ or
fear; and the absence of such a feeling is
called Г«AbhayaГӯ or fearlessness. Right
knowledge of Bhaya and Abhaya, therefore,
consists in shaking off fear by knowing the
truth of both, i.e., ascertaining the causes
which go to make one feel nervous and the
way how to overcome this feeling and attain
the state of fearlessness.
The Lord now defines the RГҘjasika type of intellect.

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
вҖәГҘstras as worth performing for a man at a
particular time and place with due regard to
his grade in society, stage in life, temperament
and circumstances, is KГҘrya for him; while
that which has been declared in the вҖәГҘstras
as not only worth abstaining from or forbidden
but as something which must not be performed
in any case, is AkГҘrya. Whereas sinful acts
prohibited by the вҖәГҘstras are worth abstaining
from for all, even out of noble acts enjoined
by the scriptures some are worth performing
for a particular class while others are worth
abstaining from for that very class. For example,
while service is worth performing for a вҖәВөudra
(a member of the labouring class) the
performance of sacrifice and study of Vedas
etc., are worth shunning for him; while the
practice of Viveka (discrimination between
real and unreal, eternal and evanescent, matter
and spirit etc.), VairГҘgya (dispassion), control
of the mind and senses and so on are worth
performing for a recluse, the performance of
sacrifices and practice of charity etc., are worth
shunning for him; performing sacrifices and
officiating at them, bestowing and accepting
gifts, and the study and teaching of the Vedas
are worth performing, while service as a means
of subsistence is worth abstaining from for
the BrГҘhmaЖ’a (a member of the priestly class);
even so, for VaiвҖ№ya (a member of the trading
class) agriculture, rearing of cows and trade
etc., are worth pursuing, while accepting gifts
is worth abstaining from. This proves that a
particular duty is not worth performing for all
simply because it is included in the category
of Dharma enjoined by the scriptures. In this
way, that which is Dharma can be worth
performing and worth shunning too. Here lies
the difference between Dharma and KГҘrya,
on the one hand, and between Adharma and
AkГҘrya, on the other. The intellect which is
at a loss to judge whether a particular course
of action, which a man is required to choose
or reject, is worth adopting or abstaining
from, and to decide what he should do and
how and what he should not, is said not to
perceive correctly what is KГҘrya and what is
AkГҘrya.
Such an intellect, which is unable to
determine the real nature of things, is never
established in Viveka and remains distracted
and fickle due to its contact with RajoguЖ’a;
hence it is RГҘjasika. And a RГҘjasika tendency
is said to result in sorrow; hence he who
seeks blessedness should eliminate the RГҘjasika
traits present in oneГӯs intellect through
association with saints, the study of good
books and cherishing noble ideas, and at
the same time strive to awaken and foster
SГҘttvika impulses.
ГҝГ•ГҠ Sп¬ӮГҖВ¬в—ҠЕ“в—ҠZ ГҒвҖһU ВЎЕёГҝпЈҝГ»ГҠГҲ в—ҠвҖһUГҠГ·Гҝв—ҠЛңГ” Sп¬ӮГҖВ¬в—ҠГҘГҝSГҝ Е“в—ҠВёSГҝ ГІГҠГҠГҝГғпЈҝ в—ҠвҖһUГғГҠпЈҝ Г·ГҝГҠГғЛңГ”H
(Ibid., PВөurvakhaЖ’Е’a, I.47)
Г¬Just as a little deviation from virtue is fraught with great danger, even so, a little practice of this
Dharma protects from great fear.Г®
Side by side with this Dharma, consisting of four limbs, a man should perform duties consistent

вҖўЕ“в—ҠZ Е“в—ҠВёГҒв—ҠГҒГғ ГҝГҠ в—ҠГЈГҝГғпЈҝ Гғв—ҠвҖҡГҠп¬ӮГҺГғГҠвҖ“

and sees all other things pervertedly, that intellect is TГҘmasika, Arjuna.

implied by the first half of this verse, which
can be interpreted the other way as well by
treating Г«DharmaГӯ as the object and
Г«AdharmamГӯ as a complement.
Just as this intellect imagines Adharma
to be Dharma, even so it mistakes sorrow for
joy, the evanescent for the eternal, the impure
for the pure, loss for gain, and so on. All
these instances of a distorted vision are included
in seeing all things pervertedly.
In other words, a TГҘmasika intellect is
that which has almost lost its critical faculty,
so that its verdict in all matters is warped.
This type of intellect brings down the human
soul in the scale of spiritual evolution; hence
those who seek beatitude should discard such
an intellect.
The intellect which regards denunciation
of God and other deities; defiance of the
authority of the scriptures, disrespect towards
oneГӯs parents, preceptor and others, violation
of the rules of VarЖ’a and Г…вҖ№rama, discontent,
hypocrisy, duplicity, adultery, lying, oppressing
others, taking prohibited food, acting as one
pleases, usurpation of othersГӯ rights and other
prohibited acts of a sinful nature as acts of
virtue is said to mistake Adharma for Dharma.
Such an intellect further regards fortitude,
forbearance, control of the mind and senses,
non-thieving, purity, discernment, right
knowledge, truthfulness, absence of anger,
worship of God and other deities, study of
sacred books, following the rules of VarЖ’a
and Г…вҖ№rama, carrying out the behests of
oneГӯs parents and other superiors, artlessness,
continence, taking SГҘttvika food, non-violence,
The Lord now defines SГҘttvika DhГҰti.
Е“ГҺГ ГҝГҠ ГҝГҝГҠ Е“ГҠвҒ„UГҝГғпЈҝ в—ҠЕёвҖ”В¬ЛқГҠГЎГҠпЈҝГҒГЈВәЛқГҝГҒвҲ‘ЛқВ§ГҝГҠвҖ”вҖ“
ГҝГҠпЈҝВӘпЈҝЕёГҠвҲҡГҝГҒГ·ГёГҠГҒвҒ„UГЎГҝГҠ Е“ГҺГҒГғвҖ” вҖҡГҠ В¬ГҠГ•Вё вҖҡГҠГҒГ»п¬ӮвҲ‘В§ГӢH 33H
Е“ГҺГ ГҝГҠ firmness; ГҝГҝГҠ by which; Е“ГҠвҒ„UГҝГғпЈҝ (man) controls; в—ҠЕёвҖ”В¬ЛқГҠГЎГҠпЈҝГҒГЈВәЛқГҝГҒвҲ‘ЛқВ§ГҝГҠвҖ” the functions of the
mind, the vital airs and senses; ГҝГҠпЈҝВӘпЈҝЕё through the Yoga of meditation; вҖўвҲҡГҝГҒГ·ГёГҠГҒвҒ„UГЎГҝГҠ
unwavering; Е“ГҺГҒГғвҖ” firmness; вҖҡГҠ that; В¬ГҠГ•Вё O son of PГҰthГҘ (KuntвҲӮ), Arjuna; вҖҡГҠГҒГ»п¬ӮвҲ‘В§ГӢ (is) SГҘttvika.
The unwavering firmness by which man controls through the Yoga of meditation
the functions of the mind, the vital airs and the sensesГіthat firmness, Arjuna, is
SГҘttvika.                                                                                     (33)
The faculty of resolutely maintaining or
keeping up any activity, feeling or idea,Гі
sustained by which an activity, feeling or
idea does not languish but endures for a long
time,Гіsuch a faculty is called Г«DhГҰtiГӯ or
firmness. But so long as man continues with
the help of this firmness to clutch various
objects with different motives, its inconstancy
or fickleness does not cease. When, however,
man sets before him a fixed goal, the DhГҰti
becomes constant. SГҘttvika DhГҰti has only
one object, the realization of God. That is
why it is called Г«AvyabhicГҘriЖ’вҲӮГӯ.
The idea is that the DhГҰti which remains
fixed on one and only one object, viz., God-
realization, which never deviates from its
purpose, which has no diverse motives and
by which man keeps his mind and senses
etc., directed towards God for the sake of
His realization and ever maintains his control
over them, allowing them on no account to
waver and get attached to sense-objects,Гі

(34)
The Lord now defines TГҘmasika DhГҰti.

The firmness by which an evil-minded person does not give up sleep, fear, anxiety,
sorrow and vanity as well, that firmness is TГҘmasika.

of no other quality than RajoguЖ’a hence it

Just as the Lord classified JВӨГҘna, Karma,

Having thus classified Buddhi and DhГҰti under three heads, viz., SГҘttvika, RГҘjasika and
TГҘmasika, in order of sequence, in order that the SГҘttvika types of both may be chosen and
the other two types of each, viz., RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika, may be eschewed, the Lord now

SГҘttvika joy.

Now hear from Me the threefold joy too. That in which the SГҘdhaka (aspirant)
finds enjoyments through the practice of adoration, meditation and service to God etc.,
and whereby he reaches the end of sorrow,Гіsuch a joy, though appearing as poison in
the beginning, tastes like nectar in the end; hence that joy, born as it is of the placidity
of mind, brought about by meditation on God, has been declared as SГҘttvika.   (36-37)
TГҘmasika propensities such as carelessness

This TГҘmasika DhГҰti is a source of evil

738
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
(distinguishing the real from the unreal, the
eternal from the ephemeral and so on),
VairГҘgya (dispassion), control of the mind and
senses and endurance etc., as a means to the
attainment of SГҘttvika joy on hearing its glory.
It is a matter of common experience that on
hearing from his elders the glory of learning,
a boy tries to pursue his studies, but having
no real experience of its glory, he finds it most
unpleasant and difficult in the beginning to
prosecute his studies to the exclusion of play
and recreation. Even so, the man who strives
for the attainment of SГҘttvika joy finds it
extremely taxing and irksome to continue the
practice of Viveka, VairГҘgya, вҖәama, Dama,
TitikВ¶ГҘ and so on, abstaining from sense-
enjoyments. This is what is meant by the Lord
when He speaks of SГҘttvika joy as appearing
like poison in the beginning.
As a result of continued practice when
the SГҘdhaka begins to experience the joy
derived from meditation, it tastes like nectar to
him; at that time all worldly enjoyments begin
to appear insignificant, negligible and
disagreeable to him. This is what is sought to
be conveyed by the words Г«PariЖ’ГҘme
amГҰtopamamГӯ.
The SГҘttvika joy referred to above is
experienced only when the mind gets purified
through constant practice of meditation on
God. This is what is meant by the compound
adjective 
Г«Г…tmabuddhiprasГҘdajamГӯ. 
By
calling it SГҘttvika the Lord means that this
type of joy alone is joy par excellence, the
RГҘjasika and TГҘmasika types of joy being no
joy at all in the real sense of the term. They
are joy in name only, eventually they are the
same as sorrow; hence a man seeking his
redemption should constantly find enjoyment
in SГҘttvika joy alone, and not allow himself
to be caught into the trap of RГҘjasika and
TГҘmasika joys.
The Lord now defines RГҘjasika joy.
ГҒп¬ӮВ·ГҝпЈҝГҒГЈВәЛқГҝвҖҡВўГҝГҠпЈҝВӘГҠlГ»ГҠЕ’ВӘЛқпЈҝЛҳв—ҠГҺГғГҠпЈҝВ¬в—Ҡв—ҠЛңГ” 
вҖ“
В¬ГҒвҒ„UГЎГҠГҠв—ҠпЈҝ ГҒп¬ӮВ·ГҒв—Ҡп¬Ӯ ГғГ вҖҡГҲПҖВў вҒ„UГҠВЎвҖҡВў Sв—ҠГҺГғв—ҠЛңГ”H 38H
ГҒп¬ӮВ·ГҝпЈҝГҒГЈВәЛқГҝвҖҡВўГҝГҠпЈҝВӘГҠГғЛңГ” (proceeds) from the contact of the senses with their objects; ГҝГғЛңГ” which;
ГғГғЛңГ” that (joy); вҖўВӘЛқпЈҝ at first, at the time of enjoyment; вҖўв—ҠГҺГғГҠпЈҝВ¬в—Ҡв—ҠЛңГ” (even though appearing) like
nectar; В¬ГҒвҒ„UГЎГҠГҠв—ҠпЈҝ in the end; ГҒп¬ӮВ·в—ҠЛң Гҹп¬Ӯ (is) like poison; ГғГғЛңГ” that; вҖҡГҲПҖв—ҠЛңГ” the delight; вҒ„UГҠВЎвҖҡв—ҠЛңГ” (as)
RГҘjasika; Sв—ҠГҺГғв—ҠЛңГ” has been spoken of.
The delight which follows from the contact of the senses with their objects is
eventually poison-like, though appearing at first as nectar; hence it has been spoken
of as RГҘjasika.                                                                             (38)
The indeclinable Г«AgreГӯ in this verse
refers to the time when a man begins to
enjoy some object with his senses and mind
for the sake of attaining RГҘjasika joy. A man
experiences such delight only so long as he
enjoys some object with his senses and mind;
this is what is meant by the compound word
Г«ViВ¶ayendriyasaвүҲyogГҘtГӯ. And due to his
attachment for the same the joy appears
exceedingly agreeable to him; in the face of
such a joy he cares a straw for any unseen
joy. This is what is meant by the words
Г«Agre amГҰtopamamГӯ.
Like poison, enjoyment of this RГҘjasika
delight is fraught with bitter consequences. This
type of joy is joy only in appearance, it is no
joy in the real sense of the term. The idea is
that as man fondly enjoys worldly pleasures
with his senses and mind, regarding them as
joy itself, impressions of those enjoyments are
left on the mind, due to which he seeks once
more to obtain those very enjoyments; and with

=== PAGE 740 ===

* Chapter XVIII *
739
that end in view he impulsively perpetrates
sins of various kinds through attachment. And
in order to reap the evil consequences of such
sinful acts he has to be reborn in subhuman
species like insects, moths, beasts and birds
etc., and to undergo terrible suffering in hells
abounding in tortures.
With the growth of attachment for sense-
objects, failure to obtain them again fills a man
with grief and the absence of those objects too
makes him extremely miserable. Seeing others
more happy and prosperous than himself he is
filled with jealousy; and the loss of strength,
vitality, energy, intelligence and valour as well
as the feeling of exhaustion following in the
wake of enjoyment are very painful. Similarly
there are many other bitter consequences
too. Therefore, even though this momentary
pleasure derived from the contact of senses
with their objects is really speaking disagreeable
in every way, nevertheless, a sensual man
ignorantly and fondly enjoys pleasures
regardless of consequences and, taking them
to be joy itself, undergoes terrible suffering of
various kinds and in numerous ways as a
result thereof. His case is similar to that of an
ailing man who takes an unwholesome diet
due to attachment and love of flavour regardless
of consequences, and suffers in consequence
due to aggravation of his malady or meets
with death. Or his case may be compared to
that of a moth, which on account of its attachment
to colour, the object of sight, finds pleasure in
deliberately dashing against the flame of a
lamp, taking it to be an embodiment of joy,
but suffers in the end by getting singed and
dies.
This momentary pleasure appears as
agreeable to us only because of our attachment
to sense-objects, and attachment is a form of
RajoguЖ’a; hence it is RГҘjasika and binds
man through attachment. Therefore, a seeker
of beatitude should not allow himself to be
enticed into the trap of this type of delight.
The Lord now characterizes TГҘmasika delight.
ГҝЕ’ВӘЛқпЈҝ ГёГҠЕёГҲвҖҷГЈЕ“пЈҝ Гё вҖҡГҲПҖВў в—ҠГҠпЈҝвҖһUЕёв—ҠГҠГ в—ҠЕёвҖ”вҖ“
ГҒЕёВәЛқГҠвҲӮUSГҝВ¬Лқв—ҠГҠЕ’ГҠпЈҝГ Г•Вў 
ГғГ»ГҠГҠв—ҠвҖҡв—ҠГҲЕ’ГҠNUГғв—ҠЛңГ”H 39H
ГҝГғЛңГ” which; вҖўВӘЛқпЈҝ during enjoyment; Гё and; вҖўЕёГҲвҖҷГЈЕ“пЈҝ in the end; Гё as well; вҖҡГҲПҖв—ҠЛңГ” the delight;

That which stupefies the self during its enjoyment as well as in the end,Гіderived
from sleep, indolence and obstinate error, such delight has been called TГҘmasika. (39)
mind and senses as well as the Spirit, which
identifies itself with them. And due to
attachment for this joy, man has to be reborn
in the end in the species of stupid and inanimate
creatures such as trees, mountains etc., hence
this joy is said to stupefy the Self even in the
end.
Similarly, the sense of comfort that one
feels on giving up exertion of the mind,
senses and body while one remains lying,
abstaining from all forms of activity, is referred
to as Г«Г…lasyottha SukhaГӯ or joy derived from
During sleep the function of our mind
and senses is suspended; this relieves us of
the suffering caused by exhaustion and gives
rest to these organs. The joy that we experience
thereby is referred to here as Г«Nidrottha SukhaГӯ
or joy derived from sleep. This joy lasts only
so long as we are asleep, it is anything but
constant; hence it is momentary or ephemeral.
Besides this, the mind, intellect and senses
lose their perspicacity, they are no longer
capable of perceiving anything. In this way
this joy is said to stupefy the self, viz., the

740
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
indolence. Like the joy derived from sleep,
this joy also robs the mind and senses of their

enjoyment; and inasmuch as it leads to rebirth
in the species of stupid creatures as a result
of infatuation and attachment, it stupefies the
Self even in the end.
Idle 
pursuits 
undertaken 
through
attachment for the sake of diversion and
foolish and deliberate neglect of dutyГіthis is
called Г«PramГҘdaГӯ or obstinate error. And the
delight that one ignorantly finds in taking to
idle pursuits because of the amusement they
afford, and in neglecting oneГӯs duty because
one is spared exertion thereby is Г«PramГҘdottha
SukhaГӯ or joy derived from obstinate error.
During the time a man is engaged in some
frivolous pursuit by way of recreation, he
fails to perceive what he should do and what
he should not; his faculty of judgment gets
obscured through ignorance. And it is only
when a manГӯs judgment gets clouded that he
neglects his duty. In this way this joy derived
from obstinate error stupefies the self during
its enjoyment. And in order to reap the
consequences of lying, duplicity, violence
and other sinful acts that one is liable to
commit due to ignorance and attachment in
course of such idle pursuits, as well as of the
neglect of duty involved therein, one is reborn
in vile species like swine and dogs and cast
into hells. In this way it stupefies the soul
even in the end.
Sleep, error and slothГіall these three
are products of TamoguЖ’a (XIV.17); hence
the joy that is derived from these is TГҘmasika
in character. And it is by depicting these as
agreeable that TamoguЖ’a binds the human
soul (XIV.8); hence a man seeking beatitude
should not allow himself to be caught in the
trap of this TГҘmasika joy, which is momentary,
stupefying and only apparent.
any other part of the universe.
The word Г«SattvamГӯ here denotes

three GuЖ’as born of PrakГҰti, viz., Sattva,

for the birth of living beings in various species

* Chapter XVIII *
741
in the middle region and in the celestial and
all other worlds, there is no object or living
being free from or beyond these three GuЖ’as.
For while all material creation is undeniably
for the same essence as the GuЖ’as, being a
product of the same, all living beings are
connected with those GuЖ’as as well as with
the objects which are their products; hence
they too are  characterized by the three GuЖ’as.
Here it may be urged that those who
have risen above the three GuЖ’as are also
comprised in GodГӯs creation; how, then, can
it be said that no living being in this universe
is free from the GuЖ’as ? In this connection it
may be submitted that even though in the
eyes of the world the GuЖ’ГҘtвҲӮta (he who has
risen above the three GuЖ’as) forms part of
the universe, really speaking, the creation
does not exist in his eyes nor does he abide
in GodГӯs creation or in the body for the
matter of that; it is in God alone that he lives
in eternal union with Him, hence he is identical
in essence with God. That is why he cannot
be counted among ordinary creatures. Of
course, if he is called a living being from the
point of view of his body, which is a
conglomeration of mind, intellect and senses
etc., and is perceptible to all, we see no
objection to it; for that conglomeration is
nothing but a product of the GuЖ’as, hence
how can it be declared as existing beyond the
GuЖ’as ? Thus it can be safely asserted that no
living being or object in this creation is free
from the three GuЖ’as.
In the opening verse of this chapter Arjuna sought enlightenment on the truth about
SaвүҲnyГҘsa and TyГҘga separately; hence with a view to expounding the truth of both, the
Lord first of all cited the opinion of various thinkers on the subject and then gave His own
definitions of TyГҘga (Renunciation) and a TyГҘgвҲӮ (men of renunciation) in verses 4 to 12
above. Thereafter, discussing the true conception of SaвүҲnyГҘsa (SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga) in verses 13
to 17, He classified in verses 18 to 40 principal categories such as JВӨГҘna, Karma and KartГҘ
etc., according to the predominance of the three GuЖ’as in order that one may adopt
SattvaguЖ’a which is helpful to the practice of SaвүҲnyГҘsa, and reject Rajas and Tamas, which
are prejudicial to the same, and wound up the topic by declaring in the end the entire
creation as characterized by the three GuЖ’as.
While discussing the nature of TyГҘga in the introductory verses of this chapter, the Lord
stated that it was not advisable to shirk oneГӯs prescribed duty (XVIII.7); on the other hand,
He declared real TyГҘga to consist in continuing to perform oneГӯs allotted duties while
renouncing attachment and the desire for fruit (XVIII.9). But in that context He did not
mention what duty is prescribed for whom. Therefore, in order to discuss in brief the true
nature of prescribed duties, the part played by Bhakti or Devotion in Karmayoga discussed
under the name of TyГҘga and to show that its practice ultimately leads to the attainment of
highest perfection, the Lord resumes discussion of the topic of Karmayoga under the name
of TyГҘga and undertakes to point out the natural duties allotted to the BrГҘhmaЖ’a, the
KВ¶atriya, the VaiвҖ№ya and the вҖәВөudra.
вҖҷЛқГҠrГҠГ”ГЎГҠЛҮГҠГҒГІГҠГҝГҒп¬ӮвүҘГҠГҠВў вүҘГҠГҚВәЛқГҠГЎГҠГҠВў Гё В¬вҒ„UГЈГғВ¬вҖ“

The duties of the BrГҘhmaЖ’as, the KВ¶atriyas and the VaiвҖ№yas, as well as of the
вҖәВөudras, have been divided according to their inborn qualities, Arjuna.              (41)
it is in accordance with the manifestation of
these GuЖ’as that men are born in different
castes such as the BrГҘhmaЖ’as and so on.
Therefore, it is on the basis of these GuЖ’as
that the duties of the four VarЖ’as or grades of
society have been apportioned by the вҖәГҘstras.
The man who possesses an abundance of
unmixed Sattva is born as a BrГҘhmaЖ’a; hence
control of the mind and senses etc., have been
declared to be his natural duties or functions.
He whose nature is marked by an abundance
of Rajas mixed with Sattva is born as a KВ¶atriya;
hence exhibition of valour and interpidity have
been declared as his natural duties. He whose
nature possesses an abundance of Rajas
blended with Tamas is born as a VaiвҖ№ya; hence
agriculture and rearing of cows etc., have been
declared to be his natural avocation. And he
whose nature is dominated by Tamas mixed
with Rajas is born as a вҖәВөudra; hence service
of the other three classes has been declared
his natural duty. This very idea has been
explained at length in the commentary on verse
13 of Chapter IV.
The BrГҘhmaЖ’a, the KВ¶atriya and the
VaiвҖ№yaГіall these three belong to the category
of Dvijas or the twice-born. All the three are
entitled to wear the sacred thread (YajВӨopavвҲӮta)
and to study the Vedas and perform Vedic
rites such as sacrifices and so on. That is
why the three words Г«BrГҘhmaЖ’aГӯ, Г«KВ¶atriyaГӯ
and Г«ViвҖ№aГӯ have been compounded. The вҖәВөudras
are not included among the Dvijas and are
not competent to wear the sacred thread,
study the Vedas or perform sacrifices and
other Vedic rites. It is in order to bring out
this distinction that the word Г«вҖәВөudrГҘЖ’ГҘmГӯ has
been separately used.

of

or

of living beings. This is what is sought to be

Г«SvabhГҘvaprabhavaiВЁГӯ before Г«GuЖ’aiВЁГӯ. Again,
According to the introduction given in the preceding verse the Lord first mentions the

* Once upon a time king ViвҖ№vГҘmitra, son of GГҘdhi, accidentally arrived at the hermitage of the great
sage VasiВ¶Вўha. He was followed by a huge army. Through the grace of NandinвҲӮ, a celestial cow yielding all
desires, VasiВ¶Вўha treated the monarch alongwith his army to dishes of various kinds and gave him a present
of jewels, wearing apparel and ornaments. The wonderful cow caught the imagination of ViвҖ№vГҘmitra, who
asked VasiВ¶Вўha for the same. VasiВ¶Вўha refused to part with the cow on the plea that she had been kept by him
for the service of gods, the manes and newcomers, as well as for sacrificial purposes. ViвҖ№vГҘmitra was proud
of his strength in men and arms, he wanted to take her away by force. NandinвҲӮ approached VasiВ¶Вўha and
piteously told him that the hard-hearted men of the royal army mercilessly belaboured her with whips and
cudgels and wondered how he had shut his eyes to their tyranny. VasiВ¶Вўha gave her the following reply:Гі

Thereupon NandinвҲӮ assumed a terrible aspect, her tail began to rain fire; thereafter, a host of barbarous
tribes sprang up from her tail. The army of ViвҖ№vГҘmitra was utterly confounded. NandinвҲӮГӯs host did not kill
a single member of the royal army, they all fled in terror. ViвҖ№vГҘmitra saw none who could protect him. Then

is real strength.Г®

Г¬Having attained the highest position of a BrГҘhmaЖ’a, the pious sage ViвҖ№vГҘmitra too offered his
homage to the BrГҘhmaЖ’a sage VasiВ¶Вўha, the best of those who mutter sacred formulas.

This is what is meant by declaring them as
the natural duties of a BrГҘhmaЖ’a. They,
however, include some common duties as well.
This should be taken to mean that although
they are not the natural duties of KВ¶atriyas
and others, yet they can be performed by them
with some effort inasmuch as God-realization
is open to all and sundry.
The duties mentioned above are
purely SГҘttvika in character, hence they are
particularly congenial to the temperament of
a BrГҘhmaЖ’a; that is why the Lord has thought
fit to include these alone in the duties of a
BrГҘhmaЖ’a; and has not gone into further
detail. They should, therefore, be supplemented
by other duties that have been mentioned in
ManusmГҰti (the Code of Manu) and other
works. Study and teaching of the Vedas,
performing sacrifices and officiating at them,
and accepting as well as bestowing giftsГі
are the six duties of a BrГҘhmaЖ’a mentioned
in ManusmГҰti.*
in the mind expressing through the senses
what one feels in the mind and allowing no
contortion in the bodyГіthis is what is meant
by straightness of the mind, senses and body.
Believing in the authority and infallibility
of the Vedas and other scriptures and in the
existence of God and life after death, putting
trust in the words of holy men and reposing
unwavering faith in piety and virtueГіall this
is covered by Г«Г…stikyamГӯ.
Г«JВӨГҘnam, consists in devoutly studying
and teaching the Vedas and other scriptures
and fully grasping their teachings.
And Г«VijВӨГҘnamГӯ means directly perceiving
the reality of God through practices taught in
the Vedas and other scriptures and
recommended by holy men.
A BrГҘhmaЖ’a is dominated by unmixed
Sattva; therefore, he has a natural bias for the
duties enumerated above; his temperament is
suited to the performance of these duties, hence
he experiences no difficulty in performing them.
Having thus mentioned the duties of a BrГҘhmaЖ’a, the Lord now points out the duties
of a KВ¶atriya.
вүҘГҠГҠГ’ГҝZ ГғпЈҝВЎГҠпЈҝ Е“ГҺГҒГғЕ’ГҠВёЛҮГҝВў ГҝГҲhпЈҝ ГёГҠГҘГҝВ¬вҲӮUГҠГҝЕёв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“

Exhibition of valour, fearlessness, firmness, diligence and dauntlessness in battle,
bestowing gifts, and lordlinessГіall these constitute the natural duty of a KВ¶atriya. (43)
Г«вҖәauryamГӯ consists in fearlessly offering
righteous opposition to the mightiest foe and
remaining ever ready to fight in a just cause
and putting up a sober and stout fight on the

field of battle. The life of the great patriarch
BhвҲӮВ¶ma is a glowing example of this virtue.*
Г«TejasГӯ is that moral stamina by dint of
which a man firmly adheres to his duty under
all circumstances, resisting all pressure
exerted to the contrary, and due to which other
people hesitate to do anything wrong or that
which is not liked by him. It is also known
by other names such as majesty and glory etc.
He who does not get unnerved even
* BhвҲӮВ¶ma, the grand old man of the Kaurava army, remained a strict celibate all his life. He exhibited
in himself all the virtues of a KВ¶atriya. He received instruction in the use of arms from the avowed enemy
of the KВ¶atriya race, Lord ParaвҖ№urГҘma. When the latter pressed him hard to marry Princess AmbГҘ, the eldest
daughter of the King of KГҘвҖ№вҲӮ, he most respectfully declined in order to maintain his vow of celibacy. But
when ParaвҖ№urГҘma could not be reconciled in anyway and began to indulge in severe threats, he plainly told
him:Гі

(MahГҘ., Udyoga., 178)
Г¬I cannot abandon the duty of a KВ¶atriya either out of fear, compassion, greed of wealth or for any
other gain; this is my cherished vow. As for your boast that for many years together you wiped out the
KВ¶atriyas single-handed more than once (as many as twenty-one times), hear, O RГҘma my reactions about
it. The fact is that the heyday of your glory did not witness the birth of BhвҲӮВ¶ma or any other KВ¶atriya of
his category. You have burnt only straw. Luminaries among the KВ¶atriyas appreared only later. Surely,
RГҘma, I shall curb your pride on the field of battle presently.Г®
ParaвҖ№urГҘma got enraged. A duel ensued between them and raged furiously for full twenty days and
three; but ParasurГҘma could not get the better of BhвҲӮВ¶ma. At last it was at the intervention of NГҘrada and
the celestial sages and of BhвҲӮВ¶maГӯs celestial mother, goddess GaвҲҶgГҘ herself, who bodily appeared on the
scene, on ParaвҖ№urГҘma dropping down his bow, that the conflict ended. BhвҲӮВ¶ma neither turned his back on
the battle-field nor laid down his arms first (MahГҘ., Udyoga., 185).
Out of the eighteen days that the MahГҘbhГҘrata war lasted, the great BhвҲӮВ¶ma held the field as the
commander-in-chief of the Kaurava forces for full ten days. During the remaining eight days many
generalissimos succeeded one after the other.
BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a had taken a vow not to take up arms during the MahГҘbhГҘrata war. They say
BhвҲӮВ¶ma took a counter-vow for some reason to the effect that he would compel the Lord to break His vow
and take up arms. Although the epic does not describe the episode in the same way, the celebrated Hindi
poet SВөuradГҘsa has drawn an exquisite pen-picture of BhвҲӮВ¶maГӯs vow, BhвҲӮВ¶maГӯ says:Гі
Г¬If I fail to make вҖәrвҲӮ Hari take up arms, I should prove to be a shame to mother GaвҲҶgГҘ and shall no
longer call myself a son of вҖәГҘntanu. I shall break the mighty chariot and shake it alongwith the flag which
bears a monkey (HanumГҘn) on its crest. Should I fail to do all this, I swear by the Lord Himself, let me not
attain the high destiny of a KВ¶atriya. I shall march in front of the PГҘЖ’Е’ava  host and shed a river of blood.
Without attaining victory I shall not turn my back on the field of battle so long as there is life in me.Г®
Be that as it may, the MahГҘbhГҘrata says, on the third day from the outbreak of the war, when BhвҲӮВ¶ma
waged a fierce fight, the Lord got enraged and dropped the reins of the horses, and taking up His discus,
which blazed like the sun, leapt down from the chariot revolving it in His hand. Seeing вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a armed with
a discus, all those assembled on the field gave a loud yell of fear. Like the fire at the dissolution of the
universe, the Lord darted with great speed towards BhвҲӮВ¶ma. Seeing вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a advance towards him, discus in
hand, the high-souled BhвҲӮВ¶ma did not get the least frightened and, heroically sounding the string of his bow,
addressed Him thus: Г¬O God of gods, O abode of the universe, O Lord of LakВ¶mвҲӮ (the goddess of prosperity).
O wielder of Discus, You are welcome; I bow to You, O Refuge of all, knock me down from this excellent car
by force. KГҰВ¶Ж’a! being slain at Your hands, I shall be highly blessed here as well as hereafter. O Lord of the
Yadus, You personally rushed to kill me; this  has enhanced my glory in all the three worlds.Г®
Arjuna ran, clasped the LordГӯs feet from behind and somehow persuaded Him to return.
(MahГҘ., BhвҲӮВ¶ma., 59)

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
when faced with the gravest danger,Гіe.g.,
on being mortality wounded on the battle-
field, on the death of his son, grandson or
any other near relation, on losing his all or on
being confronted with any other grave
disasterГіand remains devoted to his legitimate
duty and never shirks it, is said to have what
is called Г«DhГҰtiГӯ or firmness.
Once again, on the ninth day of the war, Lord beheld that the grand old man had let loose a hell of
destruction on the PГҘЖ’Е’ava host. Dropping down the rein of the horses, the Lord darted once more towards
BhвҲӮВ¶ma, whip in hand. And on every stride of the Lord the earth cracked, as it were, due to the fire of energy
of the Lord. The heroes of the Kaurava army lost their nerve and exclaimed: Г¬BhвҲӮВ¶ma is gone!Г® Г¬BhвҲӮВ¶ma
is no more!Г® Seeing the Lord rush towards him as a lion towards an elephant, BhвҲӮВ¶ma did not lose his
balance and, stringing his bow, the grand old man said:Гі

Г¬O lotus-eyed Lord, O God of gods, my obeisance to You. O best of the Yadus, hail, hail to You;
slay me in this great battle. O sinless Lord, O KГҰВ¶Ж’a, meeting my death at Your hands today, I shall be
blessed in everyway in this world. O Protector of cows, honoured in this way by you I have been honoured
by all the three worlds. O sinless One, I am Your bond-slave; strike me at Your will.
Arjuna ran and clasped the hands of the Lord, but the latter did not stop and moved along, dragging
Arjuna behind Him. At last, when Arjuna reminded him of His vow and took a solemn pledge to kill BhвҲӮВ¶ma,
the Lord returned to His post.
Having fought for ten days when BhвҲӮВ¶ma was thinking of dropping his body, the seers and Vasus (a
class of gods) present in the sky told BhвҲӮВ¶ma that they approved of his idea. Thereafter, refusing to strike
вҖәikhaЖ’Е’вҲӮ, BhвҲӮВ¶ma the life long BrahmacГҘrвҲӮ, succumbed to the shafts of Arjuna and fell down on a bed of
arrows. While falling down BhвҲӮВ¶ma saw that the sun was still coursing in a southerly direction; hence he
did not give up the ghost. Goddess GangГҘ (BhвҲӮВ¶maГӯs mother) despatched great seers in the form of swans
to him. BhвҲӮВ¶ma told them that he would survive till the sun had changed its course and would give up the
ghost only at the proper hour. Hardly an inch of space had been left in BhвҲӮВ¶maГӯs body, which was not
penetrated by ArjunaГӯs shafts (MahГҘ., BhвҲӮВ¶ma., 119). His head alone was suspended in the air. The grey-
haired warrior asked for a pillow. Duryodhana and others ran with valuable soft cushions. BhвҲӮВ¶ma laughed
and said, Г¬My valiant sons, such pillows hardly fit in with the bed of a fallen warrior.Г® At last he asked
Arjuna to provide him with a pillow suited to his needs. Arjuna shot three arrows under the head of BhвҲӮВ¶ma
in such a way that it was raised and the shafts served him as a pillow. BhвҲӮВ¶ma was much pleased at this
and spoke to Arjuna thus:Гі

Г¬It is in this way that a KВ¶atriya who is strictly devoted to his duty should repose on a bed of arrows
while casting off his mortal coil on the field of battle.Г®
The great BhвҲӮВ¶ma was lying wounded on his bed of arrows. At this expert surgeons, proficient in the
art of extracting arrows, were summoned. BhвҲӮВ¶ma told them that he had attained the highest destiny of a
KВ¶atriya; the surgeons were, therefore, no longer of any use to him (Ibid., 120).
The wounds and cuts in the body of BhвҲӮВ¶ma were causing great pain to him. He asked for cold water.
People ran with jars of ice-cold water. BhвҲӮВ¶ma reminded them that he was lying on a bed of arrows and
was awaiting the change of course of the sun, and wondered what kind of drink they had brought for him.
At last he summoned Arjuna and told him that his throat was getting parched with thirst and asked him
to provide him with water as Arjuna was competent to do so. Arjuna mounted the chariot, strung his
illustrious bow, GГҘЖ’Е’вҲӮva, and shot an arrow consecrated with a mystical, formula called ParjanyГҘstra into
the ground to the right of his granduncle, BhвҲӮВ¶ma. Immediately there welled up a jet of nectar-like and
fragrant water and fell into BhвҲӮВ¶maГӯs mouth. BhвҲӮВ¶ma quaffed that drink to his heartГӯs content (Ibid., 121).
At the conclusion of the MahГҘbhГҘrata war King YudhiВ¶Вўhira called on BhвҲӮВ¶ma along with BhagavГҘn
вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a. All leading sages and seers, who had realized God, were present on the scene. Seeing the Lord,

=== PAGE 748 ===

* Chapter XVIII *
747
BhвҲӮВ¶ma greeted and extolled Him. вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a told BhвҲӮВ¶ma that it would take some time before the sun would
change its course towards the north; in the meantime He requested BhвҲӮВ¶ma to relieve YudhiВ¶Вўhira of his
grief imparting to him the knowledge of the sacred lore that he had acquired. BhвҲӮВ¶ma submitted to the Lord
that his body was smarting with pain on account of its wounds, that his mind and intellect were fidgety,
that he was unable to speak, that he fainted every now and then, that it was by the LordГӯs grace alone that
he had been surviving till then. Moreover, he felt that it would be presumptuous on his part to speak,
anything in the presence of the Preceptor of the Universe. He was feeling tongue-tied, and therefore begged
to be excused. With tears of love gushing from His eyes the Lord spoke in a voice choked with emotion,
Г¬BhвҲӮВ¶ma dear, your exhaustion, fits of swoon, the sensation of burning agony, pangs of hunger and torporГі
everything shall presently disappear by My grace. Knowledge of every description shall flash on your mind,
your intellect shall have a fixed resolve, your mind shall be ever established in the quality of Sattva, your
reason shall begin to guide you in the matter of Dharma or any other branch of learning that you may fix
your mind upon.Г® He further told BhвҲӮВ¶ma that instead of doing it Himself He was asking him to speak only
in order that the fame and glory of His devotee may be enhanced. By the grace of God all the agonies that
BhвҲӮВ¶ma felt in his body disappeared then and there, his mind became alert and his intellect was fully
awakened. By dint of his lifelong chastity, intuition, wisdom and devotion of God, BhвҲӮВ¶ma, whose
knowledge was unfathomable, instructed YudhiВ¶Вўhira in all the aspects of Dharma with the same youthful
vigour as he had evinced on the field of battle for ten days, and brought solace to the latterГӯs heart, which
was tormented with grief (MahГҘ., вҖәГҘnti., AnuвҖ№ГҘsana).
Having lain on his bed arrows for full fifty days and eight, BhвҲӮВ¶ma resolved to give up his ghost on
the day the sun turned towards the north, and spoke to BhagavГҘn вҖәrвҲӮ KГҰВ¶Ж’a as follows: Г¬O Deity, O Supreme
God of gods, O Lord who is adored by gods as well as demons, O Trivikrama (He who in His cosmic form
measured all the three worlds in three strides), O Wielder of the celebrated Conch, Discus and Mace, I offer
obeisance to You. O VГҘsudeva (all-pervading Lord), You are HiraЖ’yГҘtmГҘ (flaming like fire), the supreme
PuruВ¶a SavitГҘ (the Creator), VirГҘВў (one endowed with a cosmic Body), the embodied soul, atom-shaped, the
supreme Spirit and SanГҘtana (the everlasting one). O lotus-eyed one, O highest Person, pray redeem this
soul. O KГҰВ¶Ж’a, O Vaikuntha, O PuruВ¶ottama, now grant me leave to depart. I strongly admonished the dull-
headed Duryodhana to realize the truth that:Гі

Г¬Where there is KГҰВ¶Ж’a, Dharma or righteousness is there, and where there is righteousness, victory is
assured.Г®
Г¬But the fool did not listen to my advice. I recognize You to be the Ancient Person Himself. You
are NГҘrГҘyaЖ’a Himself descended on earth.

(MahГҘ; Anu; 167., 45)
Г¬KГҰВ¶Ж’a, pray! allow me to cast off my body. Leaving this body with Your leave I shall attain the
supreme state.Г®
The Lord gave His assent. Thereupon BhвҲӮВ¶ma stopped the inhalation and exhalation of his breath
through Yoga and gradually began to push it upwards. As the outgoing breath left a particular part of the
body in its upward journey, the shafts stuck into that part were immediately forced out and the wounds
got healed. In an instant all the arrows dropped out of BhвҲӮВ¶maГӯs body, not a single wound was left and his
vital breath forced its way upwards through what is known as Brahmarandhra (an apperture in the crown
of the head). People saw that a column of light issued from the Brahmarandhra and disappeared in the sky
in the twinkling of an eye.
The diligence shown in dispensing justice
to two or more contending parties, in
determining and discharging oneГӯs duty, in
fighting with the enemy and in dealing properly
with oneГӯs friends, foes and neutrals, is referred
to here as Г«DГҘkВ¶yam.Гӯ
He who never turns his back on the
field of battle even in the face of the gravest
danger, who offers righteous opposition to
the enemy under all circumstances and
continues to exert his strength against him
and remains fixed to his post risking his own

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
life, serves as an illustration of Г«Yuddhe
apalГҘyanamГӯ. Keeping this very ideal before
him the youthful hero Abhimanyu courted

single-handed, but refused to lay down his
arms (MahГҘ., DroЖ’a, 49.22). Even in the

Rajputs did not turn their back on the enemy

against hundreds and thousands.
Liberally bestowing oneГӯs possessions
on deserving persons according to their needs
is what is called Г«DГҘnamГӯ (XVII.20).
Г«IsvarabhГҘvaВЁГӯ consists in deterring the

the people as oneГӯs own progeny in a loving

The temperament of the KВ¶atriyas is
dominated by RajoguЖ’a mixed with Sattva;
hence they have a natural bias towards these
duties, they experience no difficulty in
performing them. Of these duties, again, those
of a universal nature, such as firmness, charity
etc., being open to all, are neither Г«AdharmaГӯ
(prohibited) nor Paradharma (alien) for non-
KВ¶atriyas; they are, however, not their natural
duties. That is why their performance involves
exertion on their part. This is what is meant
by declaring the above duties as natural to
KВ¶atriya.
The ManusmГҰti or the Code of Manu
briefly mentions the following as the duties
for a KВ¶atriya. They are: (1) governance of
the people, (2) bestowing gifts, (3) performing
sacrifices (4) study of the Vedas and (5) non-
attachment to sensuous pleasures. The Lord,
however refers in this context to those duties
alone that bear special affinity to the
temperament of the KВ¶atriyas: hence He has
included in this verse only two of the five
duties prescribed by Manu, viz., governance
of the people and charity under the names of
Г«вҲ«svarabhГҘvaГӯ and Г«DГҘnaГӯ, which bear special
affinity to the KВ¶atriya temperament. The
other duties of the warrior class have not
been dealt with at length in this verse. Hence
the duties mentioned in this verse should be
taken to include other duties as well that
have been prescribed for the KВ¶atriyas in
other scriptures.

Keeping cows in oneГӯs own household
like Nanda and other cowherds, grazing them

Г«VГҘЖ’ijyamГӯ consists in buying and selling

Giving less or taking more in weight,

inferior quality for a superior quality or taking
a superior quality for an inferior quality by
changing the quality of or adulterating a
commodity, taking more or giving less than

resorting to lying, duplicity, stealing and
violence or any other unlawful means in any
business whatsoeverГіall these are foul
practices in trade. Honesty in trade consists
in avoiding all these foul practices and
conducting oneГӯs dealings in a truthful manner.
It is through such dealings that TulГҘdhГҘra
attained perfection.*

* In KГҘвҖ№вҲӮ (the modern Banaras) there was a VaiвҖ№ya trader, TulГҘdhГҘra by name. He was a great ascetic

A BrГҘhmaЖ’a, named JГҘjali, performed austere penance on the sea-shore. Birds built nests in his

was greatly surprised at this wonderful knowledge of TulГҘdhГҘra. Asked by JГҘjali, TulГҘdhГҘra gave a

Living by the service of the twiceborn

вҖ“ Today it is alleged that the division of society into four grades or orders is a selfish creation of
members of the higher or twice-born classes, who have been in power. But careful thought would reveal
that for the healthy organization of society VarЖ’adharma is most essential and it is no human creation either.
The institution of VarЖ’a is a creation of God. The Lord Himself says:Гі
ГёГҠГғГҲп¬ӮВёГЎГҝZ в—ҠГҝГҠ вҖҡГҺCГ”ВўU ВӘГҲГЎГҠвҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГҒп¬ӮГ·ГҠВӘвүҘГҠвҖ”вҖ“
(GвҲӮtГҘ IV.13)
Г¬The four orders of society (viz., the BrГҘhmaЖ’a, the KВ¶atriya, the VaiвҖ№ya and the вҖәВөudra) have been
created by Me, classifying them according to the mode of PrakГҰti predominant in each and apportioning
corresponding duties to them.Г®
The great sages and seers of India who were endowed with a prophetic vision and had an intuition
of the past and future as well, directly perceived this divine truth and raising the edifice of society on the
solid foundation of this truth, gave it an ordered shape and made it peaceful and prosperous, morally sound,
Having thus enumerated the natural duties of the four VarЖ’as, the Lord now tells us,
in the following two verses, the mode of their performance which would easily enable the
performer to attain the highest perfection. The LordГӯs motive in doing so is to reveal the true
nature of Г«Karmayoga coupled with Devotion and its reward.
Sп¬ӮпЈҝ Sп¬ӮпЈҝ вҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГЎГҝГҒГ·вҒ„UГғвҖ” вҖҡВўГҒвҖҡГҒhВў вҲӮUГ·ГғпЈҝ ЕёвҒ„UвҖ”вҖ“
Sп¬ӮвҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГҒЕёвҒ„UГғвҖ” ГҒвҖҡГҒhВў ГҝГ•ГҠ ГҒп¬ӮГЈЕ’ГҒГғ ГғГ«ВҝГҺUГЎГҠГҲH 45H

* Chapter XVIII *
751
active, unselfish, beneficient and secure. For the efficient organization of the human society the need of
a fourfold division has been felt at all time and in all climes and the same has existed and does exist in
all parts of the world. But the systematized form in which this division existed in this land of sages and
seers did not exist anywhere else.
For establishing and maintaining the rule of Dharma or righteousness in society and in order that the
society may continue to lead a happy life, and for diligently removing any clog that may present itself in the
smooth working of the society, for ending the vicious circle of Karma, for resolving tangles and for giving an
authoritative decision when the society is faced with a conflict of duties, what is needed is a clear and unfogged
brain. Even so muscular strength or prowess is essential for protecting Dharma as well as the society that has
taken its stand on Dharma. Wealth and food are essential for properly nourishing the brain and the muscles. And
for the proper discharge of these functions physical exertion or labour is equally essential.
That is why the BrГҘhmaЖ’a represents the brain of the social organism, the KВ¶atriya is its arm,  the
VaiвҖ№ya its thigh and the вҖәВөudra represents its feet. They are the four essential limbs of the same social
organism and depend for their protection and life on mutual help and co-operation. Contempt or contumely
being altogether out of the question, one cannot afford to entertain the least disregard for another. Nor is
there any idea of superiority or inferiority between them. All of them are superior in their own position and
sphere of activity. The BrГҘhmaЖ’a is superior by virtue of his knowledge and wisdom, the KВ¶atriya by virtue
of his valour, the VaiвҖ№ya by virtue of his wealth or material resources, and the вҖәВөudra by virtue of his
numerical strength or capacity for physical exertion. And all the four have their full utility. They have
sprung from the body of one and the same God,Гіthe BrГҘhmaЖ’a from His mouth, the KВ¶atriya from His arms,
the VaiвҖ№ya from His thighs and the вҖәВөudra from his feetГі
вҖҷЛқГҠrГҠГ”ГЎГҠГҠпЈҝЛҳSГҝ в—ҠГҲПҖв—ҠГҠвҖҡГӢЕ’ЛңГ” вҖҷГҠвҖһГҚU вҒ„UГҠВЎГЈГҝвҖ” вҲ‘ГҺВ§ГғвҖ”вҖ“ в„ўВ§MВ§ ГғЕ’SГҝ ГҝЕ’ЛңГ”п¬ӮГ’вүҘГҝвҖ” В¬Е’ЛңГ”ГЁГҝГҠВў вүҘГҠГҚВәЛқГҠпЈҝ вҖўВЎГҠГҝГғH
(В§Rgveda-SaвүҲhitГҘ X.90.12)
But the respective strength of these orders or classes is neither conducive to the attainment of their
own selfish ends nor aims at self-exaltation by relegating others to a subordinate position. Duties have
been divided between them as between the essential limbs of the social organism according to their
respective capacity or aptitude. And all this has been done in order that one may discharge oneГӯs own
duty and enable others to do the same! Since duties have been properly apportioned without any idea
of superiority or inferiority, balance of power is maintained between the four orders. None can disregard
another or encroach upon the legitimate rights of another. Built as it is on the solid foundation of division
of duties on the basis of qualifications this institution of VarЖ’a is so well-organized that balance of power
is automatically maintained. By clearly and separately mentioning the duties of each order, God Himself
as well as the seers who have laid down rules for the conduct of society have afforded all enhanced facilities
for discharging their respective duties unhampered and the due discharge of oneГӯs own duty can never
disturb the balance of power.
By using the pronoun Г«SveГӯ twice in
this verse the Lord intends to convey that a
man reaches the supreme state by performing
whatever duty is natural to him. That is to
say, the same reward which is obtained by a
BrГҘhmaЖ’a through practices such as the
subjugation of his mind and senses and so
on, by a KВ¶atriya through exhibition of valour,
governance of the people, bestowing gifts,
and other such duties, and by a VaiвҖ№ya through
agriculture and other such pursuits is gained
by a вҖәВөudra through acts of service. Therefore,
whatever duty is natural to a man is conducive
to the highest good for him. For attaining
Sп¬ӮпЈҝ Sп¬ӮпЈҝ вҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГҒГЎГҠ to his own natural duty; вҖўГҒГ·вҒ„UГғвҖ”Г” keenly devoted; вҖҡВўГҒвҖҡГҒhв—ҠЛңГ” the highest
perfection (in the form of God-realization); вҲӮUГ·ГғпЈҝ attains; ЕёвҒ„UвҖ” man; Sп¬ӮГ”вҲ‘В§в—ҠВёГҒЕёвҒ„UГғвҖ” (a man) engaged
in his natural duty; ГҒвҖҡГҒhв—ҠЛңГ” (the highest) perfection; ГҝГ•ГҠ whereby; ГҒп¬ӮГЈЕ’ГҒГғ attains; ГғГғЛңГ” that (mode
of performance); oГҺГЎГҠГҲ hear.
Keenly devoted to his own natural duty, man attains the highest perfection in the
form of God-realization. Hear the mode of performance whereby the man engaged in
his inborn duty reaches that highest consummation.                                  (45)

=== PAGE 753 ===

752
* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
blessedness a particular VarЖ’a need not adopt
the duties or vocation of another.
The word Г«SaвүҲsiddhimГӯ in this verse does
not stand for accomplishment in the shape of
purity of heart or the attainment of heaven or
the acquisition of supernatural powers such
as that of assuming an atomic form; it denotes
that highest consummation which is variously
Even though the aforesaid four divisions of society naturally exist in Europe and other countries as
well, there is no balance of power between them inasmuch as the divisions are not governed by definite
principles. That is why the intellect now prevail, over military strength, while at other times the masses or
the proletariate get the better of the capitalist. In the Indian division of society this does not happen
inasmuch as it prescribes separate duties for each group or division.
In the caste system adopted by the В°RВ¶is the BrГҘhmaЖ’a occupies the highest rank; he is the law-giver
of the society; it is his precepts that are respected by all. He is the teacher and guide of all; but he does
not accumulate riches nor wields the rod of punishment and has no relish for luxuries and enjoyments either.
Self-interest has no place in his life as it were. Spurning wealth and power and high position, he lives on
fruits and root and resides with his family in a forest far from the din and bustle of town life. He remains
engaged day and night in the performance of penance, the practice of virtue and the acquisition of
knowledge or wisdom. By dint of his remarkable power of religious austerities coupled with control of mind
and the senses, endurance, forbearance etc., he acquires the eye of wisdom, which is so difficult to attain,
and perceiving the truth in the transcendental light of that wisdom, propagates that truth in the society in
a selfless spirit through pious and morally upright men. He seeks no return for his services. He leads a most
simple life, depending on whatever the society voluntarily bestows on him or on whatever he gets by way
of alms. Such is the pious ideal of his life.
The KВ¶atriya rules over all. He punishes the offender and rewards men of good conduct. Wielding the rod
of punishment he does not allow the wicked to rear their head and protects Dharma and society from evil-doers,
burglars, dacoits and enemies. A KВ¶atriya meets out punishment, but does not frame the laws himself. He
follows the laws made by the BrГҘhmaЖ’as. It is in accordance with those laws that he levies taxes on the people
and, again, it is in accordance with those very laws that he spends the revenue so collected in a systematic
manner in the interest of the people. The laws are framed by the BrГҘhmaЖ’as and the stock of wealth lies in the
hands of the VaiвҖ№ya. The role of the KВ¶atriya is that of a constitutional manager and guardian only.
The sources of wealth, viz., trade, cattle and foodГіall these remain in the keeping of the VaiвҖ№ya. He
produces and multiplies wealth, but not for himself. Protected by the knowledge of the BrГҘhmaЖ’a and the
physical strength of the KВ¶atriya he spends the wealth in the interest of all the four castes in accordance
with the laws referred to above. He has no share in the administration of the state nor does he need it. For
the BrГҘhmaЖ’a and the KВ¶atriya never meddle with his trade, they never take his wealth with a selfish motive,
on the other hand, they protect it and with the help of their wisdom and prowess, manage things in such
an organized way that he is able to carry on his business efficiently and unhampered. Thus he has no
discontent in his heart. And he gladly accepts from the very start the leadership of the BrГҘhmaЖ’a and the
KВ¶atriya and also deems it necessary to do so; for therein lie his own interests. He willingly pays the tax
to the king, renders services to the BrГҘhmaЖ’a and duly provides food and clothing etc., to the вҖәВөudra with
honour, fully satisfying his needs.
Now comes the вҖәВөudra; he is naturally superior in number. In the вҖәВөudra physical strength predominates,
but intellectual capacity is a bit deficient in him. That is why physical exertion alone has been assigned
to him. And for the well-being of the community physical strength is most essential too. But his physical
strength is in no way less valuable than any other kind of strength. It is on the numerical strength of the
вҖәВөudra that the other three VarЖ’as take their stand. He is the basis or foundation. It is on the strength of the
feet that the body moves. That is why the other three VarЖ’as look upon the вҖәВөudra as their beloved limb.
In return for his labour the VaiвҖ№ya gives him ample money, the KВ¶atriya protects his wealth and family and
the BrГҘhmaЖ’a shows him the path of virtue, the way to God-realization. No other VarЖ’a robs him of his
termed as God-realization, attainment of the
supreme goal, of the eternal state, of the
supreme state or of Brahma who is all peace.
Besides this, the eternal duties of a BrГҘhmaЖ’a
include the  study and teaching of the Vedas
and God-realization; hence it is merely
preposterous to believe that these duties can
lead to anything else than the supreme state.

=== PAGE 754 ===

* Chapter XVIII *
753
In the first half of this verse the Lord
proclaimed that engaged in oneГӯs own duties
man attains the highest perfection. Here it
may be urged that actions, as a matter of fact,
bind a man; how, then, can a man who is
keenly devoted to them attain the highest
consummation? The Lord answers this possible
query in the latter half. That is to say. He
undertakes to point out unequivocally, in the
next verse, the way to attain the supreme
state even while engaged in those duties.
The word Г«NaraВЁГӯ denotes every human
being belonging to the four orders of society,
hence the latter half of the verse expounds that
evey human being is qualified to attain liberation.
It further shows that for the realization of God
one need not actually give up oneГӯs duties,
that a man can attain God by merely continuing
to perform without interruption duties
appropriate to his VarЖ’a or grade in society
and Г…вҖ№rama or stage in life with the object of
God-realization (XVIII.56).
livelihood for the realization of its own ends nor do the other VarЖ’as selfishly underpay him, nor do they
ill-treat him taking him to be inferior or lower in rank. They all believe that every VarЖ’a gets its own due,
that none puts another under any obligation. Yet all help one another and all advance his interests along
with theirs and consider his advancement as their own and his degradation as their own downfall. Under
such circumstances the вҖәВөudra, who is superior in number, remains contented; none of the four orders is
handicapped by another and none of them is subjected to ignominy at the hands of another.
Like four brothers forming a joint family, the four VarЖ’as remain engaged in discharging their
respective and essential duties, ungrudgingly divided according to the capacity of each, for the joint
advancement of their family. They add to the strength of the community by advancing the interests of one
another,Гіthe BrГҘhmaЖ’a by establishing the rule of virtues, the KВ¶atriya by exerting his valour, the VaiвҖ№ya
by using his material resources and the вҖәВөudra by employing the strength of physical exertion or labour.
Neither do they all seek to undertake the same work or occupation nor do they entertain any feeling of
superiority or inferiority in performing separate duties. That is how balance of power is maintained between
them and the cause of virtue is strengthened and promoted more and more. Such is the grand idea underlying
the institution of VarЖ’a or the caste system.
In this division of society into so many VarЖ’as or castes is based on the diversity of GuЖ’as or qualities and
Karma or duties. This however, does not mean that the VarЖ’a of a man is changed by his taking to an arbitrary
occupation. VarЖ’a has its root in birth and occupation is the principal factor in preserving its identity or
individual character. In this way birth and occupation both are essential in determining oneГӯs VarЖ’a. Those who
determine VarЖ’a by occupation alone do not really believe in VarЖ’a. If VarЖ’a is determined by occupation
alone, there is no knowing how many times a man will have to change his VarЖ’a in the course of a single day.
Society will no longer be governed by any order or law. There will be complete chaos. But no such thing exists
in the Indian caste system or VarЖ’adharma, as it is called. Had VarЖ’a been determined by occupation alone, the
Lord would not have taught in the GвҲӮtГҘ the duties of a KВ¶atriya to Arjuna, who had got ready to take up the
duties of a BrГҘhmaЖ’a at the commencement of the Battle. It is only in accordance with oneГӯs virtuous or sinful
acts performed in previous lives that one is born in different VarЖ’as or castes. He who is born in a particular
VarЖ’a or caste should perform the duties prescribed for that VarЖ’a alone; for that is his own Dharma or duty. And
death in the performance of oneГӯs duty has been proclaimed by the Lord as conducive to blessednessГіГ«Sп¬ӮЕ“в—ҠпЈҝВё
ГҒЕёЕ“ЕёВў ГјГҠпЈҝГҝвҖ”Гӯ At the same time Г«ParadharmaГӯ, the duty of another, has been declared as Г«fraught with danger.Гӯ This
is quite true; for the balance of power in society is maintained only when all perform their respective duties and
then only is the Dharma of a society maintained and promoted. The abandonment of oneГӯs duty and the
adoption of anotherГӯs duty are harmful both for the individual and the community. It is a matter for regret that
due to various causes disorganization has set in the caste system of the Aryan race. No caste or VarЖ’a is devoted
to its own duty. There is a general tendency to act according to oneГӯs own will and the evil consequences of this
tendency are becoming evident.
ГҝГғвҖ” В¬Лқп¬ӮГҺГҒГ»ГҠГ·ГҚВёГғГҠЕёГҠВў ГҝпЈҝЕё вҖҡп¬ӮВёГҒв—ҠЕ’Вў ГғГғв—ҠЛңГ”вҖ“

* Bhagavad-GвҲӮtВөa *
Realizing this with supreme faith and reverence
and wholly giving up the feeling of mineness,
attachment and the desire for fruit, he who
serves the whole universe through the
performance of His natural duties in
accordance with the  commandment of God
and for the sake of His pleasure only, or in
other words, he who performs his duties in a
selfless spirit in the above manner with a
view to gratifying all living beings, is said to
worship God through the performance of his
natural duties.
Any man, to whatever VarЖ’a or Г…вҖ№rama
he belongs, can attain God or the supreme
Spirit in the form of the highest perfection by
worshipping Him through his actions; God-
realization is equally open to all. A KВ¶atriya
who worships God through exhibition of
valour and other duties reaches the same
state which is attained by a BrГҘhmaЖ’a
worshipping Him through his religious
practices. Such as the control of mind and
senses etc., by dedicating those practices to
Him. Even so, a вҖәВөudra who worships God
through his acts of service reaches the same
supreme goal as is reached by the VaiвҖ№ya
worshipping Him through agriculture and
other appropriate pursuits. Hence that is a
most easy way to get rid of the shackles of
Karma and realize God. Therefore, a man
should practise worship of God by performing
his duties in the aforesaid spirit. This is what
is meant by the latter half of this verse.
In order to show how to worship God
through the performance of oneГӯs duties the
Lord first of all draws the attention of Arjuna,
in the first half of this verse, to His all-
pervading aspect along with His virtues, glory
and power. The idea is that while performing
each and every duty a man should bear in
mind that it is from God that the whole of
this universe including all animate and inanimate
beings has emanated, and that it is He who
pervades it, or in other words, it is God alone
who has revealed Himself in the form of this
universe through His YogamГҘyГҘ or deluding
potency. Hence this world is identical with
Him. How He pervades the whole of this
universe, has been made clear in the
commentary on verse 4 of Chapter IX.
God is the creator, preserver and destroyer
of this universe; He is all powerful, all
supporting, the director of all, the universal
soul, the inner controller of all and all-pervading.
The whole of this universe is His creation
and it is He who has revealed Himself through
His own YogamГҘyГҘ in the form of the universe;
hence the whole of this universe is His. The
body, senses, mind and intellect of the
worshipper, as well as whatever duties
appropriate to his VarЖ’a or grade in society,
such as sacrifice, charity and so on, are being
done by him, also belong to Him, and the
worshipper himself is His. And being the
Self of all gods and other living beings, it is
He who is the enjoyer of all acts (V.29).
In the preceding verse it was stated that a man attains the highest perfection by
worshipping God through the performance of his natural duties. This raises the question:
If a KВ¶atriya abstains from his stern duties such as fighting etc., and strives to attain God
by earning his livelihood through a peaceful avocation such as teaching etc., or even so if

Him, from whom the tide of creation has streamed forth and by whom all this universe

* Chapter XVIII *
755
A Г«well-performedГӯ duty is that which is
accomplished with due attention to all its
details. The Lord, however, uses the
qualification Г«ViguЖ’aВЁГӯ with Г«SvadharmaВЁГӯ;
hence Г«ParadharmГҘtГӯ should be taken by
implication to be qualified by some such
adjective as Г«Possessed of merit.Гӯ Therefore,
the words Г¬SvanuВ¶ВўhitГҘt paradharmГҘtГ® should
be taken to mean a duty which, though
possessed of merit and performed with
scrupulous care, is not prescribed for the
performer, but only for others. The special
duties of a BrГҘhmaЖ’a are marked by a larger
proportion of noble virtues such as non-
violence etc., than the duties of a KВ¶atriya, a
VaiвҖ№ya and a вҖәВөudra; the duties pertaining to
the order of SaвүҲnyГҘsa possess more merits
than those pertaining to the life of a
householder; even so, the duties of a VaiвҖ№ya
and a KВ¶atriya are of greater merit than the
duties of a вҖәВөudra. But just as it is spiritually
beneficial for a wife to serve her husband in
any case, even though he has an ugly
appearance and is devoid of merit, even so
that duty alone which has been prescribed
for a man is good for him, no matter if it is
devoid of merit on the face of it and even
though it has not been fully acomplished in
all its details. This is what is conveyed by the
first half of this verse.
That duty alone which has been prescribed
for a man with due regard to his grade in
society, stage in life, temperament and
circumstances etc., is Г«SvadharmaГӯ or natural
duty for him. Lying, duplicity, thieving,
violence, cheating, adultery and other prohibited
acts are Г«SvadharmaГӯ for none; and actions
motivated by desire too are binding on none.
Hence they have not been recognized as the
Svadharma of any. Other than these, those
that have been declared as the special duties
attaching to a particular VarЖ’a or Г…вҖ№rama,
and for which men belonging to other VarЖ’as
and Г…вҖ№ramas are not eligible, are the exclusive
Svadharma of men belonging to that particular
VarЖ’a or Г…вҖ№rama; while those duties which
have been declared as open to every Dvija or
member of the twice-born classes, viz., the
study of the Vedas and the performance of
sacrifices etc., are the Svadharma of the twice-
born classes. Again, those duties of a general
or universal character for which men and
women of all classes and Г…вҖ№ramas are eligible,
viz., Devotion to God, truthfulness, service
of oneГӯs parents, control of the senses, chastity
and modesty etc., are the Svadharma of all.
The adjective Г«ViguЖ’aВЁГӯ signifies
deficiency of merit. The natural duty of a
KВ¶atriya is to engage in a conflict, and punish
the wicked etc. The duties of a VaiвҖ№ya such
as agriculture etc., are likewise characterized
by a preponderance of evils like destruction

Therefore, Arjuna, one should not relinquish oneГӯs innate duty, even though it
may have a measure of evil; for, even as fire is covered with smoke, all undertakings
are beset with some imperfection or the other.                                                                     (48)
Those duties alone which have been
prescribed for a particular man with due regard
to his grade in society, stage in life, temperament
and circumstances, are Г«SahajaГӯ or innate for
him. Therefore, the words Г«Sahajam KarmaГӯ
in this verse refer to those very duties or actions
which have been variously termed in the course

The abandonment of such innate duties
as are possessed of excellent virtues being out
of the question, even those prescribed and
legitimate duties which ordinarily appear as
tainted with evils like violence etc., are not
really defective, though appearing as such,
and hence should not be given up. That is to
say, they too should be duly performed; for far
from incurring sin through their performance
one is liable to be held guilty for renouncing
them. This is what is meant by the first half of
this verse.
Just as fire is mixed with smoke, which
cannot be wholly separated from it, even so
every undertaking is tainted with demerit.
Every form of activity does involve in one
way or other destruction of life in some form;
for even in the case of those belonging to the
order of recluses activities such as answering
the calls of nature, bathing and begging alms
from door to door involve destruction of life
in some measure, and sacrifice and other acts
of the BrГҘhmaЖ’a too involve destruction of
small creatures due to the elaborate ritual.
Therefore, ordinarily speaking, the duties of
any VarЖ’a or Г…вҖ№rama whatsoever are not
altogether free from blemish; and none can
help doing some work (III.5). Hence even
after giving up his own duty a man must do
some work; and whatever he does will be
contaminated with sin. That is why a man
must not give up his own duty under the
belief that a particular action or duty is inferior
or tainted with evil; on the other hand, he

In order to expound the truth of TyГҘga and SaвүҲnyГҘsa in response to ArjunaГӯs enquiry,
the Lord discussed the subject of TyГҘga in verses 4 to 12 and expounded SaвүҲnyГҘsa or the

relating to TyГҘga, which is only another name for Karmayoga or the path of Action, He
discussed from verse 41 up to the preceding verse the nature of innate actions and their
obligatory character, and showing the alliance of Bhakti with Karmayoga pointed out God-
realization as its reward. But while on the subject of SaвүҲnyГҘsa the Lord did not mention
its reward, nor did He point out how to practise SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga along with worship
disclaiming the doership of actions. Therefore, in order to point out the method and reward
of carrying this practice in seclusion with discrimination and dispassion, the Lord resumes
the discussion of SГҘвҲҶkhyayoga.

The realization of Brahma or the Absolute
follows immediately after the attainment of
this Г«SiddhiГӯ. This is what is indicated in the

The word Г«BrahmaГӯ stands for the eternal
and immutable supreme Spirit or the Absolute,
which is without form and attributes and
which is Truth, Consciousness and Bliss
solidified. And the attainment of Brahma
consists in merging oneГӯs identity in It through
the realization of Truth as mentioned in verse
55 below.
The substantive Г«NiВ¶ВўhГҘГӯ, qualified by

JВӨГҘnayoga (the path of Knowledge), which

called the Г«ParГҘ NiВ¶ВўhГҘ of KnowledgeГӯ.

Attraction and aversion lie hidden in all
enjoyments of sense, they are great enemies
of the SГҘdhaka (III.34). Therefore, wiping
out from oneГӯs mind all traces of attraction or
aversion for any enjoyment of this world or
the next, or for any living being, object,
activity or occurrenceГіthis is what is meant
by the phrase Г«RГҘgadveВ¶au vyudasyaГӯ; and
he who remains ever merged in dispassion is
said to have taken a firm stand on VairГҘgyaГі
Г«VairГҘgyam samupГҘвҖ№ritaВЁГӯ.
Г«AhaвҲҶkГҘraГӯ is the feeling of identification
with oneГӯs body, senses and mind; it is due
to this that man comes to regard himself as
the doer in relation to what is being done by
the mind, intellect and body. Therefore,
completely getting rid of this feeling of self-
identification with the body is what is meant
by giving up egotism. The audacity to bring
others under oneГӯs sway in a wrongful manner
through coercion is called Г«BalamГӯ or violence;
vain consciousness of oneГӯs riches, large family,
learning, birth and physical strength etc., is
known as Г«DarpaГӯ or arrogance; the longing
for obtaining the enjoyments of this world
and the next is spoken of as Г«KГҘmaГӯ; the
feeling of excitement or provocation aroused
in the mind when somebody acts against
oneГӯs will or behaves wrongfully,Гіwhich
manifests itself in the form of blood-shot
eyes, the throbbing of lips, a burning sensation
in the heart and a distortion of the face, is
termed as Г«KrodhaГӯ. The means of enjoyment
An intellect which retains no trace of
sins incurred in previous lives is referred to
here by the words Г«ViвҖ№uddhayГҘ BuddhyГҘГӯ,
and he who is possessed of such a purified
intellect is called Г«BuddhyГҘ viвҖ№uddhayГҘ yuktaВЁГӯ.
Г«LaghvГҘвҖ№вҲӮГӯ is he who is regulated in diet
(VI.17), that is to say, who takes articles of
food which are suitable for spiritual practice,
easily digestible and SГҘttvika in character
(XVII.8) and whose diet is suited to his
nature, needs and digestive capacity, governed
by certain rules and fixed in quantity.
The word Г«ViВ¶ayГҘnГӯ in this verse stands
for all worldly pleasures of sense. The man
referred to in this verse does not waste his
precious time in the enjoyment of such
pleasures. In order to carry on uninterrupted
spiritual practice he takes up his residence on
a river bank, in a place of worship, forest or
mountain cave or any other such place, which
has a holy atmosphere, is not frequented by
many, is naturally secluded and clean or has
been cleaned by sweeping and washing. This
is what is meant by the expressions Г«вҖәabdГҘdвҲӮn
viВ¶ayГҘn tyaktvГҘГӯ and Г«ViviktasevвҲӮГӯ.
Through unwavering firmness as defined
in verse 33 above, and with irreproachable
persistence he empties his mind of all thoughts
of mundane objects and does not allow the
senses to run after worldly enjoyments. This
is what is meant by the phrase Г«DhГҰtyГҘ ГҘtmГҘnam
niyamyaГӯ. And  through such discipline he
succeeds in subduing the mind, senses and
bodyГіrobs them of their freedom as well as
of their capacity to unsettle the intellect. This
ГҒп¬Ӯв—ҠГҲГ«Гҝ giving up; ГҒЕёв—ҠВёв—ҠвҖ” devoid of the feeling of meum; вүҘГҠГҠГЈГғвҖ” tranquil of heart, calm; вҖҷЛқrГҠГ”Г·ГҚГҝГҠГҝ for
